Convection had a hard time getting organized at first with only spotty cells forming ahead of the front.  By early evening, a small MCS formed over western Iowa and advanced east-southeast across the state.  The MCS produced hail in many locations along with gusty winds. There were several reports of gutter damage and dents in car hoods due to the hail in Panora.  Overall, damage was limited with this event since tree leaves were only beginning to come out and the crops had not yet been planted.

Another very narrow mesoscale band of snow produced 6-7 inches of snow in just over 12 hours from Lowden, to Wheatland, to Park View, Iowa across parts of Cedar, Clinton, and Scott counties.

The low pressure mentioned in the narrative above tracked across South Dakota and Minnesota during the evening hours of the 18th.  The airmass, though quite unstable based on the soundings, was significantly capped over Iowa.  The Omaha sounding showed a Lifted Index around -7 C, with CAPE values around 1500 J/kg.  In spite of the instability, the cap over Iowa was quite strong with CIN values around 300 J/kg.  Thunderstorms erupted to the west of the area along a cold front as it entered the state.  Initially, the storms produced severe weather to the west of Iowa where the atmosphere was not as capped.  As the line moved into Iowa, it weakened due to the cap and the presence of much drier air.  Ironically, most of the storms produced lighter wind speeds as they passed than the ambient wind was prior to the storms approach.  Needless to say, this made the task of differentiation between thunderstorm winds and gradient high winds more difficult.  High winds were reported in a few areas.  Thunderstorm winds blew down a barn in Hancock County near Klemme, trapping several head of cattle under the barn.  Trees were also downed in the area.  The ASOS recorded a 58 MPH wind at the Mason City Airport as the storms moved through.  The storms did produce some small hail as they moved through.  Spotty reports showed hail was in the three quarter inch up to nickle size.

The month of May began somewhat dry with the first significant rain event taking place on the 8th into the 9th.  Rivers rose, however most remained below flood stage.  The North River in Central Iowa rose above flood stage for a brief time.  Damage was limited as the river only eclipsed the flood stage by a foot and one half.  Minor property and crop damage were reported.

A warm front lifted north across the state during the previous night.  At the same time, intense low pressure formed to the west of Iowa and tracked northeast through South Dakota into Minnesota.  The low intensified as a negatively tilted upper level trough of low pressure in the upper atmosphere lifted northeast out of the southwest U.S.  A strong jet and pronounced dry slot was evident in the warm sector of the system.  The jet and dry slot tracked across Iowa during the afternoon and evening of the 18th.  This set the stage for the high wind event.  A very strong south to southwest flow developed over the state.  Profiler data showed wind of 50 kts at 500 meters during much of the morning into the early part of the afternoon.  The winds increased to 75 kts at the 500 meter level by the late afternoon and evening hours.  The strong winds in the lowest range gates were not realized during much of the day do to strong upward vertical velocities.  Strong subsidence developed during the late afternoon and evening hours, allowing for sufficient mixing and for the strong winds to be realized at the surface.  During the day, winds were in the 30 to 35 MPH range across the state, with frequent gusts to around 55 MPH.  During the late afternoon and early evening hours, winds increased with sustained winds of 40 MPH or more across most of the state, with many areas reporting gusts of 60 to 65 MPH.  Widespread light damage was reported over the area, with scattered reports of significant wind damage.   Several reports of tree, power line, and out building damage were received from around the state.  Reports of semi-tractortrailers being blown off the roads.  U.S. Highway 18 in Cerro Gordo County was closed by two overturned trucks that were blown over by the wind.  Also, traffic was rerouted in Warren County at the junction of U.S. Highway 65-69 due to a truck being blown over.  In addition to the wind damage problems, dry conditions around the state lead to areas of blowing dust.  Visibilities were restricted at times, especially over the west half of the state.  In Kossuth County, the visibility dropped to as low as three eigths of a mile at the Algona Airport for about an hour.

The strong winds in the lowest range gates were not realized during much of the day do to strong upward vertical velocities.  Strong subsidence developed during the late afternoon and evening hours, allowing for sufficient mixing and for the strong winds to be realized at the surface.  During the day, winds were in the 30 to 35 MPH range across the state, with frequent gusts to around 55 MPH.  During the late afternoon and early evening hours, winds increased with sustained winds of 40 MPH or more across most of the state, with many areas reporting gusts of 60 to 65 MPH.  Widespread light damage was reported over the area, with scattered reports of significant wind damage.   Several reports of tree, power line, and out building damage were received from around the state.  Reports of semi-tractortrailers being blown off the roads.  U.S. Highway 18 in Cerro Gordo County was closed by two overturned trucks that were blown over by the wind.  Also, traffic was rerouted in Warren County at the junction of U.S. Highway 65-69 due to a truck being blown over.  In addition to the wind damage problems, dry conditions around the state lead to areas of blowing dust.  Visibilities were restricted at times, especially over the west half of the state.  In Kossuth County, the visibility dropped to as low as three eigths of a mile at the Algona Airport for about an hour.

A warm front lifted north across the state during the previous night.  At the same time, intense low pressure formed to the west of Iowa and tracked northeast through South Dakota into Minnesota.  The low intensified as a negatively tilted upper level trough of low pressure in the upper atmosphere lifted northeast out of the southwest U.S.  A strong jet and pronounced dry slot was evident in the warm sector of the system.  The jet and dry slot tracked across Iowa during the afternoon and evening of the 18th.  This set the stage for the high wind event.  A very strong south to southwest flow developed over the state.  Profiler data showed wind of 50 kts at 500 meters during much of the morning into the early part of the afternoon.  The winds increased to 75 kts at the 500 meter level by the late afternoon and evening hours.  The strong winds in the lowest range gates were not realized during much of the day do to strong upward vertical velocities.  Strong subsidence developed during the late afternoon and evening hours, allowing for sufficient mixing and for the strong winds to be realized at the surface. Several reports of tree, power line, and out building damage were received from around the state.  Reports of semi-tractortrailers being blown off the roads.  U.S. Highway 18 in Cerro Gordo County was closed by two overturned trucks that were blown over by the wind.  Also, traffic was rerouted in Warren County at the junction of U.S. Highway 65-69 due to a truck being blown over.  In addition to the wind damage problems, dry conditions around the state lead to areas of blowing dust.  Visibilities were restricted at times, especially over the west half of the state.  In Kossuth County, the visibility dropped to as low as three eigths of a mile at the Algona Airport for about an hour.

An intense low pressure moved across the northern tier of states during the day on the 27th.  Very strong south-southwest winds developed with profilers indicating wind of 50 to 65 kts within the lowest range gates.  Temperatures warmed into the mid 80s to low 90s with the atmosphere becoming adiabatic to between 750 and 600 mb, allowing for strong mixing down to the surface.  Winds increased during the day and reached high wind criteria by the early afternoon.  Most of the state saw sustained winds of 40 MPH or higher.  Several locations gusting to between 60 and 65 MPH.  The winds also produced blowing dust around the state.  Isolated reports of visibility reduced to near zero were received.  Spotty damage occurred around the state.  An 18 inch tree limb broke off and fell through the roof of a house in Windsor Heights.  A two to three foot diameter tree was snapped off at the base in Ames.  There were numerous reports of power outages caused by downed tree limbs.  In Union County, a 200 X 40 foot chicken house had the roof blown off.  There were several head of cattle in addition to the chickens contained therein trapped in the debris.

A very warm and unstable airmass was in place over Iowa.  The moisture was in limited supply with this event.  A cold front approached the state from the northwest.  This front moved into the state during the late afternoon and evening of the 16th.  Moisture was limited with this system.  Surface dew points were in the mid 50s to around 60 during the early part of the afternoon over the southeast half of the state.  The moisture was very shallow.  Afternoon mixing lowered dew points into the upper 40s over the southeast, and into the upper 20s and 30s over the northwest just ahead of the front.  Temperatures warmed into mid 80s to low 90s which made the airmass quite unstable.  CAPE values were around 1000 J/kg with Lifted Indices around -4 C. just ahead of the front.  Convection had a hard time getting organized at first with only spotty cells forming ahead of the front.  By early evening, a small MCS formed over western Iowa and advanced east-southeast across the state.  The MCS produced hail in many locations along with gusty winds.  In spite of the "inverted V" type upper air soundings, high winds were not as widespread as would normally be expected.  Several locations did report gusts to around 60 MPH.  High winds blew a car off of Interstate 80 in Poweshiek County southeast of Malcom.  A wind gust of 70 MPH was reported at the Ames Airport in Story County.  For the most part, the hail was in the three quarter to one inch diameter size.  Hail did cover the ground in Carroll County at Lidderdale.  The largest hail report came from Guthrie County north of Panora into the Panora area.  Golf ball size hail was reported at that location.  There were several reports of gutter damage and dents in car hoods due to the hail in Panora.  Overall, damage was limited with this event since tree leaves were only beginning to come out and the crops had not yet been planted.

Thunderstorms developed north and east of a warm front that roughly paralleled Interstate 80 and produced copious amounts of BB and pea sized hail.  A single severe storm moved East across Northern Cedar county and into Clinton county producing large hail.  Golfball hail covered the ground to the southeast of Mechanicsville, Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report relayed by KCRG TV

The month of May began somewhat dry with the first significant rain event taking place on the 8th into the 9th.  Rivers rose, however most remained below flood stage.  The North River in Central Iowa rose above flood stage for a brief time.  Damage was limited as the river only eclipsed the flood stage by a foot and one half.  Minor property and crop damage were reported.

May 2004 began rather dry with an average of only 0.19 inches of rain over the first week of the month.  However, the second week of May brought seasonally normal rainfall.  Heavy rain occurred during the third week of the month.  May's greatest rain events came back to back.  The first round of heavy rain producing thunderstorms began on the morning of the 21st and continued into the morning of the 22ns.  Heaviest rains were in North Central and Northeast Iowa where storm totals included 4.75 inches at Emmetsburg, 6.22 inches at Mason City, and 5.14 inches at Decorah.  Another round of storms began on the afternoon of the 22nd and continued into the morning of the 23rd.  Some of the rain totals reported with this second series of storms included 6.67 inches near Ames and 4.15 inches at Marshalltown.  A statewide average of 2.97 inches of rain fell from these two systems.  Locally heavy rain continued to fall through much of the remainder of the month.  The greatest rainfall amount occurred during the early morning hours of the 30th when 4.37 inches of rain fell at Fairfield.  Damage reports were still coming in but it does appear from preliminary reports that roughly six percent of Iowa's 2004 crop was flood out.  Based on a $23 per acre cost to replant it is estimated that the floods have cost Iowa farmers some $15.2 million dollars.  The rainfall that occurred over the later part of May was Iowa's greatest since July 1993.  Rainfall totals for the month varied form 4.01 inches at Burlington to 14.87 inches at Lansing.  Radar derived estimates suggest that May rainfall was actually below normal in some areas over the far Northwest...such as Buena Vista and Van Buren Counties.  However, complete rain gage reports were not yet available for these areas.  Excessive rainfall produced major flooding in many areas across the state during the second half of May.  This was the first widespread major flood event in many years.  The heaviest rainfall occurred over the Northeastern sections of the state.  Additionally, several episodes of severe thunderstorms produced large hail, high winds, and tornadoes during May.  Severe weather was reported on 15 days during the month with all but 7 of Iowa's 99 counties reporting severe thunderstorm events.  The month's largest severe weather outbreaks occurred on the 8th (26 counties, mostly west central to northeast) ; 17th (20 counties, mostly southwest to east central); 21st (37 counties, mostly northwest to east central); 22nd (31 counties, mostly southwest to east central) and the 24th (33 counties, mostly southwest one half).  An exact tornado count is not yet available for the state, but central Iowa did report 32 tornadoes for the month with an estimated total of 50 tornadoes statewide.  Nearly all were F0 and F1 intensity with minimal damage to communities.  There was a tornado of F2 intensity that did produce significant damage to the communities of Bradgate and Palo (both on May 21).

A collapsing rain shower or possibly a very weak thunderstorm pulled down higher winds aloft and flattened a newly finished barn on highway 2 about 1 mile East of Donnellson, Iowa.  This event was part of a much large gradient wind event across Eastern Iowa, Northern Illinois, and Northeast Missouri during the pre-dawn and daylight hours of 5 March 2004.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported by co-op

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late winter storm dropped heavy snow over parts of northeast Nebraska and western Iowa.  Due to relatively warm temperatures during the event, most of the snow was heavy and wet and the rain/snow line remained nearly stationary for much of that time. In addition...with temperatures a little above freezing on the southern end of the snow area, snow accumulations dropped off sharply.  For instance, a small area in far north Omaha picked up 5 to 6 inches, while the southern part of the city received only an inch or two, and then only on grassy surfaces.  Meanwhile 40 miles or so the southwest much of the snow melted as it fell leaving only a trace.  Most of the heavier snow, 6 inches or more, fell along and northeast of a line from around Center in Knox county to just east and northeast of Council Bluffs in Pottawattamie county in west central Iowa.Heavier amounts in eastern Nebraska included 13.5 inches in Crofton, 10 inches in Walthill and Oakland, 9 inches at Lyons, 8 inches at Wausa and Creighton, 7 inches at Fort Calhoun, Tekamah, West Point, and Blair.Heavier totals in western Iowa included around 13 inches at Castana and near Little Sioux, 11 inches in Mapleton, 9 inches at Harlan and 8 inches in Logan and Kennebec.

Heavy snow spread into Iowa as a sustained southerly flow of moist air took place over the central U.S.  Low pressure formed over the southwest U.S. and tracked northeast across north Texas into northern Indiana.  A closed upper level low pressure was associated with this storm and helped produce significant lift over the area.  A broad band of snow developed during the early morning hours of the 5th, with snow spreading over much of the area by around sunrise.  Southwest Iowa had already seen 1 to 3 inches by sunrise however.  As the dry progressed, bands of heavy snow developed in the warm conveyer ahead of the upper low.  Snowfall peaked during the mid to late afternoon hours with snowfall rates of 2 inches or more per hour reported at some locations.  Visibility was reduced to under 100 meters in many areas for a one to two hour period due to heavy snow.  The snow hit in Des Moines just before the rush hour.  With a snowfall rate of 2 inches per hour for two hours, this caused significant travel problems.  Elsewhere around the state, travel was hampered though it was not stopped.  The Iowa DOT recommended no travel over about the southwest third of the state.  Many activities were called off on the evening of t he 5th, with numerous school closings or delays on the morning of the 6th.  Fortunately, wind was not a major factor with this system.  Wind speeds were in the 10 to 20 MPH range.  This caused drifting of snow, but very little blowing snow.  The heaviest snow amount received came from Osceola in Clarke County with 10.2 inches.  Several areas reported 8 to 9 inches in a band extending from Guthrie Center, to the southwest part of the Des Moines Metropolitan area, south to Lamoni.

An intense storm system with an associated tropopause fold produced sustained gradient winds of 30 to 35 knots (35 to 40 mph) with gusts to 48 knots (55 mph) across Eastern Iowa, Northern Illinois, and Northeast Missouri.  Measured gusts of 50 knots (58 mph) and 54 knots (62 mph) were reported at the Clinton, Iowa, and Peru, Illinois airports.  Iowa DOT RWIS sites at De Witt, Iowa, and the junction of Interstate 80/280 reported sustained winds of 36 knots (41 mph) in a few 5 minute intervals during the daylight hours.  A few trees were downed with known power outages affecting 15,000 people.

The largest hail report came from Guthrie County north of Panora into the Panora area.  Golf ball size hail was reported at that location.  There were several reports of gutter damage and dents in car hoods due to the hail in Panora.  Overall, damage was limited with this event since tree leaves were only beginning to come out and the crops had not yet been planted.

A very warm and unstable airmass was in place over Iowa.  The moisture was in limited supply with this event.  A cold front approached the state from the northwest.  This front moved into the state during the late afternoon and evening of the 16th.  Moisture was limited with this system.  Surface dew points were in the mid 50s to around 60 during the early part of the afternoon over the southeast half of the state.  The moisture was very shallow.  Afternoon mixing lowered dew points into the upper 40s over the southeast, and into the upper 20s and 30s over the northwest just ahead of the front.  Temperatures warmed into mid 80s to low 90s which made the airmass quite unstable.  CAPE values were around 1000 J/kg with Lifted Indices around -4 C. just ahead of the front.  Convection had a hard time getting organized at first with only spotty cells forming ahead of the front.  By early evening, a small MCS formed over western Iowa and advanced east-southeast across the state.  The MCS produced hail in many locations along with gusty winds.  In spite of the "inverted V" type upper air soundings, high winds were not as widespread as would normally be expected.  Several locations did report gusts to around 60 MPH.  High winds blew a car off of Interstate 80 in Poweshiek County southeast of Malcom.  A wind gust of 70 MPH was reported at the Ames Airport in Story County.  For the most part, the hail was in the three quarter to one inch diameter size.  Hail did cover the ground in Carroll County at Lidderdale.  The largest hail report came from Guthrie County north of Panora into the Panora area.  Golf ball size hail was reported at that location.  There were several reports of gutter damage and dents in car hoods due to the hail in Panora.  Overall, damage was limited with this event since tree leaves were only beginning to come out and the crops had not yet been planted.

The cold front that had moved southeast across the state the previous night became stationary across southern Iowa by the morning of the 17th.  A sharp contrast in dew point temperatures was found across the front.  Dew points were in the upper 50s to mid 60s south of the front, with dew points in the 30s north of it.  During the late afternoon the front began to return north as a warm front.  By evening a strengthening southerly jet in the low levels impinged on the front with wind speeds around 50 kts.  The overall low levels of the atmosphere were relatively dry and the airmass was capped initially.  The LCL height was around 750 mb.  There atmosphere was quite unstable with Lifted Indices in the -6 to -10 C range.  CAPE values were in the 2500 to 3500 J/kg range.  Thunderstorms erupted over northern Iowa shortly after sunset.  The storms formed into discrete supercells.  A tornado touched down east of Mason City from one of the stronger cells.  It was in open area and did not cause any damage during its brief touchdown.  The dominant severe weather mode for the event was hail.  Baseball size hail fell in Palo Alto County in Mallard.  There were numerous reports of hail three quarters of an inch to one and one quarter of an inch in diameter.  The largest hail was around two inches in diameter in Palo Alto County.  Hail reportedly covered the ground as the storms passed.  During the late stages of the event, a thunderstorms produced high winds as it swept across Interstate 35 west of Northwood in Worth County.  Two vehicles and one semi-tractortrailer truck were blown off of the interstate near mile post 214.  In addition, one telephone was blown down by the high wind.

A late season winter storm developed in the Northern Rockies and moved southeast into the Ohio Valley.  Snow began in Eastern Iowa during the morning of 15 March and became heavy in the late afternoon and overnight hours.  The snow tapered to flurries just before dawn on 16 March and ended by mid-morning.  Mesoscale banding of snow produced a band of 10+ inches that was 1-2 counties wide.  Final snowfall amounts included Brighton, Iowa 16 inches; Sigourney, Iowa 14.3 inches; Columbus Jct and Wapello 12 and 11.5 inches respectively; North English and Washington 12 and 10.2 inches respectively; and Mediapolis, Iowa 10 inches.  Another very narrow mesoscale band of snow produced 6-7 inches of snow in just over 12 hours from Lowden, to Wheatland, to Park View, Iowa across parts of Cedar, Clinton, and Scott counties.
Hail the size of pennies and nickels was reported by the public and law enforcement officials.
Hail the size of pennies and nickels was reported by the public and law enforcement officials.

The seasons first severe weather took place during the early morning hours of the 24th.  A warm front lifted north into Iowa from Missouri during the night.  Unstable air with dew points in the 50s was lifted over the front.  Initially, there was a cap in place over the state and as moisture increased elevated convection took off.  The area that lifted was above 700 mb.  A large area of thunderstorms developed over the north half of Iowa.  Most of the storms were not severe.  Those that did become severe were only marginal and produced penny size hail in Webster and Marshall Counties.

Heavy snow spread into Iowa as a sustained southerly flow of moist air took place over the central U.S.  Low pressure formed over the southwest U.S. and tracked northeast across north Texas into northern Indiana.  A closed upper level low pressure was associated with this storm and helped produce significant lift over the area.  A broad band of snow developed during the early morning hours of the 5th, with snow spreading over much of the area by around sunrise.  Southwest Iowa had already seen 1 to 3 inches by sunrise however.  As the dry progressed, bands of heavy snow developed in the warm conveyer ahead of the upper low.  Snowfall peaked during the mid to late afternoon hours with snowfall rates of 2 inches or more per hour reported at some locations.  Visibility was reduced to under 100 meters in many areas for a one to two hour period due to heavy snow.  The snow hit in Des Moines just before the rush hour.  With a snowfall rate of 2 inches per hour for two hours, this caused significant travel problems.  Elsewhere around the state, travel was hampered though it was not stopped.  The Iowa DOT recommended no travel over about the southwest third of the state.  Many activities were called off on the evening of t he 5th, with numerous school closings or delays on the morning of the 6th.  Fortunately, wind was not a major factor with this system.  Wind speeds were in the 10 to 20 MPH range.  This caused drifting of snow, but very little blowing snow.  The heaviest snow amount received came from Osceola in Clarke County with 10.2 inches.  Several areas reported 8 to 9 inches in a band extending from Guthrie Center, to the southwest part of the Des Moines Metropolitan area, south to Lamoni.

A strong low pressure dropped southeast across Ontario, passing northeast of Lake Superior, during the day on the 20th.  At the same time a nearly 1050 mb polar high pressure area moved south into central and eastern Saskatchewan.  A strong northwest flow was set up between the two systems.  Along with strong subsidence and a southward push of polar air, strong winds developed over Iowa.  All of Iowa saw sustained winds of 25 to 35 MPH, with gusts to 50 to 55 MPH.  A few areas reached high wind criteria.  Sustained winds of 40 MPH or higher were reported over parts of north central into west central Iowa.  Winds gusted to 50 kts in Guthrie County at Panora, and in Carroll County at Glidden and Carroll.  Scattered damage was reported.  Fortunately, since the trees had not yet leafed out damage was limited.

Heavy snow produced accumulations of 6 to 18 inches...with the 18 inches reported throughout the Sioux City area. Schools and several businesses were closed for the day. Travel was brought to a standstill for a while...especially in the Sioux City area.

Strong low pressure dropped southeast across northern Wisconsin into northern Lower Michigan during the day on the 7th.  As is typical of an "Alberta Clipper" type system, very strong winds swept down the southwest side of the low pressure circulation.  Although all of Iowa reported winds of 25 to 35 MPH with gusts to around 50 MPH, a narrow ribbon of high winds buffeted north central and northeast Iowa.  The winds were centered from near Fairmount, Minnesota through Mason city into Waterloo.  Winds were sustained just above 40 MPH in this band with wind gusts as high as 56 MPH.  The winds lasted a couple hours in any one location with the enter event taking several hours to cross the state.  Spotty damage was reported, but since spring foliage was not yet out the damage was limited.

A late winter storm moved into Iowa, leaving behind it one of the heaviest snowfalls on record in some areas, and a record March snowfall in several locations.  The storm system was not what one would typically expect in that the low took a southeast track from the Rockies, southeast into Kansas, then east into Missouri.  The overall flow pattern over the central U.S. as northwest, however a closed low in the mid levels formed and passed over Iowa.  The surface features were not all that impressive.  Considerable dynamics were found in the mid level of the atmosphere.  Analysis showed a  large area of isentropic lift with upward vertical velocities in the 10 to 15 microbars/sec in the dendritic layer.  Low and mid level winds of 55 kts drew copious moisture north into Iowa.  Snow developed during the predawn hours of the 15th and became heavy before sunrise.  Heavy snow criteria was reached by 0700 CST in west central Iowa.  The heavy snow spread east and reached central parts of central Iowa by the morning rush hour, and the southeast sections of the Des Moines CWA by late afternoon.  The heaviest snow fell in a band about 30 miles wide extending from just south of Denison, through Des Moines, to between Oskaloosa and Ottumwa.  In this band snowfall was in the 12 to 18 inch range.  Some of the heavier totals included 18.1 inches in Windsor Heights, 16.3 inches in Carroll, 16.2 inches at the WFO in Johnston, and 16 inches at Knoxville in Marion County, and Perry in Dallas County.  The official calendar day snowfall in Des Moines, measured at the WFO in Johnston, for the 15th was 15.6 inches.  That total will stand as the record for the greatest calendar day snowfall for the month of March and the third highest calendar day snowfall on record for the city.  The all-time record snowfall for Des Moines is 19.8 inches, set on 01 January, 1942, with 26  December 1898 coming in second with 17 inches.  In spite of the heavy snowfall, travel problems were limited.  Most of the cities were able to keep streets cleared of snow as soil temperatures were in the upper 30s to low 40s at the onset of the snow.  Rural areas had some problems with drifting.  Though it was not all that windy during the storm, east to northeast winds of 15 to 20 MPH did cause some drifting.  Several event cancellations occurred on the evening of the 15th.  A few schools in the harder hit area remained closed or started classes late on the morning of the 16th.

Many activities were called off on the evening of t he 5th, with numerous school closings or delays on the morning of the 6th.  Fortunately, wind was not a major factor with this system.  Wind speeds were in the 10 to 20 MPH range.  This caused drifting of snow, but very little blowing snow.  The heaviest snow amount received came from Osceola in Clarke County with 10.2 inches.  Several areas reported 8 to 9 inches in a band extending from Guthrie Center, to the southwest part of the Des Moines Metropolitan area, south to Lamoni.

The front that moved south across the state during the previous afternoon became stalled over southern Iowa during the evening of the 20th and early morning of the 21st.  The airmass was moderately unstable with CAPE values in the 1500 to 2000 J/kg range.  The low level jet helped to initiate elevated convection.  The thunderstorms fired off from around 600 Mb.  A storm moved through Sac County and produced a 60 MPH wind gust.  Another of the storms produced a 59 MPH wind gust in Franklin County at Latimer.

A very unstable airmass was over Iowa and helped kick off the seasons first severe weather outbreak.  At the surface a warm frontal boundary extended nearly east to west across the state and provided the focus for thunderstorm development.  By the late afternoon, lifted indices were approaching -10 C. with CAPE values around 5000 J/kg over western Iowa.  Surface temperatures warmed into the upper 80s with dew points in the low to mid 70s.  A southwest surface wind of 15 to 25 kts pushed over the front.  Northeast of the frontal boundary winds were easterly around 10 to 15 kts.  Actually, the surface boundary was further enhanced by outflow from convective complexes during the day, further sharpening the contrast.  Thunderstorms continued to fire along and north of the boundary through the afternoon and into the evening.  This resulted in widespread flash flooding as very heavy rains fell on areas that had had significant rainfall the previous night.  There were reports in north central into northeast Iowa of 2 to 4 inches of rainfall in a little more than an hours time.  Major flooding took place in the Mason City area where evacuations were taking place.  The thunderstorms became most intense during the afternoon into the early evening.  There were several tornadoes across northeast into north central Iowa.  Most were relatively brief touchdowns and were in open areas.  Reports of multi-vortex tornadoes were received from Grundy County.  The days most significant tornado touched down in Pocahontas County east of Rolfe and tracked into Humboldt County through the Bradgate area.  The tornado damaged or destroyed over 75% of the town of Bradgate (pop 120).  Tornado damage in Bradgate was very extensive impacting most of the town.  Hardly a building was not impacted.   Outbuildings, light structures and garages were heavily damaged or destroyed.  Two homes were destroyed.  Several homes were damaged ranging from minor to extensive.  The Survey Team found that the majority of the damage was F1, with a few cases of F2 damage.  The F2 damage was to the snapping of very large trees just west of town and the structural damage to two buildings.  Nearly all of the 53 homes there reported damage with several totally destroyed.  Significant widespread property damage was reported in the tornadoes path.  There were several injuries in the town, the most significant being a broken leg.  A police officer was reportedly hit by debris from the tornado and suffered a broken leg.  According to law officials, the damage path of the tornado was 2 miles wide and about 8 miles long.  Storm chasers on the scene reported the tornado itself to be one half mile wide at one point.  Reports of hail were numerous through the afternoon and evening.  Most of the hail was in the three quarter inch to one inch diameter range.  There were a few reports of golf ball size hail from the stronger storms.  Winds were not a major problem with the activity.  There were spotty reports of winds to around 60 MPH, but most reports were in the 40 to 50 MPH range.  One of the stronger wind reports was a 64 MPH wind gust in Tripoli in Bremer County.  This gust was measured by a mesonet station there.  During the mid evening hours, thunderstorms rumbled across Grundy County.  Lightning struck the Sheriff's office there and destroyed a significant amount of office equipment.  Damage was reported around $100,000.

Major flooding occurred on the Turkey River as a result of rainfall totals of 8 to 10 inches.  At Garber, a new all-time flood record was established when the river crested at 32.8 feet on May 23.  The previous flood of record occurred on May 17, 1999 when the river crested at 30.91 feet.  At Elkader, the river crested at 25.6 feet on May 23, which threatened to overtop the dike and forced evacuations on the south side of town.  Numerous homes were so badly damaged, residents had to either rebuild or move to a different location.  The town of Elkport (Clayton County) was also hit hard by flooding, with several bridges and highways damaged by high water.  Damage to roads, structures and agricultural land caused by  flood waters was estimated at nearly 3.5 million dollars.

A moist and unstable airmass was in place over Iowa during the afternoon of the 19th.  A southerly flow in the low levels brought dew point temperatures of the low 70s F. into southern Iowa.  This moist air was being pushed over a warm frontal boundary that lifted northeast across the state.  The airmass became capped over much of the state during the afternoon with a 2.4 degree C. cap in place over southwest Iowa by the early evening.  A small cluster of thunderstorms formed over northwest Iowa during the mid to late afternoon hours.  The storms moved east across the northern tier of Iowa Counties during the late afternoon and evening hours.  They produced hail of three quarters of an inch to an inch in diameter and wind gusts to around 60 MPH.  At one point the storm spiked in intensity.  It produced one and one half inch diameter hail as it passed southwest of Lakota in Kossuth County.

A cold front dropped south across the state during the afternoon of the 20th and by the late afternoon was along the southern border.  Thunderstorms erupted in the unstable air by the late afternoon.  Surface temperatures were in the low to mid 80s with dew point readings in the low to mid 70s.  One of the thunderstorms became severe and dropped three quarter inch diameter hail in Lucas County at Chariton.

The front that moved south across the state during the previous afternoon became stalled over southern Iowa during the evening of the 20th and early morning of the 21st.  The airmass was moderately unstable with CAPE values in the 1500 to 2000 J/kg range.  The low level jet helped to initiate elevated convection.  The thunderstorms fired off from around 600 Mb.  A storm moved through Sac County and produced a 60 MPH wind gust.  Another of the storms produced a 59 MPH wind gust in Franklin County at Latimer.

The synoptic situation was very similar to what it was the previous day.  A complex weather situation was in place due to the convection from the previous night.  An outflow boundary extended east to west across northern Missouri.  The synoptic frontal boundary extended from central Nebraska across northern Iowa.  The air was clear over Iowa during the day, allowing for temperatures to recover.  Dew point values also recovered quite rapidly with readings in the upper 60s to low 70s by the late afternoon hours.  Lifted indices dropped into the -5 to -10 C. range by evening with CAPE values approaching 5000 J/kg.  By the evening hours, the frontal boundary and outflow boundary had converged and were located along Interstate 80.  Explosive thunderstorm development took place during the very late afternoon and evening hours in spite of the weak shear profile.  Discrete supercells formed in a narrow line along Interstate 80 from near Des Moines on west.  The storms became tornadic quickly.  To add insult to injury, a strong thunderstorm complex formed over Nebraska and moved east along the line of convection over Iowa.  As the event took shape, all modes of severe weather occurred.  The storms developed so rapidly that there was little more than a half hour from the first hail and wind report to the first tornado touchdown.  Hail and high wind reports were too numerous to mention individually here.  Numerous reports of golf ball size hail were received.  Boone and Story Counties reported some of the largest hail with the event as baseball size hail pelted the are.  There was one report of very large hail northwest of Slater in Story County.  Hail five and one half inches in diameter punched 8 holes through a steel roof.  There was severe house siding and roof damage in the area.  There were numerous tornadoes, especially in the central Iowa Iowa.  At one point there were several supercells around the metropolitan Des Moines area.  Two of the stronger tornadoes touched down west and south of Des Moines.  Fortunately, most of the tornado tracks were over open areas and resulted in little damage.  High winds became a problem, especially after sunset, as the complex from Nebraska moved in.  The system became more of a derecho during the late night hours.  Eighty plus MPH winds pelted southwest Iowa and downed barns and several buildings in Adams and Cass Counties.  A beta bow element formed in central Iowa shortly before midnight.  The bow echo produced a measured wind gust of 106 MPH in northern Warren County.  The high winds continued to push east along Interstate 80 for the next hour or so before it started to weaken.  The other major problem was with heavy rain.  The convection from the previous night set the stage by saturating soils.  The echos trained for several hours across central Iowa with very heavy rainfall.  Rainfall rates of 4 to 5 inches per hour were common under the heavier cells.  Several locations received 2 to 5 inch rainfalls in under  two hours, resulting in widespread flash flooding.  Reports of major highways being inundated by water were common.  By the end of the night, a swath of 4 to 9 inch rainfall was evident north of Interstate 80.  Longer term river flooding became a major problem by the morning of the 23rd.  There were two lightning related injuries.  Two people were injured when they were struck by lightning out of doors in Nevada.  The lightning struck close by as they were carrying groceries into their home.  The jolt caused them to fall, resulting in laceration to the head.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado tracked through open fields for the most part.  It also lifted from time to time.

A strong low pressure system tracked northeast through eastern Nebraska into Minnesota.  The upper level low pressure came over Iowa during the late afternoon.  Thunderstorms developed with fairly limited CAPE across western Iowa.  The freezing level was quite low, being only around 9500 feet.  The storms took on supercell characteristics as they tracked into the state.  Hail was reported with most of the storms.  Most of the hail was pea to marble size, but the hail fell in copious amounts.  West of town in Adair County, hail accumulated to a depth of 4 cm and nearly closed down Interstate 80 for a short while.  One cell became stronger as it moved into the Des Moines metropolitan area.  The storm produced a funnel cloud over the city for about 15 minutes from 1700 to 1715.  The storm also produced nickle size hail in the Drake University area of the city.

The low pressure mentioned in the narrative above tracked across South Dakota and Minnesota during the evening hours of the 18th.  The airmass, though quite unstable based on the soundings, was significantly capped over Iowa.  The Omaha sounding showed a Lifted Index around -7 C, with CAPE values around 1500 J/kg.  In spite of the instability, the cap over Iowa was quite strong with CIN values around 300 J/kg.  Thunderstorms erupted to the west of the area along a cold front as it entered the state.  Initially, the storms produced severe weather to the west of Iowa where the atmosphere was not as capped.  As the line moved into Iowa, it weakened due to the cap and the presence of much drier air.  Ironically, most of the storms produced lighter wind speeds as they passed than the ambient wind was prior to the storms approach.  Needless to say, this made the task of differentiation between thunderstorm winds and gradient high winds more difficult.  High winds were reported in a few areas.  Thunderstorm winds blew down a barn in Hancock County near Klemme, trapping several head of cattle under the barn.  Trees were also downed in the area.  The ASOS recorded a 58 MPH wind at the Mason City Airport as the storms moved through.  The storms did produce some small hail as they moved through.  Spotty reports showed hail was in the three quarter inch up to nickle size.

A cold front moved southeast slowly southeast across Iowa during the day on the 17th.  The surface front moved about half way through the state, while the front at 850 mb remained over the northwest part of the state.  The airmass was unstable, but not extremely unstable.  Lifted indices were around -2 to -5 C during the afternoon.  The convective inhibition had dropped to zero by mid afternoon with CAPE values around 1500 J/kg.  Thunderstorms erupted in the vicinity to just north of the surface cold front by late afternoon.  An MCS developed over southwest Iowa and tracked east across the state during the late afternoon and evening.  The main severe weather mode from this system was hail.  There were numerous reports of three quarter to one inch diameter hail during the period.  Some of the larger hail reached golf ball to baseball in size.  The largest hail fell in Madison County in the Winterset area as baseball size hail pummeled the area twice.  A few storms produced high winds.  One of the stronger storms was over Adams County.  In what appeared to be a macroburst, winds of around 60 MPH downed power poles and  a few outbuildings east of the Corning area.  The power poles were set on fire as they fell.  Later in the evening, over Monroe and Mahaska Counties, a thunderstorm turned into a bow echo segment and caused considerable damage near the Eddyville area.  One mile east of Eddyville on 340th St. along the Wapello-Mahaska county line was the hardest hit area.  Two farmstead received extensive damage around 9:15 p.m.  A microburst likely occurred on the farmstead located along the north side of the road (western-most farmstead), followed by a damage path spreading one-quarter mile east-southeast to a farmstead along the south side of the road.  At the southern farmstead, two farm outbuildings were destroyed.  Three poles rooted five feet into the ground were lifted complete out of the ground.  The roof of a shed was peeled back.   Pieces of the outbuildings from the northern farmstead spread out to the east and southeast up to 150 yards in width near the southern farmstead.  Length of the visible damage path was around ½ mile.  Persons in both farmsteads reported the duration of the strongest winds to be from 20 to 30 seconds.  Maximum winds were estimated to be around 85 mph.  When all was said and done, high winds flattened a barn and several outbuildings, silos, and trees.  Boards from the barn were driven through a car east of Eddyville along the southern Mahaska County border.  Several roads were blocked by fallen trees throughout the area.  Several hogs were killed when one of the barns collapsed near Eddyville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A farm was damaged by thunderstorm winds. The amount of damage was not known.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, moist, and unstable airmass was in place over Iowa.  The warm front that had produced the convection during the early morning hours of the 8th had become stationary over northern Iowa.  With lifted indices below -10 C. over central Iowa and continued strong moisture advection, thunderstorms erupted over the central and northeast parts of the state during the late afternoon and evening hours.  For the most part, the thunderstorms produced hail during this event.  There were numerous reports of hail of one to two inches in diameter.  Hail reportedly covered the ground in Guthrie County, into parts of Dallas County.  During the later evening hours, the thunderstorms began to sink south and took on a bow echo type configuration, though the line was not a true bow echo.  High winds began to be reported with Cass County being hit the worst.  A swath of damage 7 miles long was reported from west of Lewis to southeast of Lewis.  Embedded in the winds was a small tornado.  The tornado touched down intermittently along the 7 mile path and destroyed or damaged several grain bins and downed numerous trees.  A large corn crib was destroyed southeast of Atlantic and there were numerous reports of trees over highways.  In addition to the hail and high winds, heavy rain occurred from central into parts of west central Iowa.  Flash flooding occurred in Audubon, Guthrie, Madison, and Adair Counties.  Rain fell as the thunderstorms trained and redeveloped repeatedly over the same areas.  Rainfall rates were in the one to three inch per hour range with rainfall total reports of over 4 inches received in a few places in under two hours.  Water flooded over highways, even some U.S. Highways, to as depth of one to two feet.  This resulted in closure of some highways.

A second consecutive night of flash flooding affected much of northeast Iowa, when rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches occurred in three hours.  Law enforcement officials reported water 3 feet deep covering roads in Arlington (Fayette County) and McGregor (Clayton County).  In addition, mudslides were reported between McGregor and Marquette (Clayton County).

The largest hail fell in Madison County in the Winterset area as baseball size hail pummeled the area twice.  A few storms produced high winds.  One of the stronger storms was over Adams County.  In what appeared to be a macroburst, winds of around 60 MPH downed power poles and  a few outbuildings east of the Corning area.  The power poles were set on fire as they fell.  Later in the evening, over Monroe and Mahaska Counties, a thunderstorm turned into a bow echo segment and caused considerable damage near the Eddyville area.  One mile east of Eddyville on 340th St. along the Wapello-Mahaska county line was the hardest hit area.  Two farmstead received extensive damage around 9:15 p.m.  A microburst likely occurred on the farmstead located along the north side of the road (western-most farmstead), followed by a damage path spreading one-quarter mile east-southeast to a farmstead along the south side of the road.  At the southern farmstead, two farm outbuildings were destroyed.  Three poles rooted five feet into the ground were lifted complete out of the ground.  The roof of a shed was peeled back.   Pieces of the outbuildings from the northern farmstead spread out to the east and southeast up to 150 yards in width near the southern farmstead.  Length of the visible damage path was around ½ mile.  Persons in both farmsteads reported the duration of the strongest winds to be from 20 to 30 seconds.  Maximum winds were estimated to be around 85 mph.  When all was said and done, high winds flattened a barn and several outbuildings, silos, and trees.  Boards from the barn were driven through a car east of Eddyville along the southern Mahaska County border.  Several roads were blocked by fallen trees throughout the area.  Several hogs were killed when one of the barns collapsed near Eddyville.

A cold front moved southeast slowly southeast across Iowa during the day on the 17th.  The surface front moved about half way through the state, while the front at 850 mb remained over the northwest part of the state.  The airmass was unstable, but not extremely unstable.  Lifted indices were around -2 to -5 C during the afternoon.  The convective inhibition had dropped to zero by mid afternoon with CAPE values around 1500 J/kg.  Thunderstorms erupted in the vicinity to just north of the surface cold front by late afternoon. A very unstable airmass was over Iowa and helped kick off the seasons first severe weather outbreak.  At the surface a warm frontal boundary extended nearly east to west across the state and provided the focus for thunderstorm development.  By the late afternoon, lifted indices were approaching -10 C. with CAPE values around 5000 J/kg over western Iowa.  Surface temperatures warmed into the upper 80s with dew points in the low to mid 70s.  A southwest surface wind of 15 to 25 kts pushed over the front.  Northeast of the frontal boundary winds were easterly around 10 to 15 kts.  Actually, the surface boundary was further enhanced by outflow from convective complexes during the day, further sharpening the contrast.  Thunderstorms continued to fire along and north of the boundary through the afternoon and into the evening.  This resulted in widespread flash flooding as very heavy rains fell on areas that had had significant rainfall the previous night.  There were reports in north central into northeast Iowa of 2 to 4 inches of rainfall in a little more than an hours time.  Major flooding took place in the Mason City area where evacuations were taking place.  The thunderstorms became most intense during the afternoon into the early evening.  There were several tornadoes across northeast into north central Iowa.  Most were relatively brief touchdowns and were in open areas.  Reports of multi-vortex tornadoes were received from Grundy County.  The days most significant tornado touched down in Pocahontas County east of Rolfe and tracked into Humboldt County through the Bradgate area.  The tornado damaged or destroyed over 75% of the town of Bradgate (pop 120).  Tornado damage in Bradgate was very extensive impacting most of the town.  Hardly a building was not impacted.   Outbuildings, light structures and garages were heavily damaged or destroyed.  Two homes were destroyed.  Several homes were damaged ranging from minor to extensive.  The Survey Team found that the majority of the damage was F1, with a few cases of F2 damage.  The F2 damage was to the snapping of very large trees just west of town and the structural damage to two buildings.  Nearly all of the 53 homes there reported damage with several totally destroyed.  Significant widespread property damage was reported in the tornadoes path.  There were several injuries in the town, the most significant being a broken leg.  A police officer was reportedly hit by debris from the tornado and suffered a broken leg.  According to law officials, the damage path of the tornado was 2 miles wide and about 8 miles long.  Storm chasers on the scene reported the tornado itself to be one half mile wide at one point.  Reports of hail were numerous through the afternoon and evening.  Most of the hail was in the three quarter inch to one inch diameter range.  There were a few reports of golf ball size hail from the stronger storms.  Winds were not a major problem with the activity.  There were spotty reports of winds to around 60 MPH, but most reports were in the 40 to 50 MPH range.  One of the stronger wind reports was a 64 MPH wind gust in Tripoli in Bremer County.  This gust was measured by a mesonet station there.  During the mid evening hours, thunderstorms rumbled across Grundy County.  Lightning struck the Sheriff's office there and destroyed a significant amount of office equipment.  Damage was reported around $100,000.

Once again it was a very active severe weather day over Iowa.  The frontal system that had moved through the state on the night of the 22nd was returning north as a warm front.  This time Iowa did not get completely into the warm sector.  The surface front was dragged north into Iowa by low pressure that moved out of Kansas northeast into central Iowa by the mid to late evening.  The airmass was pushed southeast out of the state by a cold front that passed through the state during the evening and early morning hours.  The airmass in the warm sector became very unstable.  Lifted indices were around -13 C with CAPE values greater than 5000 J/kg by the late afternoon.  The air advected north into Iowa during the day and into the evening.  Thunderstorms erupted in two places.  The first to affect the DSM CWA was the development of several elevated supercells over west central into southwest Iowa.  The storms continued to lift east and northeast across the area.  A few tornadoes touched down, however with the very elevated nature of the storms it was very difficult for the tornadoes to reach the ground.  By the late afternoon, a solid squall line developed along the cold front.  The line took on a Line Echo Wave Pattern by evening.  Several segments of the line bowed out, resulting in high winds.  At times the winds reached 80 MPH.  One of the thunderstorms produced high winds in the Early area of Sac County.  They overturned a semi-tractortrailer truck, causing $80,000 damage and causing minor injuries to the driver.  Tornadoes that formed along this line actually formed where the Rear Flank Downdraft cut through the line.  A clear meso was formed and the small tornadoes advanced around the meso and were in front of the thunderstorm line.  Touchdowns were all brief.  Hail was common with the stronger storm, with much of it in the one to one and three-quarter inch in diameter.  Given the antecedent soil conditions in Iowa, flash flooding became a major issue.  Numerous counties reported flash flooding as rainfall of one to 4 inches in spots resulted in flooded highways.  The thunderstorms formed into an MCC by the early evening hours.  As they moved out of the state, they delivered one more punch to the state.  High winds developed in the wake of the complex.  Several counties of north central, northeast, and central Iowa received high winds.  Damage occurred in several locations to both trees and out buildings.  Sustained winds across the area were in the 25 to 45 MPH range with some higher gusts.  Clarion reported a wind gust of 63 MPH for the highest wind gust reported.

Warm advection took place over Iowa during the late night hours of the 28th into the morning of the 29th.  A warm front was positioned to the south of Iowa, extending from southern Illinois into central Nebraska.  A 50 kt low level jet pushed over the top of the front and resulted in the formation of an MCS.  Most of the thunderstorms were not severe, however there were a few supercells embedded in the MCS which produced large hail.  Golf Ball size hail was reported in parts of Crawford and Sac Counties.  The storms initially produced high winds in Carroll County near Breda, totally destroying a barn.  As the night progressed, the storms took on more of a bow shape.  Several beta bow elements tracked east-southeast into western and central Iowa.  They produced winds of 60 to 65 MPH in a few locations.  High winds downed trees in the Pomeroy area of Calhoun County.  A machine shed was blown over south of Lidderdale in Carroll County.

A strong push of moisture streamed into Iowa on a 50 to 70 kt low level jet.  Lifted indices were around -9 C at the beginning of the event with CAPE in the 2500 to 4000 J/kg range.  Thunderstorms erupted to the west and southwest of Iowa with the initial development being discrete supercells to the southwest, and linear ahead of an approaching cold front to the west.  The storms became much more multi-cellular as they moved into Iowa.  As a result, competition for moisture caused the storms to weaken.  The storms that were severe in Iowa produced high winds in what appears to be several microburst events.  Winds of 74 MPH were recorded at the Ames Airport, with 73 MPH winds measured at a mesonet site in Adair.  A few other reports of severe winds were received with tree damage south of Adair and also in the Earlham area.  There were only a few reports of hail from the storms.  Hail that did occur was limited to an inch or less in diameter.

A warm, moist, and unstable airmass was in place over Iowa.  The warm front that had produced the convection during the early morning hours of the 8th had become stationary over northern Iowa.  With lifted indices below -10 C. over central Iowa and continued strong moisture advection, thunderstorms erupted over the central and northeast parts of the state during the late afternoon and evening hours.  For the most part, the thunderstorms produced hail during this event.  There were numerous reports of hail of one to two inches in diameter.  Hail reportedly covered the ground in Guthrie County, into parts of Dallas County.  During the later evening hours, the thunderstorms began to sink south and took on a bow echo type configuration, though the line was not a true bow echo.  High winds began to be reported with Cass County being hit the worst.  A swath of damage 7 miles long was reported from west of Lewis to southeast of Lewis.  Embedded in the winds was a small tornado.  The tornado touched down intermittently along the 7 mile path and destroyed or damaged several grain bins and downed numerous trees.  A large corn crib was destroyed southeast of Atlantic and there were numerous reports of trees over highways.  In addition to the hail and high winds, heavy rain occurred from central into parts of west central Iowa.  Flash flooding occurred in Audubon, Guthrie, Madison, and Adair Counties.  Rain fell as the thunderstorms trained and redeveloped repeatedly over the same areas.  Rainfall rates were in the one to three inch per hour range with rainfall total reports of over 4 inches received in a few places in under two hours.  Water flooded over highways, even some U.S. Highways, to as depth of one to two feet.  This resulted in closure of some highways.

The synoptic situation was very similar to what it was the previous day.  A complex weather situation was in place due to the convection from the previous night.  An outflow boundary extended east to west across northern Missouri.  The synoptic frontal boundary extended from central Nebraska across northern Iowa.  The air was clear over Iowa during the day, allowing for temperatures to recover.  Dew point values also recovered quite rapidly with readings in the upper 60s to low 70s by the late afternoon hours.  Lifted indices dropped into the -5 to -10 C. range by evening with CAPE values approaching 5000 J/kg.  By the evening hours, the frontal boundary and outflow boundary had converged and were located along Interstate 80.  Explosive thunderstorm development took place during the very late afternoon and evening hours in spite of the weak shear profile.  Discrete supercells formed in a narrow line along Interstate 80 from near Des Moines on west.  The storms became tornadic quickly.  To add insult to injury, a strong thunderstorm complex formed over Nebraska and moved east along the line of convection over Iowa.  As the event took shape, all modes of severe weather occurred.  The storms developed so rapidly that there was little more than a half hour from the first hail and wind report to the first tornado touchdown.  Hail and high wind reports were too numerous to mention individually here.  Numerous reports of golf ball size hail were received.  Boone and Story Counties reported some of the largest hail with the event as baseball size hail pelted the are.  There was one report of very large hail northwest of Slater in Story County.  Hail five and one half inches in diameter punched 8 holes through a steel roof.  There was severe house siding and roof damage in the area.  There were numerous tornadoes, especially in the central Iowa Iowa.  At one point there were several supercells around the metropolitan Des Moines area.  Two of the stronger tornadoes touched down west and south of Des Moines.  Fortunately, most of the tornado tracks were over open areas and resulted in little damage.  High winds became a problem, especially after sunset, as the complex from Nebraska moved in.  The system became more of a derecho during the late night hours.  Eighty plus MPH winds pelted southwest Iowa and downed barns and several buildings in Adams and Cass Counties.  A beta bow element formed in central Iowa shortly before midnight.  The bow echo produced a measured wind gust of 106 MPH in northern Warren County.  The high winds continued to push east along Interstate 80 for the next hour or so before it started to weaken.  The other major problem was with heavy rain.  The convection from the previous night set the stage by saturating soils.  The echos trained for several hours across central Iowa with very heavy rainfall.  Rainfall rates of 4 to 5 inches per hour were common under the heavier cells.  Several locations received 2 to 5 inch rainfalls in under  two hours, resulting in widespread flash flooding.  Reports of major highways being inundated by water were common.  By the end of the night, a swath of 4 to 9 inch rainfall was evident north of Interstate 80.  Longer term river flooding became a major problem by the morning of the 23rd.  There were two lightning related injuries.  Two people were injured when they were struck by lightning out of doors in Nevada.  The lightning struck close by as they were carrying groceries into their home.  The jolt caused them to fall, resulting in laceration to the head.

The synoptic situation was very similar to what it was the previous day.  A complex weather situation was in place due to the convection from the previous night.  An outflow boundary extended east to west across northern Missouri.  The synoptic frontal boundary extended from central Nebraska across northern Iowa.  The air was clear over Iowa during the day, allowing for temperatures to recover.  Dew point values also recovered quite rapidly with readings in the upper 60s to low 70s by the late afternoon hours.  Lifted indices dropped into the -5 to -10 C. range by evening with CAPE values approaching 5000 J/kg.  By the evening hours, the frontal boundary and outflow boundary had converged and were located along Interstate 80.  Explosive thunderstorm development took place during the very late afternoon and evening hours in spite of the weak shear profile.  Discrete supercells formed in a narrow line along Interstate 80 from near Des Moines on west.  The storms became tornadic quickly.  To add insult to injury, a strong thunderstorm complex formed over Nebraska and moved east along the line of convection over Iowa.  As the event took shape, all modes of severe weather occurred.  The storms developed so rapidly that there was little more than a half hour from the first hail and wind report to the first tornado touchdown.  Hail and high wind reports were too numerous to mention individually here.  Numerous reports of golf ball size hail were received.  Boone and Story Counties reported some of the largest hail with the event as baseball size hail pelted the are.  There was one report of very large hail northwest of Slater in Story County.  Hail five and one half inches in diameter punched 8 holes through a steel roof.  There was severe house siding and roof damage in the area.  There were numerous tornadoes, especially in the central Iowa Iowa.  At one point there were several supercells around the metropolitan Des Moines area.  Two of the stronger tornadoes touched down west and south of Des Moines.  Fortunately, most of the tornado tracks were over open areas and resulted in little damage.  High winds became a problem, especially after sunset, as the complex from Nebraska moved in.  The system became more of a derecho during the late night hours.  Eighty plus MPH winds pelted southwest Iowa and downed barns and several buildings in Adams and Cass Counties.  A beta bow element formed in central Iowa shortly before midnight.  The bow echo produced a measured wind gust of 106 MPH in northern Warren County.  The high winds continued to push east along Interstate 80 for the next hour or so before it started to weaken.  The other major problem was with heavy rain.  The convection from the previous night set the stage by saturating soils. Once again it was a very active severe weather day over Iowa.  The frontal system that had moved through the state on the night of the 22nd was returning north as a warm front.  This time Iowa did not get completely into the warm sector.  The surface front was dragged north into Iowa by low pressure that moved out of Kansas northeast into central Iowa by the mid to late evening.  The airmass was pushed southeast out of the state by a cold front that passed through the state during the evening and early morning hours.  The airmass in the warm sector became very unstable.  Lifted indices were around -13 C with CAPE values greater than 5000 J/kg by the late afternoon.  The air advected north into Iowa during the day and into the evening.  Thunderstorms erupted in two places.  The first to affect the DSM CWA was the development of several elevated supercells over west central into southwest Iowa.  The storms continued to lift east and northeast across the area.  A few tornadoes touched down, however with the very elevated nature of the storms it was very difficult for the tornadoes to reach the ground.  By the late afternoon, a solid squall line developed along the cold front.  The line took on a Line Echo Wave Pattern by evening.  Several segments of the line bowed out, resulting in high winds.  At times the winds reached 80 MPH.  One of the thunderstorms produced high winds in the Early area of Sac County.  They overturned a semi-tractortrailer truck, causing $80,000 damage and causing minor injuries to the driver.  Tornadoes that formed along this line actually formed where the Rear Flank Downdraft cut through the line.  A clear meso was formed and the small tornadoes advanced around the meso and were in front of the thunderstorm line.  Touchdowns were all brief.  Hail was common with the stronger storm, with much of it in the one to one and three-quarter inch in diameter.  Given the antecedent soil conditions in Iowa, flash flooding became a major issue.  Numerous counties reported flash flooding as rainfall of one to 4 inches in spots resulted in flooded highways.  The thunderstorms formed into an MCC by the early evening hours.  As they moved out of the state, they delivered one more punch to the state.  High winds developed in the wake of the complex.  Several counties of north central, northeast, and central Iowa received high winds.  Damage occurred in several locations to both trees and out buildings.  Sustained winds across the area were in the 25 to 45 MPH range with some higher gusts.  Clarion reported a wind gust of 63 MPH for the highest wind gust reported.

A warm, moist, and unstable airmass was in place over Iowa.  The warm front that had produced the convection during the early morning hours of the 8th had become stationary over northern Iowa.  With lifted indices below -10 C. over central Iowa and continued strong moisture advection, thunderstorms erupted over the central and northeast parts of the state during the late afternoon and evening hours.  For the most part, the thunderstorms produced hail during this event.  There were numerous reports of hail of one to two inches in diameter.  Hail reportedly covered the ground in Guthrie County, into parts of Dallas County.  During the later evening hours, the thunderstorms began to sink south and took on a bow echo type configuration, though the line was not a true bow echo.  High winds began to be reported with Cass County being hit the worst.  A swath of damage 7 miles long was reported from west of Lewis to southeast of Lewis.  Embedded in the winds was a small tornado.  The tornado touched down intermittently along the 7 mile path and destroyed or damaged several grain bins and downed numerous trees.  A large corn crib was destroyed southeast of Atlantic and there were numerous reports of trees over highways.  In addition to the hail and high winds, heavy rain occurred from central into parts of west central Iowa.  Flash flooding occurred in Audubon, Guthrie, Madison, and Adair Counties.  Rain fell as the thunderstorms trained and redeveloped repeatedly over the same areas.  Rainfall rates were in the one to three inch per hour range with rainfall total reports of over 4 inches received in a few places in under two hours.  Water flooded over highways, even some U.S. Highways, to as depth of one to two feet.  This resulted in closure of some highways.

Numerous reports of large hail were received by law enforcement officials, amateur radio operators, spotters and the public.  Hail sizes ranged from pennies to as large as baseballs.  In fact, hail as large as 3 inches in diameter caused extensive damage in Charles City (Floyd County), knocking out windows and damaging roofs of residences and businesses.  Over 300 vehicles at car dealerships on the south side of Charles City sustained considerable damage due to the large hail, with damage estimates totaling around a half million dollars.

A strong push of moisture streamed into Iowa on a 50 to 70 kt low level jet.  Lifted indices were around -9 C at the beginning of the event with CAPE in the 2500 to 4000 J/kg range.  Thunderstorms erupted to the west and southwest of Iowa with the initial development being discrete supercells to the southwest, and linear ahead of an approaching cold front to the west.  The storms became much more multi-cellular as they moved into Iowa.  As a result, competition for moisture caused the storms to weaken.  The storms that were severe in Iowa produced high winds in what appears to be several microburst events.  Winds of 74 MPH were recorded at the Ames Airport, with 73 MPH winds measured at a mesonet site in Adair.  A few other reports of severe winds were received with tree damage south of Adair and also in the Earlham area.  There were only a few reports of hail from the storms.  Hail that did occur was limited to an inch or less in diameter.
