Very cold air aloft moved in over Iowa under a closed low pressure system aloft.  With strong heating and steep lapse rates, the atmosphere became very unstable.  A strong dust devil formed in Des Moines at a softball field and injured 5 small girls there.  One of the girls, each 5 to 6 years old, was actually picked p off the ground.  She was treated for a fractured ankle.  Later in the afternoon, convection became stronger.  Small showers developed and there reports of cold air funnels.  A landspout touched down in Hancock County north of Hayfield.  The landspout tracked southeast for about 3 miles before dissipating.  It tracked through open fields and did not cause any damage.

The airmass remained unstable over Iowa through the mid day hours of the 30th.  The cold front mentioned above had not yet passed through the state.  At the same time, a mesoscale low pressure system lifted north into the state by mid day.  Thunderstorms erupted along the eastern periphery of the low and became marginally severe over parts of southeast Iowa.  Three quarter inch diameter hail fell in Wapello and Appanoose Counties as the narrow line of storms passed.

The synoptic situation was very similar to what it was the previous day.  A complex weather situation was in place due to the convection from the previous night.  An outflow boundary extended east to west across northern Missouri.  The synoptic frontal boundary extended from central Nebraska across northern Iowa.  The air was clear over Iowa during the day, allowing for temperatures to recover.  Dew point values also recovered quite rapidly with readings in the upper 60s to low 70s by the late afternoon hours.  Lifted indices dropped into the -5 to -10 C. range by evening with CAPE values approaching 5000 J/kg.  By the evening hours, the frontal boundary and outflow boundary had converged and were located along Interstate 80.  Explosive thunderstorm development took place during the very late afternoon and evening hours in spite of the weak shear profile.  Discrete supercells formed in a narrow line along Interstate 80 from near Des Moines on west.  The storms became tornadic quickly.  To add insult to injury, a strong thunderstorm complex formed over Nebraska and moved east along the line of convection over Iowa.  As the event took shape, all modes of severe weather occurred.  The storms developed so rapidly that there was little more than a half hour from the first hail and wind report to the first tornado touchdown.  Hail and high wind reports were too numerous to mention individually here.  Numerous reports of golf ball size hail were received.  Boone and Story Counties reported some of the largest hail with the event as baseball size hail pelted the are.  There was one report of very large hail northwest of Slater in Story County.  Hail five and one half inches in diameter punched 8 holes through a steel roof.  There was severe house siding and roof damage in the area.  There were numerous tornadoes, especially in the central Iowa Iowa.  At one point there were several supercells around the metropolitan Des Moines area.  Two of the stronger tornadoes touched down west and south of Des Moines.  Fortunately, most of the tornado tracks were over open areas and resulted in little damage.  High winds became a problem, especially after sunset, as the complex from Nebraska moved in.  The system became more of a derecho during the late night hours.  Eighty plus MPH winds pelted southwest Iowa and downed barns and several buildings in Adams and Cass Counties.  A beta bow element formed in central Iowa shortly before midnight.  The bow echo produced a measured wind gust of 106 MPH in northern Warren County.  The high winds continued to push east along Interstate 80 for the next hour or so before it started to weaken.  The other major problem was with heavy rain.  The convection from the previous night set the stage by saturating soils.  The echos trained for several hours across central Iowa with very heavy rainfall.  Rainfall rates of 4 to 5 inches per hour were common under the heavier cells.  Several locations received 2 to 5 inch rainfalls in under  two hours, resulting in widespread flash flooding.  Reports of major highways being inundated by water were common.  By the end of the night, a swath of 4 to 9 inch rainfall was evident north of Interstate 80.  Longer term river flooding became a major problem by the morning of the 23rd.  There were two lightning related injuries.  Two people were injured when they were struck by lightning out of doors in Nevada.  The lightning struck close by as they were carrying groceries into their home.  The jolt caused them to fall, resulting in laceration to the head.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief touchdown in open fields.

Once again it was a very active severe weather day over Iowa.  The frontal system that had moved through the state on the night of the 22nd was returning north as a warm front.  This time Iowa did not get completely into the warm sector.  The surface front was dragged north into Iowa by low pressure that moved out of Kansas northeast into central Iowa by the mid to late evening.  The airmass was pushed southeast out of the state by a cold front that passed through the state during the evening and early morning hours.  The airmass in the warm sector became very unstable.  Lifted indices were around -13 C with CAPE values greater than 5000 J/kg by the late afternoon.  The air advected north into Iowa during the day and into the evening.  Thunderstorms erupted in two places.  The first to affect the DSM CWA was the development of several elevated supercells over west central into southwest Iowa.  The storms continued to lift east and northeast across the area.  A few tornadoes touched down, however with the very elevated nature of the storms it was very difficult for the tornadoes to reach the ground.  By the late afternoon, a solid squall line developed along the cold front.  The line took on a Line Echo Wave Pattern by evening.  Several segments of the line bowed out, resulting in high winds.  At times the winds reached 80 MPH.  One of the thunderstorms produced high winds in the Early area of Sac County.  They overturned a semi-tractortrailer truck, causing $80,000 damage and causing minor injuries to the driver.  Tornadoes that formed along this line actually formed where the Rear Flank Downdraft cut through the line.  A clear meso was formed and the small tornadoes advanced around the meso and were in front of the thunderstorm line.  Touchdowns were all brief.  Hail was common with the stronger storm, with much of it in the one to one and three-quarter inch in diameter.  Given the antecedent soil conditions in Iowa, flash flooding became a major issue.  Numerous counties reported flash flooding as rainfall of one to 4 inches in spots resulted in flooded highways.  The thunderstorms formed into an MCC by the early evening hours.  As they moved out of the state, they delivered one more punch to the state.  High winds developed in the wake of the complex.  Several counties of north central, northeast, and central Iowa received high winds.  Damage occurred in several locations to both trees and out buildings.  Sustained winds across the area were in the 25 to 45 MPH range with some higher gusts.  Clarion reported a wind gust of 63 MPH for the highest wind gust reported.

Lifted indices were around -13 C with CAPE values greater than 5000 J/kg by the late afternoon.  The air advected north into Iowa during the day and into the evening.  Thunderstorms erupted in two places.  The first to affect the DSM CWA was the development of several elevated supercells over west central into southwest Iowa.  The storms continued to lift east and northeast across the area.  A few tornadoes touched down, however with the very elevated nature of the storms it was very difficult for the tornadoes to reach the ground.  By the late afternoon, a solid squall line developed along the cold front.  The line took on a Line Echo Wave Pattern by evening.  Several segments of the line bowed out, resulting in high winds.  At times the winds reached 80 MPH.  One of the thunderstorms produced high winds in the Early area of Sac County.  They overturned a semi-tractortrailer truck, causing $80,000 damage and causing minor injuries to the driver.  Tornadoes that formed along this line actually formed where the Rear Flank Downdraft cut through the line.  A clear meso was formed and the small tornadoes advanced around the meso and were in front of the thunderstorm line.  Touchdowns were all brief.  Hail was common with the stronger storm, with much of it in the one to one and three-quarter inch in diameter.  Given the antecedent soil conditions in Iowa, flash flooding became a major issue.  Numerous counties reported flash flooding as rainfall of one to 4 inches in spots resulted in flooded highways.  The thunderstorms formed into an MCC by the early evening hours.  As they moved out of the state, they delivered one more punch to the state.  High winds developed in the wake of the complex.  Several counties of north central, northeast, and central Iowa received high winds.  Damage occurred in several locations to both trees and out buildings.  Sustained winds across the area were in the 25 to 45 MPH range with some higher gusts.  Clarion reported a wind gust of 63 MPH for the highest wind gust reported.

A warm, moist, and unstable airmass was in place over Iowa.  The warm front that had produced the convection during the early morning hours of the 8th had become stationary over northern Iowa.  With lifted indices below -10 C. over central Iowa and continued strong moisture advection, thunderstorms erupted over the central and northeast parts of the state during the late afternoon and evening hours.  For the most part, the thunderstorms produced hail during this event.  There were numerous reports of hail of one to two inches in diameter.  Hail reportedly covered the ground in Guthrie County, into parts of Dallas County.  During the later evening hours, the thunderstorms began to sink south and took on a bow echo type configuration, though the line was not a true bow echo.  High winds began to be reported with Cass County being hit the worst.  A swath of damage 7 miles long was reported from west of Lewis to southeast of Lewis.  Embedded in the winds was a small tornado.  The tornado touched down intermittently along the 7 mile path and destroyed or damaged several grain bins and downed numerous trees.  A large corn crib was destroyed southeast of Atlantic and there were numerous reports of trees over highways.  In addition to the hail and high winds, heavy rain occurred from central into parts of west central Iowa.  Flash flooding occurred in Audubon, Guthrie, Madison, and Adair Counties.  Rain fell as the thunderstorms trained and redeveloped repeatedly over the same areas.  Rainfall rates were in the one to three inch per hour range with rainfall total reports of over 4 inches received in a few places in under two hours.  Water flooded over highways, even some U.S. Highways, to as depth of one to two feet.  This resulted in closure of some highways.

May 2004 began rather dry with an average of only 0.19 inches of rain over the first week of the month.  However, the second week of May brought seasonally normal rainfall.  Heavy rain occurred during the third week of the month.  May's greatest rain events came back to back.  The first round of heavy rain producing thunderstorms began on the morning of the 21st and continued into the morning of the 22ns.  Heaviest rains were in North Central and Northeast Iowa where storm totals included 4.75 inches at Emmetsburg, 6.22 inches at Mason City, and 5.14 inches at Decorah.  Another round of storms began on the afternoon of the 22nd and continued into the morning of the 23rd.  Some of the rain totals reported with this second series of storms included 6.67 inches near Ames and 4.15 inches at Marshalltown.  A statewide average of 2.97 inches of rain fell from these two systems.  Locally heavy rain continued to fall through much of the remainder of the month.  The greatest rainfall amount occurred during the early morning hours of the 30th when 4.37 inches of rain fell at Fairfield.  Damage reports were still coming in but it does appear from preliminary reports that roughly six percent of Iowa's 2004 crop was flood out.  Based on a $23 per acre cost to replant it is estimated that the floods have cost Iowa farmers some $15.2 million dollars.  The rainfall that occurred over the later part of May was Iowa's greatest since July 1993.  Rainfall totals for the month varied form 4.01 inches at Burlington to 14.87 inches at Lansing.  Radar derived estimates suggest that May rainfall was actually below normal in some areas over the far Northwest...such as Buena Vista and Van Buren Counties.  However, complete rain gage reports were not yet available for these areas.  Excessive rainfall produced major flooding in many areas across the state during the second half of May.  This was the first widespread major flood event in many years.  The heaviest rainfall occurred over the Northeastern sections of the state.  Additionally, several episodes of severe thunderstorms produced large hail, high winds, and tornadoes during May.  Severe weather was reported on 15 days during the month with all but 7 of Iowa's 99 counties reporting severe thunderstorm events.  The month's largest severe weather outbreaks occurred on the 8th (26 counties, mostly west central to northeast) ; 17th (20 counties, mostly southwest to east central); 21st (37 counties, mostly northwest to east central); 22nd (31 counties, mostly southwest to east central) and the 24th (33 counties, mostly southwest one half).  An exact tornado count is not yet available for the state, but central Iowa did report 32 tornadoes for the month with an estimated total of 50 tornadoes statewide.  Nearly all were F0 and F1 intensity with minimal damage to communities.  There was a tornado of F2 intensity that did produce significant damage to the communities of Bradgate and Palo (both on May 21).

Thunderstorms erupted in northwest and north central Iowa as a warm front lifted north into the state during the night.  A strong push of moisture was drawn north over the cooler air at the surface.  The thunderstorms were elevated in nature.  Being such, the mode of severe weather was hail.  Hail of  three quarters of an inch to one inch in diameter was reported in many locations across the northwest and north central parts of the state.  The storms continued through the night before the low level jet weakened around sunrise.

The low pressure mentioned in the narrative above tracked across South Dakota and Minnesota during the evening hours of the 18th.  The airmass, though quite unstable based on the soundings, was significantly capped over Iowa.  The Omaha sounding showed a Lifted Index around -7 C, with CAPE values around 1500 J/kg.  In spite of the instability, the cap over Iowa was quite strong with CIN values around 300 J/kg.  Thunderstorms erupted to the west of the area along a cold front as it entered the state.  Initially, the storms produced severe weather to the west of Iowa where the atmosphere was not as capped.  As the line moved into Iowa, it weakened due to the cap and the presence of much drier air.  Ironically, most of the storms produced lighter wind speeds as they passed than the ambient wind was prior to the storms approach.  Needless to say, this made the task of differentiation between thunderstorm winds and gradient high winds more difficult.  High winds were reported in a few areas.  Thunderstorm winds blew down a barn in Hancock County near Klemme, trapping several head of cattle under the barn.  Trees were also downed in the area.  The ASOS recorded a 58 MPH wind at the Mason City Airport as the storms moved through.  The storms did produce some small hail as they moved through.  Spotty reports showed hail was in the three quarter inch up to nickle size.

An intense low pressure moved across the northern tier of states during the day on the 27th.  Very strong south-southwest winds developed with profilers indicating wind of 50 to 65 kts within the lowest range gates.  Temperatures warmed into the mid 80s to low 90s with the atmosphere becoming adiabatic to between 750 and 600 mb, allowing for strong mixing down to the surface.  Winds increased during the day and reached high wind criteria by the early afternoon.  Most of the state saw sustained winds of 40 MPH or higher.  Several locations gusting to between 60 and 65 MPH.  The winds also produced blowing dust around the state.  Isolated reports of visibility reduced to near zero were received.  Spotty damage occurred around the state.  An 18 inch tree limb broke off and fell through the roof of a house in Windsor Heights.  A two to three foot diameter tree was snapped off at the base in Ames.  There were numerous reports of power outages caused by downed tree limbs.  In Union County, a 200 X 40 foot chicken house had the roof blown off.  There were several head of cattle in addition to the chickens contained therein trapped in the debris.
Several reports of large hail were received from amateur radio operators, spotters and law enforcement officials.  Hail sizes varied from pennies to golf balls to tennis balls.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado caused no reported damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, moist, and unstable airmass was in place over Iowa.  The warm front that had produced the convection during the early morning hours of the 8th had become stationary over northern Iowa.  With lifted indices below -10 C. over central Iowa and continued strong moisture advection, thunderstorms erupted over the central and northeast parts of the state during the late afternoon and evening hours.  For the most part, the thunderstorms produced hail during this event.  There were numerous reports of hail of one to two inches in diameter.  Hail reportedly covered the ground in Guthrie County, into parts of Dallas County.  During the later evening hours, the thunderstorms began to sink south and took on a bow echo type configuration, though the line was not a true bow echo.  High winds began to be reported with Cass County being hit the worst.  A swath of damage 7 miles long was reported from west of Lewis to southeast of Lewis.  Embedded in the winds was a small tornado.  The tornado touched down intermittently along the 7 mile path and destroyed or damaged several grain bins and downed numerous trees.  A large corn crib was destroyed southeast of Atlantic and there were numerous reports of trees over highways.  In addition to the hail and high winds, heavy rain occurred from central into parts of west central Iowa.  Flash flooding occurred in Audubon, Guthrie, Madison, and Adair Counties.  Rain fell as the thunderstorms trained and redeveloped repeatedly over the same areas.  Rainfall rates were in the one to three inch per hour range with rainfall total reports of over 4 inches received in a few places in under two hours.  Water flooded over highways, even some U.S. Highways, to as depth of one to two feet.  This resulted in closure of some highways.

Once again it was a very active severe weather day over Iowa.  The frontal system that had moved through the state on the night of the 22nd was returning north as a warm front.  This time Iowa did not get completely into the warm sector.  The surface front was dragged north into Iowa by low pressure that moved out of Kansas northeast into central Iowa by the mid to late evening.  The airmass was pushed southeast out of the state by a cold front that passed through the state during the evening and early morning hours.  The airmass in the warm sector became very unstable.  Lifted indices were around -13 C with CAPE values greater than 5000 J/kg by the late afternoon.  The air advected north into Iowa during the day and into the evening.  Thunderstorms erupted in two places.  The first to affect the DSM CWA was the development of several elevated supercells over west central into southwest Iowa.  The storms continued to lift east and northeast across the area.  A few tornadoes touched down, however with the very elevated nature of the storms it was very difficult for the tornadoes to reach the ground.  By the late afternoon, a solid squall line developed along the cold front.  The line took on a Line Echo Wave Pattern by evening.  Several segments of the line bowed out, resulting in high winds.  At times the winds reached 80 MPH.  One of the thunderstorms produced high winds in the Early area of Sac County.  They overturned a semi-tractortrailer truck, causing $80,000 damage and causing minor injuries to the driver.  Tornadoes that formed along this line actually formed where the Rear Flank Downdraft cut through the line.  A clear meso was formed and the small tornadoes advanced around the meso and were in front of the thunderstorm line.  Touchdowns were all brief.  Hail was common with the stronger storm, with much of it in the one to one and three-quarter inch in diameter.  Given the antecedent soil conditions in Iowa, flash flooding became a major issue.  Numerous counties reported flash flooding as rainfall of one to 4 inches in spots resulted in flooded highways.  The thunderstorms formed into an MCC by the early evening hours.  As they moved out of the state, they delivered one more punch to the state.  High winds developed in the wake of the complex.  Several counties of north central, northeast, and central Iowa received high winds.  Damage occurred in several locations to both trees and out buildings.  Sustained winds across the area were in the 25 to 45 MPH range with some higher gusts.  Clarion reported a wind gust of 63 MPH for the highest wind gust reported.

Once again it was a very active severe weather day over Iowa.  The frontal system that had moved through the state on the night of the 22nd was returning north as a warm front.  This time Iowa did not get completely into the warm sector.  The surface front was dragged north into Iowa by low pressure that moved out of Kansas northeast into central Iowa by the mid to late evening.  The airmass was pushed southeast out of the state by a cold front that passed through the state during the evening and early morning hours.  The airmass in the warm sector became very unstable.  Lifted indices were around -13 C with CAPE values greater than 5000 J/kg by the late afternoon.  The air advected north into Iowa during the day and into the evening.  Thunderstorms erupted in two places.  The first to affect the DSM CWA was the development of several elevated supercells over west central into southwest Iowa.  The storms continued to lift east and northeast across the area.  A few tornadoes touched down, however with the very elevated nature of the storms it was very difficult for the tornadoes to reach the ground.  By the late afternoon, a solid squall line developed along the cold front.  The line took on a Line Echo Wave Pattern by evening.  Several segments of the line bowed out, resulting in high winds.  At times the winds reached 80 MPH.  One of the thunderstorms produced high winds in the Early area of Sac County.  They overturned a semi-tractortrailer truck, causing $80,000 damage and causing minor injuries to the driver.  Tornadoes that formed along this line actually formed where the Rear Flank Downdraft cut through the line.  A clear meso was formed and the small tornadoes advanced around the meso and were in front of the thunderstorm line.  Touchdowns were all brief.  Hail was common with the stronger storm, with much of it in the one to one and three-quarter inch in diameter.  Given the antecedent soil conditions in Iowa, flash flooding became a major issue.  Numerous counties reported flash flooding as rainfall of one to 4 inches in spots resulted in flooded highways.  The thunderstorms formed into an MCC by the early evening hours.  As they moved out of the state, they delivered one more punch to the state.  High winds developed in the wake of the complex.  Several counties of north central, northeast, and central Iowa received high winds.  Damage occurred in several locations to both trees and out buildings.  Sustained winds across the area were in the 25 to 45 MPH range with some higher gusts.  Clarion reported a wind gust of 63 MPH for the highest wind gust reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado was a brief touchdown in open areas.  The tornado itself was actually embedded within a large area of straight line winds.

The low pressure mentioned in the narrative above tracked across South Dakota and Minnesota during the evening hours of the 18th.  The airmass, though quite unstable based on the soundings, was significantly capped over Iowa.  The Omaha sounding showed a Lifted Index around -7 C, with CAPE values around 1500 J/kg.  In spite of the instability, the cap over Iowa was quite strong with CIN values around 300 J/kg.  Thunderstorms erupted to the west of the area along a cold front as it entered the state.  Initially, the storms produced severe weather to the west of Iowa where the atmosphere was not as capped.  As the line moved into Iowa, it weakened due to the cap and the presence of much drier air.  Ironically, most of the storms produced lighter wind speeds as they passed than the ambient wind was prior to the storms approach.  Needless to say, this made the task of differentiation between thunderstorm winds and gradient high winds more difficult.  High winds were reported in a few areas.  Thunderstorm winds blew down a barn in Hancock County near Klemme, trapping several head of cattle under the barn.  Trees were also downed in the area.  The ASOS recorded a 58 MPH wind at the Mason City Airport as the storms moved through.  The storms did produce some small hail as they moved through.  Spotty reports showed hail was in the three quarter inch up to nickle size.
Several reports of large hail were received from amateur radio operators, spotters and law enforcement officials.  Hail sizes varied from pennies to golf balls to tennis balls.

A weak trough of low pressure was over southern Iowa as a reinforcing cold front dropped southeast into the state.  The airmass was moderately unstable with dew point readings in the 60s.  Surface low tracked east across central Missouri during the mid part of the day.  Thunderstorms erupted across southern Iowa.  They formed into a line and became severe.  The main severe weather mode was hail.  There were numerous reports of three quarter inch to one and one quarter inch diameter hail over far southern Iowa.  Golf ball size hail pelted parts of Decatur County.  The combination of one inch diameter hail and 50 MPH winds caused considerable crop damage in Taylor County.  A swath 8 miles long and 3 miles wide was destroyed in an area extending from the hamlet of Siam, ease-southeast to the county border south of Bedford.  Heavy rainfall at the rage of of one to two inches per hour resulted in flash flooding in Ringgold County with water running over roads north of Mount Ayr.

A warm, moist, and unstable airmass was in place over Iowa.  The warm front that had produced the convection during the early morning hours of the 8th had become stationary over northern Iowa.  With lifted indices below -10 C. over central Iowa and continued strong moisture advection, thunderstorms erupted over the central and northeast parts of the state during the late afternoon and evening hours.  For the most part, the thunderstorms produced hail during this event.  There were numerous reports of hail of one to two inches in diameter.  Hail reportedly covered the ground in Guthrie County, into parts of Dallas County.  During the later evening hours, the thunderstorms began to sink south and took on a bow echo type configuration, though the line was not a true bow echo.  High winds began to be reported with Cass County being hit the worst.  A swath of damage 7 miles long was reported from west of Lewis to southeast of Lewis.  Embedded in the winds was a small tornado.  The tornado touched down intermittently along the 7 mile path and destroyed or damaged several grain bins and downed numerous trees.  A large corn crib was destroyed southeast of Atlantic and there were numerous reports of trees over highways.  In addition to the hail and high winds, heavy rain occurred from central into parts of west central Iowa.  Flash flooding occurred in Audubon, Guthrie, Madison, and Adair Counties.  Rain fell as the thunderstorms trained and redeveloped repeatedly over the same areas.  Rainfall rates were in the one to three inch per hour range with rainfall total reports of over 4 inches received in a few places in under two hours.  Water flooded over highways, even some U.S. Highways, to as depth of one to two feet.  This resulted in closure of some highways.

An intense low pressure moved across the northern tier of states during the day on the 27th.  Very strong south-southwest winds developed with profilers indicating wind of 50 to 65 kts within the lowest range gates.  Temperatures warmed into the mid 80s to low 90s with the atmosphere becoming adiabatic to between 750 and 600 mb, allowing for strong mixing down to the surface.  Winds increased during the day and reached high wind criteria by the early afternoon.  Most of the state saw sustained winds of 40 MPH or higher.  Several locations gusting to between 60 and 65 MPH.  The winds also produced blowing dust around the state.  Isolated reports of visibility reduced to near zero were received.  Spotty damage occurred around the state.  An 18 inch tree limb broke off and fell through the roof of a house in Windsor Heights.  A two to three foot diameter tree was snapped off at the base in Ames.  There were numerous reports of power outages caused by downed tree limbs.  In Union County, a 200 X 40 foot chicken house had the roof blown off.  There were several head of cattle in addition to the chickens contained therein trapped in the debris.

A warm front lifted north across the state during the previous night.  At the same time, intense low pressure formed to the west of Iowa and tracked northeast through South Dakota into Minnesota.  The low intensified as a negatively tilted upper level trough of low pressure in the upper atmosphere lifted northeast out of the southwest U.S.  A strong jet and pronounced dry slot was evident in the warm sector of the system.  The jet and dry slot tracked across Iowa during the afternoon and evening of the 18th.  This set the stage for the high wind event.  A very strong south to southwest flow developed over the state.  Profiler data showed wind of 50 kts at 500 meters during much of the morning into the early part of the afternoon.  The winds increased to 75 kts at the 500 meter level by the late afternoon and evening hours.  The strong winds in the lowest range gates were not realized during much of the day do to strong upward vertical velocities.  Strong subsidence developed during the late afternoon and evening hours, allowing for sufficient mixing and for the strong winds to be realized at the surface.  During the day, winds were in the 30 to 35 MPH range across the state, with frequent gusts to around 55 MPH.  During the late afternoon and early evening hours, winds increased with sustained winds of 40 MPH or more across most of the state, with many areas reporting gusts of 60 to 65 MPH.  Widespread light damage was reported over the area, with scattered reports of significant wind damage.   Several reports of tree, power line, and out building damage were received from around the state.  Reports of semi-tractortrailers being blown off the roads.  U.S. Highway 18 in Cerro Gordo County was closed by two overturned trucks that were blown over by the wind.  Also, traffic was rerouted in Warren County at the junction of U.S. Highway 65-69 due to a truck being blown over.  In addition to the wind damage problems, dry conditions around the state lead to areas of blowing dust.  Visibilities were restricted at times, especially over the west half of the state.  In Kossuth County, the visibility dropped to as low as three eigths of a mile at the Algona Airport for about an hour.

In addition to the wind damage problems, dry conditions around the state lead to areas of blowing dust.  Visibilities were restricted at times, especially over the west half of the state.  In Kossuth County, the visibility dropped to as low as three eigths of a mile at the Algona Airport for about an hour.

A warm front lifted north across the state during the previous night.  At the same time, intense low pressure formed to the west of Iowa and tracked northeast through South Dakota into Minnesota.  The low intensified as a negatively tilted upper level trough of low pressure in the upper atmosphere lifted northeast out of the southwest U.S.  A strong jet and pronounced dry slot was evident in the warm sector of the system.  The jet and dry slot tracked across Iowa during the afternoon and evening of the 18th.  This set the stage for the high wind event.  A very strong south to southwest flow developed over the state.  Profiler data showed wind of 50 kts at 500 meters during much of the morning into the early part of the afternoon.  The winds increased to 75 kts at the 500 meter level by the late afternoon and evening hours.  The strong winds in the lowest range gates were not realized during much of the day do to strong upward vertical velocities.  Strong subsidence developed during the late afternoon and evening hours, allowing for sufficient mixing and for the strong winds to be realized at the surface.  During the day, winds were in the 30 to 35 MPH range across the state, with frequent gusts to around 55 MPH.  During the late afternoon and early evening hours, winds increased with sustained winds of 40 MPH or more across most of the state, with many areas reporting gusts of 60 to 65 MPH.  Widespread light damage was reported over the area, with scattered reports of significant wind damage.   Several reports of tree, power line, and out building damage were received from around the state.  Reports of semi-tractortrailers being blown off the roads.  U.S. Highway 18 in Cerro Gordo County was closed by two overturned trucks that were blown over by the wind.  Also, traffic was rerouted in Warren County at the junction of U.S. Highway 65-69 due to a truck being blown over.  In addition to the wind damage problems, dry conditions around the state lead to areas of blowing dust.  Visibilities were restricted at times, especially over the west half of the state.  In Kossuth County, the visibility dropped to as low as three eigths of a mile at the Algona Airport for about an hour.

An intense low pressure moved across the northern tier of states during the day on the 27th.  Very strong south-southwest winds developed with profilers indicating wind of 50 to 65 kts within the lowest range gates.  Temperatures warmed into the mid 80s to low 90s with the atmosphere becoming adiabatic to between 750 and 600 mb, allowing for strong mixing down to the surface.  Winds increased during the day and reached high wind criteria by the early afternoon.  Most of the state saw sustained winds of 40 MPH or higher.  Several locations gusting to between 60 and 65 MPH.  The winds also produced blowing dust around the state.  Isolated reports of visibility reduced to near zero were received.  Spotty damage occurred around the state.  An 18 inch tree limb broke off and fell through the roof of a house in Windsor Heights.  A two to three foot diameter tree was snapped off at the base in Ames.  There were numerous reports of power outages caused by downed tree limbs.  In Union County, a 200 X 40 foot chicken house had the roof blown off.  There were several head of cattle in addition to the chickens contained therein trapped in the debris.

A warm, moist, and unstable airmass was in place over Iowa.  The warm front that had produced the convection during the early morning hours of the 8th had become stationary over northern Iowa.  With lifted indices below -10 C. over central Iowa and continued strong moisture advection, thunderstorms erupted over the central and northeast parts of the state during the late afternoon and evening hours.  For the most part, the thunderstorms produced hail during this event.  There were numerous reports of hail of one to two inches in diameter.  Hail reportedly covered the ground in Guthrie County, into parts of Dallas County.  During the later evening hours, the thunderstorms began to sink south and took on a bow echo type configuration, though the line was not a true bow echo.  High winds began to be reported with Cass County being hit the worst.  A swath of damage 7 miles long was reported from west of Lewis to southeast of Lewis.  Embedded in the winds was a small tornado.  The tornado touched down intermittently along the 7 mile path and destroyed or damaged several grain bins and downed numerous trees.  A large corn crib was destroyed southeast of Atlantic and there were numerous reports of trees over highways.  In addition to the hail and high winds, heavy rain occurred from central into parts of west central Iowa.  Flash flooding occurred in Audubon, Guthrie, Madison, and Adair Counties.  Rain fell as the thunderstorms trained and redeveloped repeatedly over the same areas.  Rainfall rates were in the one to three inch per hour range with rainfall total reports of over 4 inches received in a few places in under two hours.  Water flooded over highways, even some U.S. Highways, to as depth of one to two feet.  This resulted in closure of some highways.

The cold front that had moved southeast across the state the previous night became stationary across southern Iowa by the morning of the 17th.  A sharp contrast in dew point temperatures was found across the front.  Dew points were in the upper 50s to mid 60s south of the front, with dew points in the 30s north of it.  During the late afternoon the front began to return north as a warm front.  By evening a strengthening southerly jet in the low levels impinged on the front with wind speeds around 50 kts.  The overall low levels of the atmosphere were relatively dry and the airmass was capped initially.  The LCL height was around 750 mb.  There atmosphere was quite unstable with Lifted Indices in the -6 to -10 C range.  CAPE values were in the 2500 to 3500 J/kg range.  Thunderstorms erupted over northern Iowa shortly after sunset.  The storms formed into discrete supercells.  A tornado touched down east of Mason City from one of the stronger cells.  It was in open area and did not cause any damage during its brief touchdown.  The dominant severe weather mode for the event was hail.  Baseball size hail fell in Palo Alto County in Mallard.  There were numerous reports of hail three quarters of an inch to one and one quarter of an inch in diameter.  The largest hail was around two inches in diameter in Palo Alto County.  Hail reportedly covered the ground as the storms passed.  During the late stages of the event, a thunderstorms produced high winds as it swept across Interstate 35 west of Northwood in Worth County.  Two vehicles and one semi-tractortrailer truck were blown off of the interstate near mile post 214.  In addition, one telephone was blown down by the high wind.

A very warm and unstable airmass was in place over Iowa.  The moisture was in limited supply with this event.  A cold front approached the state from the northwest.  This front moved into the state during the late afternoon and evening of the 16th.  Moisture was limited with this system.  Surface dew points were in the mid 50s to around 60 during the early part of the afternoon over the southeast half of the state.  The moisture was very shallow.  Afternoon mixing lowered dew points into the upper 40s over the southeast, and into the upper 20s and 30s over the northwest just ahead of the front.  Temperatures warmed into mid 80s to low 90s which made the airmass quite unstable.  CAPE values were around 1000 J/kg with Lifted Indices around -4 C. just ahead of the front.  Convection had a hard time getting organized at first with only spotty cells forming ahead of the front.  By early evening, a small MCS formed over western Iowa and advanced east-southeast across the state.  The MCS produced hail in many locations along with gusty winds.  In spite of the "inverted V" type upper air soundings, high winds were not as widespread as would normally be expected.  Several locations did report gusts to around 60 MPH.  High winds blew a car off of Interstate 80 in Poweshiek County southeast of Malcom.  A wind gust of 70 MPH was reported at the Ames Airport in Story County.  For the most part, the hail was in the three quarter to one inch diameter size.  Hail did cover the ground in Carroll County at Lidderdale.  The largest hail report came from Guthrie County north of Panora into the Panora area.  Golf ball size hail was reported at that location.  There were several reports of gutter damage and dents in car hoods due to the hail in Panora.  Overall, damage was limited with this event since tree leaves were only beginning to come out and the crops had not yet been planted.

A weak trough of low pressure was over southern Iowa as a reinforcing cold front dropped southeast into the state.  The airmass was moderately unstable with dew point readings in the 60s.  Surface low tracked east across central Missouri during the mid part of the day.  Thunderstorms erupted across southern Iowa.  They formed into a line and became severe.  The main severe weather mode was hail.  There were numerous reports of three quarter inch to one and one quarter inch diameter hail over far southern Iowa.  Golf ball size hail pelted parts of Decatur County.  The combination of one inch diameter hail and 50 MPH winds caused considerable crop damage in Taylor County.  A swath 8 miles long and 3 miles wide was destroyed in an area extending from the hamlet of Siam, ease-southeast to the county border south of Bedford.  Heavy rainfall at the rage of of one to two inches per hour resulted in flash flooding in Ringgold County with water running over roads north of Mount Ayr.

Thunderstorms erupted in northwest and north central Iowa as a warm front lifted north into the state during the night.  A strong push of moisture was drawn north over the cooler air at the surface.  The thunderstorms were elevated in nature.  Being such, the mode of severe weather was hail.  Hail of  three quarters of an inch to one inch in diameter was reported in many locations across the northwest and north central parts of the state.  The storms continued through the night before the low level jet weakened around sunrise.

May 2004 began rather dry with an average of only 0.19 inches of rain over the first week of the month.  However, the second week of May brought seasonally normal rainfall.  Heavy rain occurred during the third week of the month.  May's greatest rain events came back to back.  The first round of heavy rain producing thunderstorms began on the morning of the 21st and continued into the morning of the 22ns.  Heaviest rains were in North Central and Northeast Iowa where storm totals included 4.75 inches at Emmetsburg, 6.22 inches at Mason City, and 5.14 inches at Decorah.  Another round of storms began on the afternoon of the 22nd and continued into the morning of the 23rd.  Some of the rain totals reported with this second series of storms included 6.67 inches near Ames and 4.15 inches at Marshalltown.  A statewide average of 2.97 inches of rain fell from these two systems.  Locally heavy rain continued to fall through much of the remainder of the month.  The greatest rainfall amount occurred during the early morning hours of the 30th when 4.37 inches of rain fell at Fairfield.  Damage reports were still coming in but it does appear from preliminary reports that roughly six percent of Iowa's 2004 crop was flood out.  Based on a $23 per acre cost to replant it is estimated that the floods have cost Iowa farmers some $15.2 million dollars.  The rainfall that occurred over the later part of May was Iowa's greatest since July 1993.  Rainfall totals for the month varied form 4.01 inches at Burlington to 14.87 inches at Lansing.  Radar derived estimates suggest that May rainfall was actually below normal in some areas over the far Northwest...such as Buena Vista and Van Buren Counties.  However, complete rain gage reports were not yet available for these areas.  Excessive rainfall produced major flooding in many areas across the state during the second half of May.  This was the first widespread major flood event in many years.  The heaviest rainfall occurred over the Northeastern sections of the state.  Additionally, several episodes of severe thunderstorms produced large hail, high winds, and tornadoes during May.  Severe weather was reported on 15 days during the month with all but 7 of Iowa's 99 counties reporting severe thunderstorm events.  The month's largest severe weather outbreaks occurred on the 8th (26 counties, mostly west central to northeast) ; 17th (20 counties, mostly southwest to east central); 21st (37 counties, mostly northwest to east central); 22nd (31 counties, mostly southwest to east central) and the 24th (33 counties, mostly southwest one half).  An exact tornado count is not yet available for the state, but central Iowa did report 32 tornadoes for the month with an estimated total of 50 tornadoes statewide.  Nearly all were F0 and F1 intensity with minimal damage to communities.  There was a tornado of F2 intensity that did produce significant damage to the communities of Bradgate and Palo (both on May 21).

The month's largest severe weather outbreaks occurred on the 8th (26 counties, mostly west central to northeast) ; 17th (20 counties, mostly southwest to east central); 21st (37 counties, mostly northwest to east central); 22nd (31 counties, mostly southwest to east central) and the 24th (33 counties, mostly southwest one half).  An exact tornado count is not yet available for the state, but central Iowa did report 32 tornadoes for the month with an estimated total of 50 tornadoes statewide.  Nearly all were F0 and F1 intensity with minimal damage to communities.  There was a tornado of F2 intensity that did produce significant damage to the communities of Bradgate and Palo (both on May 21).

Once again it was a very active severe weather day over Iowa.  The frontal system that had moved through the state on the night of the 22nd was returning north as a warm front.  This time Iowa did not get completely into the warm sector.  The surface front was dragged north into Iowa by low pressure that moved out of Kansas northeast into central Iowa by the mid to late evening.  The airmass was pushed southeast out of the state by a cold front that passed through the state during the evening and early morning hours.  The airmass in the warm sector became very unstable.  Lifted indices were around -13 C with CAPE values greater than 5000 J/kg by the late afternoon.  The air advected north into Iowa during the day and into the evening.  Thunderstorms erupted in two places.  The first to affect the DSM CWA was the development of several elevated supercells over west central into southwest Iowa.  The storms continued to lift east and northeast across the area.  A few tornadoes touched down, however with the very elevated nature of the storms it was very difficult for the tornadoes to reach the ground.  By the late afternoon, a solid squall line developed along the cold front.  The line took on a Line Echo Wave Pattern by evening.  Several segments of the line bowed out, resulting in high winds.  At times the winds reached 80 MPH.  One of the thunderstorms produced high winds in the Early area of Sac County.  They overturned a semi-tractortrailer truck, causing $80,000 damage and causing minor injuries to the driver.  Tornadoes that formed along this line actually formed where the Rear Flank Downdraft cut through the line.  A clear meso was formed and the small tornadoes advanced around the meso and were in front of the thunderstorm line.  Touchdowns were all brief.  Hail was common with the stronger storm, with much of it in the one to one and three-quarter inch in diameter.  Given the antecedent soil conditions in Iowa, flash flooding became a major issue.  Numerous counties reported flash flooding as rainfall of one to 4 inches in spots resulted in flooded highways.  The thunderstorms formed into an MCC by the early evening hours.  As they moved out of the state, they delivered one more punch to the state.  High winds developed in the wake of the complex.  Several counties of north central, northeast, and central Iowa received high winds.  Damage occurred in several locations to both trees and out buildings.  Sustained winds across the area were in the 25 to 45 MPH range with some higher gusts.  Clarion reported a wind gust of 63 MPH for the highest wind gust reported.

A warm, moist, and unstable airmass was in place over Iowa.  The warm front that had produced the convection during the early morning hours of the 8th had become stationary over northern Iowa.  With lifted indices below -10 C. over central Iowa and continued strong moisture advection, thunderstorms erupted over the central and northeast parts of the state during the late afternoon and evening hours.  For the most part, the thunderstorms produced hail during this event.  There were numerous reports of hail of one to two inches in diameter.  Hail reportedly covered the ground in Guthrie County, into parts of Dallas County.  During the later evening hours, the thunderstorms began to sink south and took on a bow echo type configuration, though the line was not a true bow echo.  High winds began to be reported with Cass County being hit the worst.  A swath of damage 7 miles long was reported from west of Lewis to southeast of Lewis.  Embedded in the winds was a small tornado.  The tornado touched down intermittently along the 7 mile path and destroyed or damaged several grain bins and downed numerous trees.  A large corn crib was destroyed southeast of Atlantic and there were numerous reports of trees over highways.  In addition to the hail and high winds, heavy rain occurred from central into parts of west central Iowa.  Flash flooding occurred in Audubon, Guthrie, Madison, and Adair Counties.  Rain fell as the thunderstorms trained and redeveloped repeatedly over the same areas.  Rainfall rates were in the one to three inch per hour range with rainfall total reports of over 4 inches received in a few places in under two hours.  Water flooded over highways, even some U.S. Highways, to as depth of one to two feet.  This resulted in closure of some highways.

An intense low pressure moved across the northern tier of states during the day on the 27th.  Very strong south-southwest winds developed with profilers indicating wind of 50 to 65 kts within the lowest range gates.  Temperatures warmed into the mid 80s to low 90s with the atmosphere becoming adiabatic to between 750 and 600 mb, allowing for strong mixing down to the surface.  Winds increased during the day and reached high wind criteria by the early afternoon.  Most of the state saw sustained winds of 40 MPH or higher.  Several locations gusting to between 60 and 65 MPH.  The winds also produced blowing dust around the state.  Isolated reports of visibility reduced to near zero were received.  Spotty damage occurred around the state.  An 18 inch tree limb broke off and fell through the roof of a house in Windsor Heights.  A two to three foot diameter tree was snapped off at the base in Ames.  There were numerous reports of power outages caused by downed tree limbs.  In Union County, a 200 X 40 foot chicken house had the roof blown off.  There were several head of cattle in addition to the chickens contained therein trapped in the debris.

A weak trough of low pressure was over southern Iowa as a reinforcing cold front dropped southeast into the state.  The airmass was moderately unstable with dew point readings in the 60s.  Surface low tracked east across central Missouri during the mid part of the day.  Thunderstorms erupted across southern Iowa.  They formed into a line and became severe.  The main severe weather mode was hail.  There were numerous reports of three quarter inch to one and one quarter inch diameter hail over far southern Iowa.  Golf ball size hail pelted parts of Decatur County.  The combination of one inch diameter hail and 50 MPH winds caused considerable crop damage in Taylor County.  A swath 8 miles long and 3 miles wide was destroyed in an area extending from the hamlet of Siam, ease-southeast to the county border south of Bedford.  Heavy rainfall at the rage of of one to two inches per hour resulted in flash flooding in Ringgold County with water running over roads north of Mount Ayr.

A warm, moist, and unstable airmass was in place over Iowa.  The warm front that had produced the convection during the early morning hours of the 8th had become stationary over northern Iowa.  With lifted indices below -10 C. over central Iowa and continued strong moisture advection, thunderstorms erupted over the central and northeast parts of the state during the late afternoon and evening hours.  For the most part, the thunderstorms produced hail during this event.  There were numerous reports of hail of one to two inches in diameter.  Hail reportedly covered the ground in Guthrie County, into parts of Dallas County.  During the later evening hours, the thunderstorms began to sink south and took on a bow echo type configuration, though the line was not a true bow echo.  High winds began to be reported with Cass County being hit the worst.  A swath of damage 7 miles long was reported from west of Lewis to southeast of Lewis.  Embedded in the winds was a small tornado.  The tornado touched down intermittently along the 7 mile path and destroyed or damaged several grain bins and downed numerous trees.  A large corn crib was destroyed southeast of Atlantic and there were numerous reports of trees over highways.  In addition to the hail and high winds, heavy rain occurred from central into parts of west central Iowa.  Flash flooding occurred in Audubon, Guthrie, Madison, and Adair Counties.  Rain fell as the thunderstorms trained and redeveloped repeatedly over the same areas.  Rainfall rates were in the one to three inch per hour range with rainfall total reports of over 4 inches received in a few places in under two hours.  Water flooded over highways, even some U.S. Highways, to as depth of one to two feet.  This resulted in closure of some highways.

An intense low pressure moved across the northern tier of states during the day on the 27th.  Very strong south-southwest winds developed with profilers indicating wind of 50 to 65 kts within the lowest range gates.  Temperatures warmed into the mid 80s to low 90s with the atmosphere becoming adiabatic to between 750 and 600 mb, allowing for strong mixing down to the surface.  Winds increased during the day and reached high wind criteria by the early afternoon.  Most of the state saw sustained winds of 40 MPH or higher.  Several locations gusting to between 60 and 65 MPH.  The winds also produced blowing dust around the state.  Isolated reports of visibility reduced to near zero were received.  Spotty damage occurred around the state.  An 18 inch tree limb broke off and fell through the roof of a house in Windsor Heights.  A two to three foot diameter tree was snapped off at the base in Ames.  There were numerous reports of power outages caused by downed tree limbs.  In Union County, a 200 X 40 foot chicken house had the roof blown off.  There were several head of cattle in addition to the chickens contained therein trapped in the debris.

Very cold air aloft moved in over Iowa under a closed low pressure system aloft.  With strong heating and steep lapse rates, the atmosphere became very unstable.  A strong dust devil formed in Des Moines at a softball field and injured 5 small girls there.  One of the girls, each 5 to 6 years old, was actually picked p off the ground.  She was treated for a fractured ankle.  Later in the afternoon, convection became stronger.  Small showers developed and there reports of cold air funnels.  A landspout touched down in Hancock County north of Hayfield.  The landspout tracked southeast for about 3 miles before dissipating.  It tracked through open fields and did not cause any damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landspout tracked across open fields for about 3 miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of moisture streamed into Iowa on a 50 to 70 kt low level jet.  Lifted indices were around -9 C at the beginning of the event with CAPE in the 2500 to 4000 J/kg range.  Thunderstorms erupted to the west and southwest of Iowa with the initial development being discrete supercells to the southwest, and linear ahead of an approaching cold front to the west.  The storms became much more multi-cellular as they moved into Iowa.  As a result, competition for moisture caused the storms to weaken.  The storms that were severe in Iowa produced high winds in what appears to be several microburst events.  Winds of 74 MPH were recorded at the Ames Airport, with 73 MPH winds measured at a mesonet site in Adair.  A few other reports of severe winds were received with tree damage south of Adair and also in the Earlham area.  There were only a few reports of hail from the storms.  Hail that did occur was limited to an inch or less in diameter.
Several reports of large hail were received from amateur radio operators, spotters and law enforcement officials.  Hail sizes varied from pennies to golf balls to tennis balls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and unstable airmass was in place over Iowa.  The moisture was in limited supply with this event.  A cold front approached the state from the northwest.  This front moved into the state during the late afternoon and evening of the 16th.  Moisture was limited with this system.  Surface dew points were in the mid 50s to around 60 during the early part of the afternoon over the southeast half of the state.  The moisture was very shallow.  Afternoon mixing lowered dew points into the upper 40s over the southeast, and into the upper 20s and 30s over the northwest just ahead of the front.  Temperatures warmed into mid 80s to low 90s which made the airmass quite unstable.  CAPE values were around 1000 J/kg with Lifted Indices around -4 C. just ahead of the front.  Convection had a hard time getting organized at first with only spotty cells forming ahead of the front.  By early evening, a small MCS formed over western Iowa and advanced east-southeast across the state.  The MCS produced hail in many locations along with gusty winds.  In spite of the "inverted V" type upper air soundings, high winds were not as widespread as would normally be expected.  Several locations did report gusts to around 60 MPH.  High winds blew a car off of Interstate 80 in Poweshiek County southeast of Malcom.  A wind gust of 70 MPH was reported at the Ames Airport in Story County.  For the most part, the hail was in the three quarter to one inch diameter size.  Hail did cover the ground in Carroll County at Lidderdale.  The largest hail report came from Guthrie County north of Panora into the Panora area.  Golf ball size hail was reported at that location.  There were several reports of gutter damage and dents in car hoods due to the hail in Panora.  Overall, damage was limited with this event since tree leaves were only beginning to come out and the crops had not yet been planted.

Thunderstorms erupted in northwest and north central Iowa as a warm front lifted north into the state during the night.  A strong push of moisture was drawn north over the cooler air at the surface. The cold front that had moved southeast across the state the previous night became stationary across southern Iowa by the morning of the 17th.  A sharp contrast in dew point temperatures was found across the front.  Dew points were in the upper 50s to mid 60s south of the front, with dew points in the 30s north of it.  During the late afternoon the front began to return north as a warm front.  By evening a strengthening southerly jet in the low levels impinged on the front with wind speeds around 50 kts.  The overall low levels of the atmosphere were relatively dry and the airmass was capped initially.  The LCL height was around 750 mb.  There atmosphere was quite unstable with Lifted Indices in the -6 to -10 C range.  CAPE values were in the 2500 to 3500 J/kg range.  Thunderstorms erupted over northern Iowa shortly after sunset.  The storms formed into discrete supercells.  A tornado touched down east of Mason City from one of the stronger cells.  It was in open area and did not cause any damage during its brief touchdown.  The dominant severe weather mode for the event was hail.  Baseball size hail fell in Palo Alto County in Mallard.  There were numerous reports of hail three quarters of an inch to one and one quarter of an inch in diameter.  The largest hail was around two inches in diameter in Palo Alto County.  Hail reportedly covered the ground as the storms passed.  During the late stages of the event, a thunderstorms produced high winds as it swept across Interstate 35 west of Northwood in Worth County.  Two vehicles and one semi-tractortrailer truck were blown off of the interstate near mile post 214.  In addition, one telephone was blown down by the high wind.

Once again it was a very active severe weather day over Iowa.  The frontal system that had moved through the state on the night of the 22nd was returning north as a warm front.  This time Iowa did not get completely into the warm sector.  The surface front was dragged north into Iowa by low pressure that moved out of Kansas northeast into central Iowa by the mid to late evening.  The airmass was pushed southeast out of the state by a cold front that passed through the state during the evening and early morning hours.  The airmass in the warm sector became very unstable.  Lifted indices were around -13 C with CAPE values greater than 5000 J/kg by the late afternoon.  The air advected north into Iowa during the day and into the evening.  Thunderstorms erupted in two places.  The first to affect the DSM CWA was the development of several elevated supercells over west central into southwest Iowa.  The storms continued to lift east and northeast across the area.  A few tornadoes touched down, however with the very elevated nature of the storms it was very difficult for the tornadoes to reach the ground.  By the late afternoon, a solid squall line developed along the cold front.  The line took on a Line Echo Wave Pattern by evening.  Several segments of the line bowed out, resulting in high winds.  At times the winds reached 80 MPH.  One of the thunderstorms produced high winds in the Early area of Sac County.  They overturned a semi-tractortrailer truck, causing $80,000 damage and causing minor injuries to the driver.  Tornadoes that formed along this line actually formed where the Rear Flank Downdraft cut through the line.  A clear meso was formed and the small tornadoes advanced around the meso and were in front of the thunderstorm line.  Touchdowns were all brief.  Hail was common with the stronger storm, with much of it in the one to one and three-quarter inch in diameter.  Given the antecedent soil conditions in Iowa, flash flooding became a major issue.  Numerous counties reported flash flooding as rainfall of one to 4 inches in spots resulted in flooded highways.  The thunderstorms formed into an MCC by the early evening hours.  As they moved out of the state, they delivered one more punch to the state.  High winds developed in the wake of the complex.  Several counties of north central, northeast, and central Iowa received high winds.  Damage occurred in several locations to both trees and out buildings.  Sustained winds across the area were in the 25 to 45 MPH range with some higher gusts.  Clarion reported a wind gust of 63 MPH for the highest wind gust reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail covered the ground.
A tornado destroyed a shed, damaged a car, tore the back off a garage, and moved a house slightly from its foundation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: May 2004 began rather dry with an average of only 0.19 inches of rain over the first week of the month.  However, the second week of May brought seasonally normal rainfall.  Heavy rain occurred during the third week of the month.  May's greatest rain events came back to back.  The first round of heavy rain producing thunderstorms began on the morning of the 21st and continued into the morning of the 22ns.  Heaviest rains were in North Central and Northeast Iowa where storm totals included 4.75 inches at Emmetsburg, 6.22 inches at Mason City, and 5.14 inches at Decorah.  Another round of storms began on the afternoon of the 22nd and continued into the morning of the 23rd.  Some of the rain totals reported with this second series of storms included 6.67 inches near Ames and 4.15 inches at Marshalltown.  A statewide average of 2.97 inches of rain fell from these two systems.  Locally heavy rain continued to fall through much of the remainder of the month.  The greatest rainfall amount occurred during the early morning hours of the 30th when 4.37 inches of rain fell at Fairfield.  Damage reports were still coming in but it does appear from preliminary reports that roughly six percent of Iowa's 2004 crop was flood out.  Based on a $23 per acre cost to replant it is estimated that the floods have cost Iowa farmers some $15.2 million dollars.  The rainfall that occurred over the later part of May was Iowa's greatest since July 1993.  Rainfall totals for the month varied form 4.01 inches at Burlington to 14.87 inches at Lansing.  Radar derived estimates suggest that May rainfall was actually below normal in some areas over the far Northwest...such as Buena Vista and Van Buren Counties.  However, complete rain gage reports were not yet available for these areas.  Excessive rainfall produced major flooding in many areas across the state during the second half of May.  This was the first widespread major flood event in many years.  The heaviest rainfall occurred over the Northeastern sections of the state.  Additionally, several episodes of severe thunderstorms produced large hail, high winds, and tornadoes during May.  Severe weather was reported on 15 days during the month with all but 7 of Iowa's 99 counties reporting severe thunderstorm events.  The month's largest severe weather outbreaks occurred on the 8th (26 counties, mostly west central to northeast) ; 17th (20 counties, mostly southwest to east central); 21st (37 counties, mostly northwest to east central); 22nd (31 counties, mostly southwest to east central) and the 24th (33 counties, mostly southwest one half).  An exact tornado count is not yet available for the state, but central Iowa did report 32 tornadoes for the month with an estimated total of 50 tornadoes statewide.  Nearly all were F0 and F1 intensity with minimal damage to communities.  There was a tornado of F2 intensity that did produce significant damage to the communities of Bradgate and Palo (both on May 21).

However, complete rain gage reports were not yet available for these areas.  Excessive rainfall produced major flooding in many areas across the state during the second half of May.  This was the first widespread major flood event in many years.  The heaviest rainfall occurred over the Northeastern sections of the state.  Additionally, several episodes of severe thunderstorms produced large hail, high winds, and tornadoes during May.  Severe weather was reported on 15 days during the month with all but 7 of Iowa's 99 counties reporting severe thunderstorm events.  The month's largest severe weather outbreaks occurred on the 8th (26 counties, mostly west central to northeast) ; 17th (20 counties, mostly southwest to east central); 21st (37 counties, mostly northwest to east central); 22nd (31 counties, mostly southwest to east central) and the 24th (33 counties, mostly southwest one half).  An exact tornado count is not yet available for the state, but central Iowa did report 32 tornadoes for the month with an estimated total of 50 tornadoes statewide.  Nearly all were F0 and F1 intensity with minimal damage to communities.  There was a tornado of F2 intensity that did produce significant damage to the communities of Bradgate and Palo (both on May 21).

May 2004 began rather dry with an average of only 0.19 inches of rain over the first week of the month.  However, the second week of May brought seasonally normal rainfall.  Heavy rain occurred during the third week of the month.  May's greatest rain events came back to back.  The first round of heavy rain producing thunderstorms began on the morning of the 21st and continued into the morning of the 22ns.  Heaviest rains were in North Central and Northeast Iowa where storm totals included 4.75 inches at Emmetsburg, 6.22 inches at Mason City, and 5.14 inches at Decorah.  Another round of storms began on the afternoon of the 22nd and continued into the morning of the 23rd.  Some of the rain totals reported with this second series of storms included 6.67 inches near Ames and 4.15 inches at Marshalltown.  A statewide average of 2.97 inches of rain fell from these two systems.  Locally heavy rain continued to fall through much of the remainder of the month.  The greatest rainfall amount occurred during the early morning hours of the 30th when 4.37 inches of rain fell at Fairfield.  Damage reports were still coming in but it does appear from preliminary reports that roughly six percent of Iowa's 2004 crop was flood out.  Based on a $23 per acre cost to replant it is estimated that the floods have cost Iowa farmers some $15.2 million dollars. A late season winter storm developed in the Northern Rockies and moved southeast into the Ohio Valley.  Snow began in Eastern Iowa during the morning of 15 March and became heavy in the late afternoon and overnight hours.  The snow tapered to flurries just before dawn on 16 March and ended by mid-morning.  Mesoscale banding of snow produced a band of 10+ inches that was 1-2 counties wide.  Final snowfall amounts included Brighton, Iowa 16 inches; Sigourney, Iowa 14.3 inches; Columbus Jct and Wapello 12 and 11.5 inches respectively; North English and Washington 12 and 10.2 inches respectively; and Mediapolis, Iowa 10 inches.  Another very narrow mesoscale band of snow produced 6-7 inches of snow in just over 12 hours from Lowden, to Wheatland, to Park View, Iowa across parts of Cedar, Clinton, and Scott counties.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief high winds associated with the passage of a cold front damaged or destroyed several outbuildings near Paullina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, moist, and unstable airmass was in place over Iowa.  The warm front that had produced the convection during the early morning hours of the 8th had become stationary over northern Iowa.  With lifted indices below -10 C. over central Iowa and continued strong moisture advection, thunderstorms erupted over the central and northeast parts of the state during the late afternoon and evening hours.  For the most part, the thunderstorms produced hail during this event.  There were numerous reports of hail of one to two inches in diameter.  Hail reportedly covered the ground in Guthrie County, into parts of Dallas County.  During the later evening hours, the thunderstorms began to sink south and took on a bow echo type configuration, though the line was not a true bow echo.  High winds began to be reported with Cass County being hit the worst.  A swath of damage 7 miles long was reported from west of Lewis to southeast of Lewis.  Embedded in the winds was a small tornado.  The tornado touched down intermittently along the 7 mile path and destroyed or damaged several grain bins and downed numerous trees.  A large corn crib was destroyed southeast of Atlantic and there were numerous reports of trees over highways.  In addition to the hail and high winds, heavy rain occurred from central into parts of west central Iowa.  Flash flooding occurred in Audubon, Guthrie, Madison, and Adair Counties.  Rain fell as the thunderstorms trained and redeveloped repeatedly over the same areas.  Rainfall rates were in the one to three inch per hour range with rainfall total reports of over 4 inches received in a few places in under two hours.  Water flooded over highways, even some U.S. Highways, to as depth of one to two feet.  This resulted in closure of some highways.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down tree branches and power lines. One house was slightly damaged when the top of a tree was snapped off by the wind and fell onto the roof.
Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including a two foot diameter limb blown down. The amount of damage was not known.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure moved across the northern tier of states during the day on the 27th.  Very strong south-southwest winds developed with profilers indicating wind of 50 to 65 kts within the lowest range gates.  Temperatures warmed into the mid 80s to low 90s with the atmosphere becoming adiabatic to between 750 and 600 mb, allowing for strong mixing down to the surface.  Winds increased during the day and reached high wind criteria by the early afternoon.  Most of the state saw sustained winds of 40 MPH or higher.  Several locations gusting to between 60 and 65 MPH.  The winds also produced blowing dust around the state.  Isolated reports of visibility reduced to near zero were received.  Spotty damage occurred around the state.  An 18 inch tree limb broke off and fell through the roof of a house in Windsor Heights.  A two to three foot diameter tree was snapped off at the base in Ames.  There were numerous reports of power outages caused by downed tree limbs.  In Union County, a 200 X 40 foot chicken house had the roof blown off.  There were several head of cattle in addition to the chickens contained therein trapped in the debris.

Thunderstorms erupted in northwest and north central Iowa as a warm front lifted north into the state during the night.  A strong push of moisture was drawn north over the cooler air at the surface.  The thunderstorms were elevated in nature.  Being such, the mode of severe weather was hail.  Hail of  three quarters of an inch to one inch in diameter was reported in many locations across the northwest and north central parts of the state.  The storms continued through the night before the low level jet weakened around sunrise.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado caused no reported damage.
Thunderstorm winds destroyed a shed and damaged a garage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The cold front that had moved southeast across the state the previous night became stationary across southern Iowa by the morning of the 17th.  A sharp contrast in dew point temperatures was found across the front.  Dew points were in the upper 50s to mid 60s south of the front, with dew points in the 30s north of it.  During the late afternoon the front began to return north as a warm front.  By evening a strengthening southerly jet in the low levels impinged on the front with wind speeds around 50 kts.  The overall low levels of the atmosphere were relatively dry and the airmass was capped initially.  The LCL height was around 750 mb.  There atmosphere was quite unstable with Lifted Indices in the -6 to -10 C range.  CAPE values were in the 2500 to 3500 J/kg range.  Thunderstorms erupted over northern Iowa shortly after sunset.  The storms formed into discrete supercells.  A tornado touched down east of Mason City from one of the stronger cells.  It was in open area and did not cause any damage during its brief touchdown.  The dominant severe weather mode for the event was hail.  Baseball size hail fell in Palo Alto County in Mallard.  There were numerous reports of hail three quarters of an inch to one and one quarter of an inch in diameter.  The largest hail was around two inches in diameter in Palo Alto County.  Hail reportedly covered the ground as the storms passed.  During the late stages of the event, a thunderstorms produced high winds as it swept across Interstate 35 west of Northwood in Worth County.  Two vehicles and one semi-tractortrailer truck were blown off of the interstate near mile post 214.  In addition, one telephone was blown down by the high wind.

A very warm and unstable airmass was in place over Iowa.  The moisture was in limited supply with this event.  A cold front approached the state from the northwest.  This front moved into the state during the late afternoon and evening of the 16th.  Moisture was limited with this system.  Surface dew points were in the mid 50s to around 60 during the early part of the afternoon over the southeast half of the state.  The moisture was very shallow.  Afternoon mixing lowered dew points into the upper 40s over the southeast, and into the upper 20s and 30s over the northwest just ahead of the front.  Temperatures warmed into mid 80s to low 90s which made the airmass quite unstable.  CAPE values were around 1000 J/kg with Lifted Indices around -4 C. just ahead of the front.  Convection had a hard time getting organized at first with only spotty cells forming ahead of the front.  By early evening, a small MCS formed over western Iowa and advanced east-southeast across the state.  The MCS produced hail in many locations along with gusty winds.  In spite of the "inverted V" type upper air soundings, high winds were not as widespread as would normally be expected.  Several locations did report gusts to around 60 MPH.  High winds blew a car off of Interstate 80 in Poweshiek County southeast of Malcom.  A wind gust of 70 MPH was reported at the Ames Airport in Story County.  For the most part, the hail was in the three quarter to one inch diameter size.  Hail did cover the ground in Carroll County at Lidderdale.  The largest hail report came from Guthrie County north of Panora into the Panora area.  Golf ball size hail was reported at that location.  There were several reports of gutter damage and dents in car hoods due to the hail in Panora.  Overall, damage was limited with this event since tree leaves were only beginning to come out and the crops had not yet been planted.

Rainfall amounts of 3 to 6 inches caused widespread flash flooding.  Numerous road washouts were reported by law enforcement officials across northeast Iowa.  In addition, two bridges were washed out and 40 homes received flood damage in Fredericksburg (Chickasaw County).  Mudslides blocked Highway 76 near Effigy Mounds and the Great River Road near Waukon Junction (Allamakee County).  Due to extensive damage caused by flooding, all counties in northeast Iowa were declared disaster areas.
