The synoptic situation was very similar to what it was the previous day.  A complex weather situation was in place due to the convection from the previous night.  An outflow boundary extended east to west across northern Missouri.  The synoptic frontal boundary extended from central Nebraska across northern Iowa.  The air was clear over Iowa during the day, allowing for temperatures to recover.  Dew point values also recovered quite rapidly with readings in the upper 60s to low 70s by the late afternoon hours.  Lifted indices dropped into the -5 to -10 C. range by evening with CAPE values approaching 5000 J/kg.  By the evening hours, the frontal boundary and outflow boundary had converged and were located along Interstate 80.  Explosive thunderstorm development took place during the very late afternoon and evening hours in spite of the weak shear profile.  Discrete supercells formed in a narrow line along Interstate 80 from near Des Moines on west.  The storms became tornadic quickly.  To add insult to injury, a strong thunderstorm complex formed over Nebraska and moved east along the line of convection over Iowa.  As the event took shape, all modes of severe weather occurred.  The storms developed so rapidly that there was little more than a half hour from the first hail and wind report to the first tornado touchdown.  Hail and high wind reports were too numerous to mention individually here.  Numerous reports of golf ball size hail were received.  Boone and Story Counties reported some of the largest hail with the event as baseball size hail pelted the are.  There was one report of very large hail northwest of Slater in Story County.  Hail five and one half inches in diameter punched 8 holes through a steel roof.  There was severe house siding and roof damage in the area.  There were numerous tornadoes, especially in the central Iowa Iowa.  At one point there were several supercells around the metropolitan Des Moines area.  Two of the stronger tornadoes touched down west and south of Des Moines.  Fortunately, most of the tornado tracks were over open areas and resulted in little damage.  High winds became a problem, especially after sunset, as the complex from Nebraska moved in.  The system became more of a derecho during the late night hours.  Eighty plus MPH winds pelted southwest Iowa and downed barns and several buildings in Adams and Cass Counties.  A beta bow element formed in central Iowa shortly before midnight.  The bow echo produced a measured wind gust of 106 MPH in northern Warren County.  The high winds continued to push east along Interstate 80 for the next hour or so before it started to weaken.  The other major problem was with heavy rain.  The convection from the previous night set the stage by saturating soils.  The echos trained for several hours across central Iowa with very heavy rainfall.  Rainfall rates of 4 to 5 inches per hour were common under the heavier cells.  Several locations received 2 to 5 inch rainfalls in under  two hours, resulting in widespread flash flooding.  Reports of major highways being inundated by water were common.  By the end of the night, a swath of 4 to 9 inch rainfall was evident north of Interstate 80.  Longer term river flooding became a major problem by the morning of the 23rd.  There were two lightning related injuries.  Two people were injured when they were struck by lightning out of doors in Nevada.  The lightning struck close by as they were carrying groceries into their home.  The jolt caused them to fall, resulting in laceration to the head.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado touched down southwest of the town of Stuart and tracked northeast through mostly open countryside.  The tornado did lift occasionally.

To add insult to injury, a strong thunderstorm complex formed over Nebraska and moved east along the line of convection over Iowa.  As the event took shape, all modes of severe weather occurred.  The storms developed so rapidly that there was little more than a half hour from the first hail and wind report to the first tornado touchdown.  Hail and high wind reports were too numerous to mention individually here.  Numerous reports of golf ball size hail were received.  Boone and Story Counties reported some of the largest hail with the event as baseball size hail pelted the are.  There was one report of very large hail northwest of Slater in Story County.  Hail five and one half inches in diameter punched 8 holes through a steel roof.  There was severe house siding and roof damage in the area.  There were numerous tornadoes, especially in the central Iowa Iowa.  At one point there were several supercells around the metropolitan Des Moines area.  Two of the stronger tornadoes touched down west and south of Des Moines.  Fortunately, most of the tornado tracks were over open areas and resulted in little damage.  High winds became a problem, especially after sunset, as the complex from Nebraska moved in.  The system became more of a derecho during the late night hours.  Eighty plus MPH winds pelted southwest Iowa and downed barns and several buildings in Adams and Cass Counties.  A beta bow element formed in central Iowa shortly before midnight.  The bow echo produced a measured wind gust of 106 MPH in northern Warren County.  The high winds continued to push east along Interstate 80 for the next hour or so before it started to weaken.  The other major problem was with heavy rain.  The convection from the previous night set the stage by saturating soils.  The echos trained for several hours across central Iowa with very heavy rainfall.  Rainfall rates of 4 to 5 inches per hour were common under the heavier cells.  Several locations received 2 to 5 inch rainfalls in under  two hours, resulting in widespread flash flooding.  Reports of major highways being inundated by water were common.  By the end of the night, a swath of 4 to 9 inch rainfall was evident north of Interstate 80.  Longer term river flooding became a major problem by the morning of the 23rd.  There were two lightning related injuries.  Two people were injured when they were struck by lightning out of doors in Nevada.  The lightning struck close by as they were carrying groceries into their home.  The jolt caused them to fall, resulting in laceration to the head.

The bow echo produced a measured wind gust of 106 MPH in northern Warren County.  The high winds continued to push east along Interstate 80 for the next hour or so before it started to weaken.  The other major problem was with heavy rain.  The convection from the previous night set the stage by saturating soils.  The echos trained for several hours across central Iowa with very heavy rainfall.  Rainfall rates of 4 to 5 inches per hour were common under the heavier cells.  Several locations received 2 to 5 inch rainfalls in under  two hours, resulting in widespread flash flooding.  Reports of major highways being inundated by water were common.  By the end of the night, a swath of 4 to 9 inch rainfall was evident north of Interstate 80.  Longer term river flooding became a major problem by the morning of the 23rd.  There were two lightning related injuries.  Two people were injured when they were struck by lightning out of doors in Nevada.  The lightning struck close by as they were carrying groceries into their home.  The jolt caused them to fall, resulting in laceration to the head.

The synoptic situation was very similar to what it was the previous day.  A complex weather situation was in place due to the convection from the previous night.  An outflow boundary extended east to west across northern Missouri.  The synoptic frontal boundary extended from central Nebraska across northern Iowa.  The air was clear over Iowa during the day, allowing for temperatures to recover.  Dew point values also recovered quite rapidly with readings in the upper 60s to low 70s by the late afternoon hours.  Lifted indices dropped into the -5 to -10 C. range by evening with CAPE values approaching 5000 J/kg.  By the evening hours, the frontal boundary and outflow boundary had converged and were located along Interstate 80.  Explosive thunderstorm development took place during the very late afternoon and evening hours in spite of the weak shear profile.  Discrete supercells formed in a narrow line along Interstate 80 from near Des Moines on west.  The storms became tornadic quickly.  To add insult to injury, a strong thunderstorm complex formed over Nebraska and moved east along the line of convection over Iowa.  As the event took shape, all modes of severe weather occurred.  The storms developed so rapidly that there was little more than a half hour from the first hail and wind report to the first tornado touchdown.  Hail and high wind reports were too numerous to mention individually here.  Numerous reports of golf ball size hail were received.  Boone and Story Counties reported some of the largest hail with the event as baseball size hail pelted the are.  There was one report of very large hail northwest of Slater in Story County.  Hail five and one half inches in diameter punched 8 holes through a steel roof.  There was severe house siding and roof damage in the area.  There were numerous tornadoes, especially in the central Iowa Iowa.  At one point there were several supercells around the metropolitan Des Moines area.  Two of the stronger tornadoes touched down west and south of Des Moines.  Fortunately, most of the tornado tracks were over open areas and resulted in little damage. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts of 3 to 6 inches caused widespread flash flooding.  Numerous road washouts were reported by law enforcement officials across northeast Iowa.  In addition, two bridges were washed out and 40 homes received flood damage in Fredericksburg (Chickasaw County).  Mudslides blocked Highway 76 near Effigy Mounds and the Great River Road near Waukon Junction (Allamakee County).  Due to extensive damage caused by flooding, all counties in northeast Iowa were declared disaster areas.

A warm front lifted north across the state during the previous night.  At the same time, intense low pressure formed to the west of Iowa and tracked northeast through South Dakota into Minnesota.  The low intensified as a negatively tilted upper level trough of low pressure in the upper atmosphere lifted northeast out of the southwest U.S.  A strong jet and pronounced dry slot was evident in the warm sector of the system.  The jet and dry slot tracked across Iowa during the afternoon and evening of the 18th.  This set the stage for the high wind event.  A very strong south to southwest flow developed over the state.  Profiler data showed wind of 50 kts at 500 meters during much of the morning into the early part of the afternoon.  The winds increased to 75 kts at the 500 meter level by the late afternoon and evening hours.  The strong winds in the lowest range gates were not realized during much of the day do to strong upward vertical velocities.  Strong subsidence developed during the late afternoon and evening hours, allowing for sufficient mixing and for the strong winds to be realized at the surface.  During the day, winds were in the 30 to 35 MPH range across the state, with frequent gusts to around 55 MPH.  During the late afternoon and early evening hours, winds increased with sustained winds of 40 MPH or more across most of the state, with many areas reporting gusts of 60 to 65 MPH.  Widespread light damage was reported over the area, with scattered reports of significant wind damage.   Several reports of tree, power line, and out building damage were received from around the state.  Reports of semi-tractortrailers being blown off the roads.  U.S. Highway 18 in Cerro Gordo County was closed by two overturned trucks that were blown over by the wind.  Also, traffic was rerouted in Warren County at the junction of U.S. Highway 65-69 due to a truck being blown over.  In addition to the wind damage problems, dry conditions around the state lead to areas of blowing dust.  Visibilities were restricted at times, especially over the west half of the state.  In Kossuth County, the visibility dropped to as low as three eigths of a mile at the Algona Airport for about an hour.

Also, traffic was rerouted in Warren County at the junction of U.S. Highway 65-69 due to a truck being blown over.  In addition to the wind damage problems, dry conditions around the state lead to areas of blowing dust.  Visibilities were restricted at times, especially over the west half of the state.  In Kossuth County, the visibility dropped to as low as three eigths of a mile at the Algona Airport for about an hour.

A warm front lifted north across the state during the previous night.  At the same time, intense low pressure formed to the west of Iowa and tracked northeast through South Dakota into Minnesota.  The low intensified as a negatively tilted upper level trough of low pressure in the upper atmosphere lifted northeast out of the southwest U.S.  A strong jet and pronounced dry slot was evident in the warm sector of the system.  The jet and dry slot tracked across Iowa during the afternoon and evening of the 18th.  This set the stage for the high wind event.  A very strong south to southwest flow developed over the state.  Profiler data showed wind of 50 kts at 500 meters during much of the morning into the early part of the afternoon.  The winds increased to 75 kts at the 500 meter level by the late afternoon and evening hours.  The strong winds in the lowest range gates were not realized during much of the day do to strong upward vertical velocities.  Strong subsidence developed during the late afternoon and evening hours, allowing for sufficient mixing and for the strong winds to be realized at the surface.  During the day, winds were in the 30 to 35 MPH range across the state, with frequent gusts to around 55 MPH.  During the late afternoon and early evening hours, winds increased with sustained winds of 40 MPH or more across most of the state, with many areas reporting gusts of 60 to 65 MPH.  Widespread light damage was reported over the area, with scattered reports of significant wind damage.   Several reports of tree, power line, and out building damage were received from around the state.  Reports of semi-tractortrailers being blown off the roads.  U.S. Highway 18 in Cerro Gordo County was closed by two overturned trucks that were blown over by the wind.  Also, traffic was rerouted in Warren County at the junction of U.S. Highway 65-69 due to a truck being blown over.  In addition to the wind damage problems, dry conditions around the state lead to areas of blowing dust.  Visibilities were restricted at times, especially over the west half of the state.  In Kossuth County, the visibility dropped to as low as three eigths of a mile at the Algona Airport for about an hour.

Strong low pressure dropped southeast across northern Wisconsin into northern Lower Michigan during the day on the 7th.  As is typical of an "Alberta Clipper" type system, very strong winds swept down the southwest side of the low pressure circulation.  Although all of Iowa reported winds of 25 to 35 MPH with gusts to around 50 MPH, a narrow ribbon of high winds buffeted north central and northeast Iowa.  The winds were centered from near Fairmount, Minnesota through Mason city into Waterloo.  Winds were sustained just above 40 MPH in this band with wind gusts as high as 56 MPH.  The winds lasted a couple hours in any one location with the enter event taking several hours to cross the state.  Spotty damage was reported, but since spring foliage was not yet out the damage was limited.

An unstable airmass was in place over Iowa during the afternoon and evening of the 27th.  Temperatures rose into the mid 60s to low 70s across the state, along with dew point readings in the upper 50s to low 60s.  A strong upper level system moved in from the southwest U.S.  A closed circulation eventually developed as the system moved through Iowa and passed to the east.  A strong cold front advanced east across the state in the evening.  Thunderstorms developed ahead of the front.  For the most part, the storms were not severe and only produced very heavy rain and some small hail.  A few reports of microburst winds were received.  The first came from Poweshiek County, where 70 MPH winds tore the roof off of a building.  The strongest storms were in the Ringgold County area.  Winds of up to 70 MPH blew a car off of a highway 8.5 miles west of Mt. Ayr.  The same cluster produced a 62 MPH downburst wind gust in Dallas County at Perry.

A strong low pressure dropped southeast across Ontario, passing northeast of Lake Superior, during the day on the 20th.  At the same time a nearly 1050 mb polar high pressure area moved south into central and eastern Saskatchewan.  A strong northwest flow was set up between the two systems.  Along with strong subsidence and a southward push of polar air, strong winds developed over Iowa.  All of Iowa saw sustained winds of 25 to 35 MPH, with gusts to 50 to 55 MPH.  A few areas reached high wind criteria.  Sustained winds of 40 MPH or higher were reported over parts of north central into west central Iowa.  Winds gusted to 50 kts in Guthrie County at Panora, and in Carroll County at Glidden and Carroll.  Scattered damage was reported.  Fortunately, since the trees had not yet leafed out damage was limited.

Heavy snow spread into Iowa as a sustained southerly flow of moist air took place over the central U.S.  Low pressure formed over the southwest U.S. and tracked northeast across north Texas into northern Indiana.  A closed upper level low pressure was associated with this storm and helped produce significant lift over the area.  A broad band of snow developed during the early morning hours of the 5th, with snow spreading over much of the area by around sunrise.  Southwest Iowa had already seen 1 to 3 inches by sunrise however.  As the dry progressed, bands of heavy snow developed in the warm conveyer ahead of the upper low.  Snowfall peaked during the mid to late afternoon hours with snowfall rates of 2 inches or more per hour reported at some locations.  Visibility was reduced to under 100 meters in many areas for a one to two hour period due to heavy snow.  The snow hit in Des Moines just before the rush hour.  With a snowfall rate of 2 inches per hour for two hours, this caused significant travel problems.  Elsewhere around the state, travel was hampered though it was not stopped.  The Iowa DOT recommended no travel over about the southwest third of the state.  Many activities were called off on the evening of t he 5th, with numerous school closings or delays on the morning of the 6th.  Fortunately, wind was not a major factor with this system.  Wind speeds were in the 10 to 20 MPH range.  This caused drifting of snow, but very little blowing snow.  The heaviest snow amount received came from Osceola in Clarke County with 10.2 inches.  Several areas reported 8 to 9 inches in a band extending from Guthrie Center, to the southwest part of the Des Moines Metropolitan area, south to Lamoni.

A very warm and unstable airmass was in place over Iowa.  The moisture was in limited supply with this event.  A cold front approached the state from the northwest.  This front moved into the state during the late afternoon and evening of the 16th.  Moisture was limited with this system.  Surface dew points were in the mid 50s to around 60 during the early part of the afternoon over the southeast half of the state.  The moisture was very shallow.  Afternoon mixing lowered dew points into the upper 40s over the southeast, and into the upper 20s and 30s over the northwest just ahead of the front.  Temperatures warmed into mid 80s to low 90s which made the airmass quite unstable.  CAPE values were around 1000 J/kg with Lifted Indices around -4 C. just ahead of the front.  Convection had a hard time getting organized at first with only spotty cells forming ahead of the front.  By early evening, a small MCS formed over western Iowa and advanced east-southeast across the state.  The MCS produced hail in many locations along with gusty winds.  In spite of the "inverted V" type upper air soundings, high winds were not as widespread as would normally be expected.  Several locations did report gusts to around 60 MPH.  High winds blew a car off of Interstate 80 in Poweshiek County southeast of Malcom.  A wind gust of 70 MPH was reported at the Ames Airport in Story County.  For the most part, the hail was in the three quarter to one inch diameter size.  Hail did cover the ground in Carroll County at Lidderdale.  The largest hail report came from Guthrie County north of Panora into the Panora area.  Golf ball size hail was reported at that location.  There were several reports of gutter damage and dents in car hoods due to the hail in Panora.  Overall, damage was limited with this event since tree leaves were only beginning to come out and the crops had not yet been planted.

The cold front that had moved southeast across the state the previous night became stationary across southern Iowa by the morning of the 17th.  A sharp contrast in dew point temperatures was found across the front.  Dew points were in the upper 50s to mid 60s south of the front, with dew points in the 30s north of it.  During the late afternoon the front began to return north as a warm front.  By evening a strengthening southerly jet in the low levels impinged on the front with wind speeds around 50 kts.  The overall low levels of the atmosphere were relatively dry and the airmass was capped initially.  The LCL height was around 750 mb.  There atmosphere was quite unstable with Lifted Indices in the -6 to -10 C range.  CAPE values were in the 2500 to 3500 J/kg range.  Thunderstorms erupted over northern Iowa shortly after sunset.  The storms formed into discrete supercells.  A tornado touched down east of Mason City from one of the stronger cells.  It was in open area and did not cause any damage during its brief touchdown.  The dominant severe weather mode for the event was hail.  Baseball size hail fell in Palo Alto County in Mallard.  There were numerous reports of hail three quarters of an inch to one and one quarter of an inch in diameter.  The largest hail was around two inches in diameter in Palo Alto County.  Hail reportedly covered the ground as the storms passed.  During the late stages of the event, a thunderstorms produced high winds as it swept across Interstate 35 west of Northwood in Worth County.  Two vehicles and one semi-tractortrailer truck were blown off of the interstate near mile post 214.  In addition, one telephone was blown down by the high wind.

6 to 9 inches of snow fell over parts of northeast Iowa.  Volunteer weather observers reported 9 inches and 7 inches respectively at Edgewood and Garnavillo (Clayton County).  Other specific snowfall reports included 6.5 inches at New Hampton (Chickasaw County), 6.2 inches near Waterville (Allamakee County) and 6.0 inches at West Union (Fayette County).

An intense low pressure moved across the northern tier of states during the day on the 27th.  Very strong south-southwest winds developed with profilers indicating wind of 50 to 65 kts within the lowest range gates.  Temperatures warmed into the mid 80s to low 90s with the atmosphere becoming adiabatic to between 750 and 600 mb, allowing for strong mixing down to the surface.  Winds increased during the day and reached high wind criteria by the early afternoon.  Most of the state saw sustained winds of 40 MPH or higher.  Several locations gusting to between 60 and 65 MPH.  The winds also produced blowing dust around the state.  Isolated reports of visibility reduced to near zero were received.  Spotty damage occurred around the state.  An 18 inch tree limb broke off and fell through the roof of a house in Windsor Heights.  A two to three foot diameter tree was snapped off at the base in Ames.  There were numerous reports of power outages caused by downed tree limbs.  In Union County, a 200 X 40 foot chicken house had the roof blown off.  There were several head of cattle in addition to the chickens contained therein trapped in the debris.

An intense low pressure moved across the northern tier of states during the day on the 27th.  Very strong south-southwest winds developed with profilers indicating wind of 50 to 65 kts within the lowest range gates.  Temperatures warmed into the mid 80s to low 90s with the atmosphere becoming adiabatic to between 750 and 600 mb, allowing for strong mixing down to the surface.  Winds increased during the day and reached high wind criteria by the early afternoon.  Most of the state saw sustained winds of 40 MPH or higher.  Several locations gusting to between 60 and 65 MPH.  The winds also produced blowing dust around the state.  Isolated reports of visibility reduced to near zero were received.  Spotty damage occurred around the state.  An 18 inch tree limb broke off and fell through the roof of a house in Windsor Heights.  A two to three foot diameter tree was snapped off at the base in Ames.  There were numerous reports of power outages caused by downed tree limbs.  In Union County, a 200 X 40 foot chicken house had the roof blown off.  There were several head of cattle in addition to the chickens contained therein trapped in the debris.

Heavy snow spread into Iowa as a sustained southerly flow of moist air took place over the central U.S.  Low pressure formed over the southwest U.S. and tracked northeast across north Texas into northern Indiana.  A closed upper level low pressure was associated with this storm and helped produce significant lift over the area.  A broad band of snow developed during the early morning hours of the 5th, with snow spreading over much of the area by around sunrise.  Southwest Iowa had already seen 1 to 3 inches by sunrise however.  As the dry progressed, bands of heavy snow developed in the warm conveyer ahead of the upper low.  Snowfall peaked during the mid to late afternoon hours with snowfall rates of 2 inches or more per hour reported at some locations.  Visibility was reduced to under 100 meters in many areas for a one to two hour period due to heavy snow.  The snow hit in Des Moines just before the rush hour.  With a snowfall rate of 2 inches per hour for two hours, this caused significant travel problems.  Elsewhere around the state, travel was hampered though it was not stopped.  The Iowa DOT recommended no travel over about the southwest third of the state.  Many activities were called off on the evening of t he 5th, with numerous school closings or delays on the morning of the 6th.  Fortunately, wind was not a major factor with this system.  Wind speeds were in the 10 to 20 MPH range.  This caused drifting of snow, but very little blowing snow.  The heaviest snow amount received came from Osceola in Clarke County with 10.2 inches.  Several areas reported 8 to 9 inches in a band extending from Guthrie Center, to the southwest part of the Des Moines Metropolitan area, south to Lamoni.

Strong low pressure dropped southeast across northern Wisconsin into northern Lower Michigan during the day on the 7th.  As is typical of an "Alberta Clipper" type system, very strong winds swept down the southwest side of the low pressure circulation.  Although all of Iowa reported winds of 25 to 35 MPH with gusts to around 50 MPH, a narrow ribbon of high winds buffeted north central and northeast Iowa.  The winds were centered from near Fairmount, Minnesota through Mason city into Waterloo.  Winds were sustained just above 40 MPH in this band with wind gusts as high as 56 MPH.  The winds lasted a couple hours in any one location with the enter event taking several hours to cross the state.  Spotty damage was reported, but since spring foliage was not yet out the damage was limited.

6 to 9 inches of snow fell over parts of northeast Iowa.  Volunteer weather observers reported 9 inches and 7 inches respectively at Edgewood and Garnavillo (Clayton County).  Other specific snowfall reports included 6.5 inches at New Hampton (Chickasaw County), 6.2 inches near Waterville (Allamakee County) and 6.0 inches at West Union (Fayette County).

A strong low pressure dropped southeast across Ontario, passing northeast of Lake Superior, during the day on the 20th.  At the same time a nearly 1050 mb polar high pressure area moved south into central and eastern Saskatchewan.  A strong northwest flow was set up between the two systems.  Along with strong subsidence and a southward push of polar air, strong winds developed over Iowa.  All of Iowa saw sustained winds of 25 to 35 MPH, with gusts to 50 to 55 MPH.  A few areas reached high wind criteria.  Sustained winds of 40 MPH or higher were reported over parts of north central into west central Iowa.  Winds gusted to 50 kts in Guthrie County at Panora, and in Carroll County at Glidden and Carroll.  Scattered damage was reported.  Fortunately, since the trees had not yet leafed out damage was limited.

A late winter storm dropped heavy snow over parts of northeast Nebraska and western Iowa.  Due to relatively warm temperatures during the event, most of the snow was heavy and wet and the rain/snow line remained nearly stationary for much of that time. In addition...with temperatures a little above freezing on the southern end of the snow area, snow accumulations dropped off sharply.  For instance, a small area in far north Omaha picked up 5 to 6 inches, while the southern part of the city received only an inch or two, and then only on grassy surfaces.  Meanwhile 40 miles or so the southwest much of the snow melted as it fell leaving only a trace.  Most of the heavier snow, 6 inches or more, fell along and northeast of a line from around Center in Knox county to just east and northeast of Council Bluffs in Pottawattamie county in west central Iowa.Heavier amounts in eastern Nebraska included 13.5 inches in Crofton, 10 inches in Walthill and Oakland, 9 inches at Lyons, 8 inches at Wausa and Creighton, 7 inches at Fort Calhoun, Tekamah, West Point, and Blair.Heavier totals in western Iowa included around 13 inches at Castana and near Little Sioux, 11 inches in Mapleton, 9 inches at Harlan and 8 inches in Logan and Kennebec.

A very warm and unstable airmass was in place over Iowa.  The moisture was in limited supply with this event.  A cold front approached the state from the northwest.  This front moved into the state during the late afternoon and evening of the 16th.  Moisture was limited with this system.  Surface dew points were in the mid 50s to around 60 during the early part of the afternoon over the southeast half of the state.  The moisture was very shallow.  Afternoon mixing lowered dew points into the upper 40s over the southeast, and into the upper 20s and 30s over the northwest just ahead of the front.  Temperatures warmed into mid 80s to low 90s which made the airmass quite unstable.  CAPE values were around 1000 J/kg with Lifted Indices around -4 C. just ahead of the front.  Convection had a hard time getting organized at first with only spotty cells forming ahead of the front.  By early evening, a small MCS formed over western Iowa and advanced east-southeast across the state.  The MCS produced hail in many locations along with gusty winds.  In spite of the "inverted V" type upper air soundings, high winds were not as widespread as would normally be expected.  Several locations did report gusts to around 60 MPH.  High winds blew a car off of Interstate 80 in Poweshiek County southeast of Malcom.  A wind gust of 70 MPH was reported at the Ames Airport in Story County.  For the most part, the hail was in the three quarter to one inch diameter size.  Hail did cover the ground in Carroll County at Lidderdale.  The largest hail report came from Guthrie County north of Panora into the Panora area.  Golf ball size hail was reported at that location.  There were several reports of gutter damage and dents in car hoods due to the hail in Panora.  Overall, damage was limited with this event since tree leaves were only beginning to come out and the crops had not yet been planted.

The cold front that had moved southeast across the state the previous night became stationary across southern Iowa by the morning of the 17th.  A sharp contrast in dew point temperatures was found across the front.  Dew points were in the upper 50s to mid 60s south of the front, with dew points in the 30s north of it. Two vehicles and one semi-tractortrailer truck were blown off of the interstate near mile post 214.  In addition, one telephone was blown down by the high wind.

A widespread snow event took place during the 1st and 2nd of February.  An Arctic airmass was in place over Iowa during the last few days of January while low pressure formed over the southern Rockies.  The surface low did not become very well organized until the late afternoon of the 2nd, however there was a pronounced upper level trough traversing the central U.S.  A large area of isentropic lift produced a broad area of snow over the central U.S.  The northward transport of warm air was very evident between 800 and 850 mb, where soundings indicated a layer of above freezing air already by the evening of the 1st.  This event was a long and drawn out event with the first snow beginning before sunrise on the 1st.  It snowed for 18 to 24 hours in most places before the snow reached warning criteria.  The snow continued through much of the day on the 2nd, however accumulation rates were low.  The evidence of the warm air was apparent during the evening of the 1st as sleet and some freezing drizzle was reported over about the southeast half of Iowa.  There were even reports of thunder received from Marshall County.  The sleet and freezing drizzle did not become a major problem, but did continue off and on for several hours from the mid afternoon through the evening of the 1st.  Snow was falling quite heavily during the evening of the 1st until a strong dry punch moved over the state.  This stopped the snow over the southeast half of the state and help to produce a narrow deformation band of snow running from near Omaha to near Mason City.  Snow was persistent in this area with snowfall totals in the 8 to 11 inch range common.  Some of the heavier totals included nearly 13 inches at Britt in Hancock County, and 11 inches in Fort Dodge in Webster County.  There were several reports in the 9 to 10 inch range as well.

An Arctic airmass was in place over Iowa during the last few days of January while low pressure formed over the southern Rockies.  The surface low did not become very well organized until the late afternoon of the 2nd, however there was a pronounced upper level trough traversing the central U.S.  A large area of isentropic lift produced a broad area of snow over the central U.S.  The northward transport of warm air was very evident between 800 and 850 mb, where soundings indicated a layer of above freezing air already by the evening of the 1st.  This event was a long and drawn out event with the first snow beginning before sunrise on the 1st.  It snowed for 18 to 24 hours in most places before the snow reached warning criteria.  The snow continued through much of the day on the 2nd, however accumulation rates were low.  The evidence of the warm air was apparent during the evening of the 1st as sleet and some freezing drizzle was reported over about the southeast half of Iowa.  There were even reports of thunder received from Marshall County.  The sleet and freezing drizzle did not become a major problem, but did continue off and on for several hours from the mid afternoon through the evening of the 1st.  Snow was falling quite heavily during the evening of the 1st until a strong dry punch moved over the state.  This stopped the snow over the southeast half of the state and help to produce a narrow deformation band of snow running from near Omaha to near Mason City.  Snow was persistent in this area with snowfall totals in the 8 to 11 inch range common.  Some of the heavier totals included nearly 13 inches at Britt in Hancock County, and 11 inches in Fort Dodge in Webster County.  There were several reports in the 9 to 10 inch range as well.

Heavy snow spread into Iowa as a sustained southerly flow of moist air took place over the central U.S.  Low pressure formed over the southwest U.S. and tracked northeast across north Texas into northern Indiana.  A closed upper level low pressure was associated with this storm and helped produce significant lift over the area.  A broad band of snow developed during the early morning hours of the 5th, with snow spreading over much of the area by around sunrise.  Southwest Iowa had already seen 1 to 3 inches by sunrise however.  As the dry progressed, bands of heavy snow developed in the warm conveyer ahead of the upper low.  Snowfall peaked during the mid to late afternoon hours with snowfall rates of 2 inches or more per hour reported at some locations.  Visibility was reduced to under 100 meters in many areas for a one to two hour period due to heavy snow.  The snow hit in Des Moines just before the rush hour.  With a snowfall rate of 2 inches per hour for two hours, this caused significant travel problems.  Elsewhere around the state, travel was hampered though it was not stopped.  The Iowa DOT recommended no travel over about the southwest third of the state.  Many activities were called off on the evening of t he 5th, with numerous school closings or delays on the morning of the 6th.  Fortunately, wind was not a major factor with this system.  Wind speeds were in the 10 to 20 MPH range.  This caused drifting of snow, but very little blowing snow.  The heaviest snow amount received came from Osceola in Clarke County with 10.2 inches.  Several areas reported 8 to 9 inches in a band extending from Guthrie Center, to the southwest part of the Des Moines Metropolitan area, south to Lamoni.

Strong low pressure dropped southeast across northern Wisconsin into northern Lower Michigan during the day on the 7th.  As is typical of an "Alberta Clipper" type system, very strong winds swept down the southwest side of the low pressure circulation.  Although all of Iowa reported winds of 25 to 35 MPH with gusts to around 50 MPH, a narrow ribbon of high winds buffeted north central and northeast Iowa.  The winds were centered from near Fairmount, Minnesota through Mason city into Waterloo.  Winds were sustained just above 40 MPH in this band with wind gusts as high as 56 MPH.  The winds lasted a couple hours in any one location with the enter event taking several hours to cross the state.  Spotty damage was reported, but since spring foliage was not yet out the damage was limited.

A winter storm hit northeast Iowa producing snow accumulations of 6 to 9 inches.  Specific snowfall reports included 8.9 inches at Osage (Mitchell County), 7.5 inches at Charles City (Floyd County) and 7 inches at Fort Atkinson (Winneshiek County).

The first major winter storm of the season began during the evening hours of 3 January 2004 and ended by 2300 CST 4 January 2004.  A majority of the heavy snow fell across Eastern Iowa and north of U.S. 34, but mesoscale banding produced heavy snow in some Illinois counties along Interstate 80.  Total snow amounts for this storm were generally 6 to 8 inches with the resultant mesoscale banding producing amounts of 8 to 10 inches across the Iowa counties of Washington, Muscatine, Keokuk, Louisa, and Jones.  Mesoscale banding of the snow during the day on 4 January 2004 resulted in some areas receiving heavy snow while others did not.  A perfect example was in Delaware County Iowa.  A mesoscale heavy snow band deposited 7 inches of snow over the southeast corner of the county while the remainder of the county saw amounts in the 4 to 6 inch range.

A tornado touched down in southern Shelby county around 9 miles south southeast of Harlan.  The tornado tracked into Pottawattamie county on the west side of Walnut.  There it strengthened to a f1 category, damaging buildings and roofs in town.  One garage was destroyed and a radio tower was overturned. A grain bin was destroyed and carried for about a half of a mile. The tornado lifted around 4 miles south southeast of Walnut.  The total path length was around 8 miles and it was on the ground for about 11 minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 mph broke some large tree limbs northwest of Glenwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in southern Shelby county around 9 miles south southeast of Harlan.  The tornado tracked into Pottawattamie county on the west side of Walnut.  There it strengthened to a f1 category, damaging buildings and roofs in town.  One garage was destroyed and a radio tower was overturned. A grain bin was destroyed and carried for about a half of a mile. The tornado lifted around 4 miles south southeast of Walnut.  The total path length was around 8 miles and it was on the ground for about 11 minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts of 70 mph were measured by the AWOS in Harlan.  This was from a storm that apparently merged with the storm that developed just to the southwest and produced the tornado that tracked across the Walnut area.

Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 70 mph blew a truck over on Interstate 80 just north of Walnut. This was from the same storm that produced the tornado that tracked across this area around the same time, and it could have been from rear flank downdraft winds wrapping around the tornado.

Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph destroyed a barn 5 miles south of Walnut.  This was from the same storm that produced the tornado that lifted just to the northwest of this damage report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three to 6 inches of rain fell over much of southwest Iowa from Friday night through Monday night May 21st through the 24th.  This caused flooding along the West Nishnabotna River from around Hancock in Pottawattamie county downstream through the Randolph area in Fremont county.  Although just mainly agricultural lowlands were flooded, a few roads west of Riverton in Fremont county were closed due to flood waters.  The river at Hancock crested at 17.2 feet at 600 am Cdt on the 25th, flood stage at Hancock is 14 feet.  The river at Randolph crested at 20.9 feet at 545 pm Cdt on the 25th, flood stage at Randolph is 19 feet.  The river at Hancock was above flood stage from around 1015 pm Cdt on the 24th through 930 am Cdt on the 25th.  The river at Randolph was above flood stage from around 11 am Cdt on the 25th through 1145 pm Cdt on the 25th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 70 mph caused roof damage to a local Walmart and several other buildings in town, and also downed a few trees and power lines.

Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 70 mph destroyed a barn and machine shed in Essex.  The wind damage could have been caused by a brief touchdown of a weak tornado as Doppler Radar showed strong rotation in the area.
Hail up to 1 inch in diameter fell for a few minutes in the Clarinda area.
A tornado destroyed a farm house and several other buildings on a farm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet moving over a warm front from Northern Missouri into Central Illinois combined with an upper level disturbance to produce thunderstorms across Iowa and Northern Illinois.  As the storms became better organized, they were prolific producers of hail from 0.75 to 1 inch.  In Muscatine County Iowa, the storms organized into a small bow echo that moved through Rock Island, Henry, Bureau, and Putnam counties in Illinois.  Southern Henry County Illinois took the brunt of the bow echo with near continuous damage from Interstate 74 to Galva, Illinois with the worst damage in Galva.	A second round of thunderstorms developed around dawn from boundaries left over from the overnight storms.  Due to a drier atmosphere, the hail produced by this round of storms was much larger.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At Kirkwood College

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable situation took shape over Iowa during the day on the 11th.  A nearly stationary frontal boundary extended nearly east to west across the state along U.S. Highway 20, or roughly from Fort Dodge to Waterloo.  Very unstable air was to the south of the front with dew point temperatures in the low to mid 70s common.  By early afternoon, CAPE values were near 5000 J/KG with lifted indices in the -10 to -12 C. range.  A dry line advanced east toward the state along with the passage of a short wave lifting northeast across the area.  Thunderstorms fired along the triple point by the early afternoon hours in northwest Iowa.  The storms became severe very quickly and produced tornadoes as the sounding profile became very favorable.  In addition, significant mid level cooling took place as 700 mb temperatures fell several degrees during the day.   Most of the storms remained over the north third of the state with numerous reports of tornadoes.  The bulk of the tornadoes progressed southeast from northwest Iowa into the central counties of the state as the afternoon turned into evening.  For the most part, the tornadoes were quite small with only a few remaining on the ground for more than a few tenths of a mile.  Two tornadoes in Palo Alto and Emmet Counties were on the ground for a significant period of time, however they tracked over open country.  There were several tornadoes in Webster County.  Most of them were also small and did little damage, however one tornado near Otho hit two farmsteads there causing considerable damage.  One farmstead was nearly destroyed, including significant damage to the house.  Hail was common with the storms with much of it in the three quarter to one and one quarter inch diameter range.  There were a few golf ball size hail reports in Webster and Hamilton Counties, and one tennis ball size hail report in Stratford in Hamilton County.  Spotty reports of high were were received.  Winds were in the 60 to 70 MPH range.  Rainfall became a problem in a couple areas.  In Kossuth County rainfall of 2 to over 3 inches fell in under 2 hours causing flooding around the County.  Several roads became flooded, including Iowa Highway 9 and U.S. Highway 169.  Very heavy rain in the 2 to 4 inch range fell in Hamilton County during a 2 hour period.  This caused considerable flooding in the county, especially over the northwest half.  There was one high wind event.  In what turned out to be a high wind event occurred in Marion County.  A 60 MPH wind was recorded in Pella.  The event was in the wake of the line of thunderstorms and actually effected a several county area with winds of 40 to 50 MPH.  Temperatures shot up 9 degrees in a few minutes at the Des Moines Airport as a 51 MPH wind gust pushed the temperature up to 90 during the mid evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Boundaries left over from the pre-dawn and morning thunderstorms once again allowed thunderstorms to develop in the afternoon.  Due to a much drier atmosphere, the hail was much smaller than in the morning storms.
An upper level disturbance allowed thunderstorms to develop during the heat of the day, producing isolated severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shingles off roof on Universal Drive

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: See write up under the 23 May to 26 May time period.
See write up under the 23 May to 26 May time period.

A strong low level jet moving over a warm front from Northern Missouri into Central Illinois combined with an upper level disturbance to produce thunderstorms across Iowa and Northern Illinois.  As the storms became better organized, they were prolific producers of hail from 0.75 to 1 inch.  In Muscatine County Iowa, the storms organized into a small bow echo that moved through Rock Island, Henry, Bureau, and Putnam counties in Illinois.  Southern Henry County Illinois took the brunt of the bow echo with near continuous damage from Interstate 74 to Galva, Illinois with the worst damage in Galva.	A second round of thunderstorms developed around dawn from boundaries left over from the overnight storms.  Due to a drier atmosphere, the hail produced by this round of storms was much larger.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported by KCRG TV

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical airmass was in place over Iowa as a weak cold front moved southeast into the state.  At the same time, a tropical feature lifted north across Texas into Oklahoma and fed moisture into the state.  Dew points south of the front rose into the mid 70s by the late morning hours.  Precipitable water values were in the one and one half to two inch range with CAPE values across the state ranging from 1500 J/kg along the front to 3500 J/kg a ways ahead of the front.  Zero to 3 km CAPE values were between 100 and 175 J/kg.  During the mid morning hours thunderstorms fired up in northwest Iowa as the MCV from the previous nights MCS moved northeast into western Iowa.  The thunderstorms expanded through the morning into the afternoon hours.  Most of the storms were tropical in nature, but did produce pea to marble size hail at many locations.  There were a few reports of hail up to an inch in diameter.  As the event unfolded, a few of the storms produced downburst winds.  Wind gusts were limited to around 60 MPH.  Two barns were blown down in Webster County by the high winds.  The main event was the heavy tropical rainfall.  A band of heavy rainfall extended from west central into north central Iowa.  A band of two to four inch rainfall extended from near Sac City toward Fort Dodge into the Mason City area.  Locally heavier amounts of 4 to 5 inches were received.  Numerous roads were flooded in Webster County with a main bridge into Fort Dodge close due to high water.  Fifteen roads were flooded in Cerro Gordo County.  In Calhoun County, U.S. Highway 20 east of Rockwell City was under water with numerous county roads flooded.  Heavy rainfall in Des Moines caused flash flooding with water flowing over 2 feet deep over U.S. Highway 6 in town.  Nearly two inches of rain fell within a 30 minute period in the area to produce the flash flood.

Temperatures shot up 9 degrees in a few minutes at the Des Moines Airport as a 51 MPH wind gust pushed the temperature up to 90 during the mid evening hours.

A very unstable situation took shape over Iowa during the day on the 11th.  A nearly stationary frontal boundary extended nearly east to west across the state along U.S. Highway 20, or roughly from Fort Dodge to Waterloo.  Very unstable air was to the south of the front with dew point temperatures in the low to mid 70s common.  By early afternoon, CAPE values were near 5000 J/KG with lifted indices in the -10 to -12 C. range.  A dry line advanced east toward the state along with the passage of a short wave lifting northeast across the area.  Thunderstorms fired along the triple point by the early afternoon hours in northwest Iowa.  The storms became severe very quickly and produced tornadoes as the sounding profile became very favorable.  In addition, significant mid level cooling took place as 700 mb temperatures fell several degrees during the day.   Most of the storms remained over the north third of the state with numerous reports of tornadoes.  The bulk of the tornadoes progressed southeast from northwest Iowa into the central counties of the state as the afternoon turned into evening.  For the most part, the tornadoes were quite small with only a few remaining on the ground for more than a few tenths of a mile.  Two tornadoes in Palo Alto and Emmet Counties were on the ground for a significant period of time, however they tracked over open country.  There were several tornadoes in Webster County.  Most of them were also small and did little damage, however one tornado near Otho hit two farmsteads there causing considerable damage.  One farmstead was nearly destroyed, including significant damage to the house.  Hail was common with the storms with much of it in the three quarter to one and one quarter inch diameter range.  There were a few golf ball size hail reports in Webster and Hamilton Counties, and one tennis ball size hail report in Stratford in Hamilton County.  Spotty reports of high were were received.  Winds were in the 60 to 70 MPH range.  Rainfall became a problem in a couple areas.  In Kossuth County rainfall of 2 to over 3 inches fell in under 2 hours causing flooding around the County.  Several roads became flooded, including Iowa Highway 9 and U.S. Highway 169.  Very heavy rain in the 2 to 4 inch range fell in Hamilton County during a 2 hour period.  This caused considerable flooding in the county, especially over the northwest half.  There was one high wind event.  In what turned out to be a high wind event occurred in Marion County.  A 60 MPH wind was recorded in Pella.  The event was in the wake of the line of thunderstorms and actually effected a several county area with winds of 40 to 50 MPH. An old frontal boundary remained nearly stationary across Iowa, extending from central Kansas northeast into central Iowa.  A strong surge of moisture took place over Kansas with dew points in the low to mid 70s pushing over the frontal boundary.  Dew points rebounded across Iowa to near 70 by the afternoon.  The airmass was very unstable to the southwest of the state with CAPE values approaching 5000 J/kg and lifted indices near -15 C.  Thunderstorms erupted around mid day to the west of Iowa and advanced into the state during the afternoon.  The small cluster of storms took on a bow configuration and produced high winds over a large area as it lifted east-northeast across the state.  There was one injury recorded in Union County at Afton.  A large tree was blown over and hit a man.  He was injured, receiving a punctured long and head injuries.  In another wind incident, a semi-tractortrailer was blown over on Interstate 35 about 3 miles south of Clear Lake.

An upper level disturbance combined with freezing levels around 4400 ft AGL allowed thunderstorms to develop that were prolific hail producers due to their slow movement.  Numerous reports of 0.25 to 0.70 inch hail were reported that accumulated several inches deep, especially on roads that led to slick driving conditions and necessitated the calling out of snow plows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Accumulated 2 inches deep

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical airmass was in place over Iowa as a weak cold front moved southeast into the state.  At the same time, a tropical feature lifted north across Texas into Oklahoma and fed moisture into the state.  Dew points south of the front rose into the mid 70s by the late morning hours.  Precipitable water values were in the one and one half to two inch range with CAPE values across the state ranging from 1500 J/kg along the front to 3500 J/kg a ways ahead of the front.  Zero to 3 km CAPE values were between 100 and 175 J/kg.  During the mid morning hours thunderstorms fired up in northwest Iowa as the MCV from the previous nights MCS moved northeast into western Iowa.  The thunderstorms expanded through the morning into the afternoon hours.  Most of the storms were tropical in nature, but did produce pea to marble size hail at many locations.  There were a few reports of hail up to an inch in diameter.  As the event unfolded, a few of the storms produced downburst winds.  Wind gusts were limited to around 60 MPH.  Two barns were blown down in Webster County by the high winds.  The main event was the heavy tropical rainfall.  A band of heavy rainfall extended from west central into north central Iowa.  A band of two to four inch rainfall extended from near Sac City toward Fort Dodge into the Mason City area.  Locally heavier amounts of 4 to 5 inches were received.  Numerous roads were flooded in Webster County with a main bridge into Fort Dodge close due to high water.  Fifteen roads were flooded in Cerro Gordo County.  In Calhoun County, U.S. Highway 20 east of Rockwell City was under water with numerous county roads flooded.  Heavy rainfall in Des Moines caused flash flooding with water flowing over 2 feet deep over U.S. Highway 6 in town.  Nearly two inches of rain fell within a 30 minute period in the area to produce the flash flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime size hail

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet moving over a warm front from Northern Missouri into Central Illinois combined with an upper level disturbance to produce thunderstorms across Iowa and Northern Illinois.  As the storms became better organized, they were prolific producers of hail from 0.75 to 1 inch.  In Muscatine County Iowa, the storms organized into a small bow echo that moved through Rock Island, Henry, Bureau, and Putnam counties in Illinois.  Southern Henry County Illinois took the brunt of the bow echo with near continuous damage from Interstate 74 to Galva, Illinois with the worst damage in Galva.	A second round of thunderstorms developed around dawn from boundaries left over from the overnight storms.  Due to a drier atmosphere, the hail produced by this round of storms was much larger.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Also reported by Amateur radio.  Hail of 1.25 to 1.5 inches in diameter also occurred on the north side of the city of Marion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance combined with a strong cold front allowed severe thunderstorms to develop in the late afternoon and evening hours across Eastern Iowa.  Training of storms allowed very heavy rain amounts to occur in short time periods causing mainly nuisance flooding.  In Linn County Iowa, training of storms across the Cedar Rapids metropolitan area caused flash flooding closing many streets and sending Indian Creek out of its banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: 1 inch in the past hour.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph blew 6 to 8 inch diameter branches off trees near Little Sioux.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable airmass was over Iowa during the afternoon ahead of a very slow moving cold front.  The cold front was located northeast to southwest over the far northwest corner of the state.  Dew point temperatures rose into the low to mid 70s with actual temperatures in the mid to upper 80s ahead of the front.  The sheer environment was very weak with less than 20 kts of sheer in both the zero to 3 km and zero to 6 km layer.  A few thunderstorms developed along the cold front during the late afternoon hours.  The storms became better organized during the early evening and produced severe weather over northwest and north central Iowa.  Hail up to the size of nickels fell in those areas.  One of the storms produced high winds in Worth County, downing trees and damaging outbuildings and an LP tank east of Northwood.

One farmstead was nearly destroyed, including significant damage to the house.  Hail was common with the storms with much of it in the three quarter to one and one quarter inch diameter range.  There were a few golf ball size hail reports in Webster and Hamilton Counties, and one tennis ball size hail report in Stratford in Hamilton County.  Spotty reports of high were were received.  Winds were in the 60 to 70 MPH range.  Rainfall became a problem in a couple areas.  In Kossuth County rainfall of 2 to over 3 inches fell in under 2 hours causing flooding around the County.  Several roads became flooded, including Iowa Highway 9 and U.S. Highway 169.  Very heavy rain in the 2 to 4 inch range fell in Hamilton County during a 2 hour period.  This caused considerable flooding in the county, especially over the northwest half.  There was one high wind event.  In what turned out to be a high wind event occurred in Marion County.  A 60 MPH wind was recorded in Pella.  The event was in the wake of the line of thunderstorms and actually effected a several county area with winds of 40 to 50 MPH.  Temperatures shot up 9 degrees in a few minutes at the Des Moines Airport as a 51 MPH wind gust pushed the temperature up to 90 during the mid evening hours.

An old frontal boundary remained nearly stationary across Iowa, extending from central Kansas northeast into central Iowa.  A strong surge of moisture took place over Kansas with dew points in the low to mid 70s pushing over the frontal boundary.  Dew points rebounded across Iowa to near 70 by the afternoon.  The airmass was very unstable to the southwest of the state with CAPE values approaching 5000 J/kg and lifted indices near -15 C.  Thunderstorms erupted around mid day to the west of Iowa and advanced into the state during the afternoon.  The small cluster of storms took on a bow configuration and produced high winds over a large area as it lifted east-northeast across the state.  There was one injury recorded in Union County at Afton.  A large tree was blown over and hit a man.  He was injured, receiving a punctured long and head injuries.  In another wind incident, a semi-tractortrailer was blown over on Interstate 35 about 3 miles south of Clear Lake.

A tropical airmass was in place over Iowa as a weak cold front moved southeast into the state.  At the same time, a tropical feature lifted north across Texas into Oklahoma and fed moisture into the state.  Dew points south of the front rose into the mid 70s by the late morning hours.  Precipitable water values were in the one and one half to two inch range with CAPE values across the state ranging from 1500 J/kg along the front to 3500 J/kg a ways ahead of the front.  Zero to 3 km CAPE values were between 100 and 175 J/kg.  During the mid morning hours thunderstorms fired up in northwest Iowa as the MCV from the previous nights MCS moved northeast into western Iowa.  The thunderstorms expanded through the morning into the afternoon hours.  Most of the storms were tropical in nature, but did produce pea to marble size hail at many locations.  There were a few reports of hail up to an inch in diameter.  As the event unfolded, a few of the storms produced downburst winds.  Wind gusts were limited to around 60 MPH.  Two barns were blown down in Webster County by the high winds.  The main event was the heavy tropical rainfall.  A band of heavy rainfall extended from west central into north central Iowa.  A band of two to four inch rainfall extended from near Sac City toward Fort Dodge into the Mason City area.  Locally heavier amounts of 4 to 5 inches were received.  Numerous roads were flooded in Webster County with a main bridge into Fort Dodge close due to high water.  Fifteen roads were flooded in Cerro Gordo County.  In Calhoun County, U.S. Highway 20 east of Rockwell City was under water with numerous county roads flooded.  Heavy rainfall in Des Moines caused flash flooding with water flowing over 2 feet deep over U.S. Highway 6 in town.  Nearly two inches of rain fell within a 30 minute period in the area to produce the flash flood.

A strong low level jet moving over a warm front from Northern Missouri into Central Illinois combined with an upper level disturbance to produce thunderstorms across Iowa and Northern Illinois.  As the storms became better organized, they were prolific producers of hail from 0.75 to 1 inch.  In Muscatine County Iowa, the storms organized into a small bow echo that moved through Rock Island, Henry, Bureau, and Putnam counties in Illinois.  Southern Henry County Illinois took the brunt of the bow echo with near continuous damage from Interstate 74 to Galva, Illinois with the worst damage in Galva.	A second round of thunderstorms developed around dawn from boundaries left over from the overnight storms.  Due to a drier atmosphere, the hail produced by this round of storms was much larger.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: 10 inch diameter limbs down

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two major rainfall events during the last 10 days of May 2004 resulted in major to near record flooding across parts of East Central and Northeast Iowa that continued into June 2004.  Following is an edited write up from the Service Hydrologist:The first event occurred from the 21st through the 24th, with the hardest hit areas mainly along and north of Interstate 80.  Reported rainfall was as high as 4 inches (Cedar Rapids) during this period, while some locations received 5 to 6 inches.   The high amounts of water from smaller creeks and streams led to rapid rises on the area's larger rivers and streams, then major to near record flooding.  Most of the hardest hit counties, flood-wise, were along the U.S. Highway 20 corridor in Iowa.  In Manchester, Iowa, the Maquoketa River reached its second highest level ever, second only to a crest in the 1950s.  Flooding closed the major east-west road through the city.  That road is also a state highway.  The high water also forced evacuations of some businesses.  Some homes were affected, too.Independence, Iowa also encountered major flooding on the Wapsipinicon River.  Its crest was the sixth highest on record.  Many homes were affected, and the high water forced the evacuation of some businesses in town just like in Manchester.  In Independence, some normally small creeks became major waterways during and after the heavy rain, which flooded some houses and businesses that were some distance from those houses that were affected by high water on the Wapsipinicon River.  High water forced the closing of Iowa Highway 150 on the south side of town, where the road crosses the river.  With this major road shut down, access to the city was inconvenient from the south.  It required one to either travel several miles east on U.S. Highway 20, then back the same several miles to Independence, or to travel west a little and then take a road into the west side of Independence.Jones County, Iowa, also experienced problems from high water on the Wapsipinicon River.  The river in Anamosa crested at the second highest level on record.  The flood fight in Anamosa was successful in keeping most of the city safe from flooding.The second major rain event of the month occurred from the 29th through the 31st.  Reported rainfall was as much as two and a half inches of rain with other locations reporting as much as 3 to 4 inches.  This additional rain led to additional rises and crests on some rivers, with most of the crests occurring in June.  The second crests were in some cases the same as or higher than the first crests.  Lower sections of the Wapsipinicon River, for example, in Clinton and Scott counties, experienced high second crests.  Large amounts of water coming downstream from the first heavy rain event combined with runoff from the second rain event to produce the second highest crest on record on the Wapsipinicon River near De Witt, Iowa.  The high water forced the closing of Old U.S. Highway 61 south of De Witt, Iowa, along with the closing of a business along the river by the highway.  The present U.S. Highway 61-a vital link between northern Scott County, Iowa, and Clinton County, Iowa-was unaffected.The Rock, Pecatonica, and La Moine rivers in Illinois also experienced flooding due to the heavy rains.  Minor flooding occurred on the Rock River while moderate flooding occurred on the Pecatonica River.  The La Moine River experienced minor flooding.The large amounts of water from the tributary rivers in Iowa and Illinois led to rises on the Mississippi River.  Although the entire river rose in response to the large amounts of water, the river rose above flood stage from Lock and Dam15 in the Quad Cities to Gregory Landing, Missouri.  Most of the rest of the crests occurred in June.  The Mississippi River in the Quad Cities did crest during May, however, and it crested just over flood stage.  Only minor flooding resulted.

Following is an edited write up from the Service Hydrologist:The first event occurred from the 21st through the 24th, with the hardest hit areas mainly along and north of Interstate 80.  Reported rainfall was as high as 4 inches (Cedar Rapids) during this period, while some locations received 5 to 6 inches.   The high amounts of water from smaller creeks and streams led to rapid rises on the area's larger rivers and streams, then major to near record flooding.  Most of the hardest hit counties, flood-wise, were along the U.S. Highway 20 corridor in Iowa.  In Manchester, Iowa, the Maquoketa River reached its second highest level ever, second only to a crest in the 1950s.  Flooding closed the major east-west road through the city.  That road is also a state highway.  The high water also forced evacuations of some businesses.  Some homes were affected, too.Independence, Iowa also encountered major flooding on the Wapsipinicon River.  Its crest was the sixth highest on record.  Many homes were affected, and the high water forced the evacuation of some businesses in town just like in Manchester.  In Independence, some normally small creeks became major waterways during and after the heavy rain, which flooded some houses and businesses that were some distance from those houses that were affected by high water on the Wapsipinicon River.  High water forced the closing of Iowa Highway 150 on the south side of town, where the road crosses the river.  With this major road shut down, access to the city was inconvenient from the south.  It required one to either travel several miles east on U.S. Highway 20, then back the same several miles to Independence, or to travel west a little and then take a road into the west side of Independence.Jones County, Iowa, also experienced problems from high water on the Wapsipinicon River.  The river in Anamosa crested at the second highest level on record.  The flood fight in Anamosa was successful in keeping most of the city safe from flooding.The second major rain event of the month occurred from the 29th through the 31st.  Reported rainfall was as much as two and a half inches of rain with other locations reporting as much as 3 to 4 inches.  This additional rain led to additional rises and crests on some rivers, with most of the crests occurring in June.  The second crests were in some cases the same as or higher than the first crests.  Lower sections of the Wapsipinicon River, for example, in Clinton and Scott counties, experienced high second crests.  Large amounts of water coming downstream from the first heavy rain event combined with runoff from the second rain event to produce the second highest crest on record on the Wapsipinicon River near De Witt, Iowa.  The high water forced the closing of Old U.S. Highway 61 south of De Witt, Iowa, along with the closing of a business along the river by the highway.  The present U.S. Highway 61-a vital link between northern Scott County, Iowa, and Clinton County, Iowa-was unaffected.The Rock, Pecatonica, and La Moine rivers in Illinois also experienced flooding due to the heavy rains.  Minor flooding occurred on the Rock River while moderate flooding occurred on the Pecatonica River.  The La Moine River experienced minor flooding.The large amounts of water from the tributary rivers in Iowa and Illinois led to rises on the Mississippi River.  Although the entire river rose in response to the large amounts of water, the river rose above flood stage from Lock and Dam15 in the Quad Cities to Gregory Landing, Missouri.  Most of the rest of the crests occurred in June.  The Mississippi River in the Quad Cities did crest during May, however, and it crested just over flood stage.  Only minor flooding resulted.

A very unstable situation took shape over Iowa during the day on the 11th.  A nearly stationary frontal boundary extended nearly east to west across the state along U.S. Highway 20, or roughly from Fort Dodge to Waterloo.  Very unstable air was to the south of the front with dew point temperatures in the low to mid 70s common.  By early afternoon, CAPE values were near 5000 J/KG with lifted indices in the -10 to -12 C. range.  A dry line advanced east toward the state along with the passage of a short wave lifting northeast across the area.  Thunderstorms fired along the triple point by the early afternoon hours in northwest Iowa.  The storms became severe very quickly and produced tornadoes as the sounding profile became very favorable.  In addition, significant mid level cooling took place as 700 mb temperatures fell several degrees during the day.   Most of the storms remained over the north third of the state with numerous reports of tornadoes.  The bulk of the tornadoes progressed southeast from northwest Iowa into the central counties of the state as the afternoon turned into evening.  For the most part, the tornadoes were quite small with only a few remaining on the ground for more than a few tenths of a mile.  Two tornadoes in Palo Alto and Emmet Counties were on the ground for a significant period of time, however they tracked over open country.  There were several tornadoes in Webster County.  Most of them were also small and did little damage, however one tornado near Otho hit two farmsteads there causing considerable damage.  One farmstead was nearly destroyed, including significant damage to the house.  Hail was common with the storms with much of it in the three quarter to one and one quarter inch diameter range.  There were a few golf ball size hail reports in Webster and Hamilton Counties, and one tennis ball size hail report in Stratford in Hamilton County.  Spotty reports of high were were received.  Winds were in the 60 to 70 MPH range.  Rainfall became a problem in a couple areas.  In Kossuth County rainfall of 2 to over 3 inches fell in under 2 hours causing flooding around the County.  Several roads became flooded, including Iowa Highway 9 and U.S. Highway 169.  Very heavy rain in the 2 to 4 inch range fell in Hamilton County during a 2 hour period.  This caused considerable flooding in the county, especially over the northwest half.  There was one high wind event.  In what turned out to be a high wind event occurred in Marion County.  A 60 MPH wind was recorded in Pella.  The event was in the wake of the line of thunderstorms and actually effected a several county area with winds of 40 to 50 MPH.  Temperatures shot up 9 degrees in a few minutes at the Des Moines Airport as a 51 MPH wind gust pushed the temperature up to 90 during the mid evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An old frontal boundary remained nearly stationary across Iowa, extending from central Kansas northeast into central Iowa.  A strong surge of moisture took place over Kansas with dew points in the low to mid 70s pushing over the frontal boundary.  Dew points rebounded across Iowa to near 70 by the afternoon.  The airmass was very unstable to the southwest of the state with CAPE values approaching 5000 J/kg and lifted indices near -15 C.  Thunderstorms erupted around mid day to the west of Iowa and advanced into the state during the afternoon.  The small cluster of storms took on a bow configuration and produced high winds over a large area as it lifted east-northeast across the state.  There was one injury recorded in Union County at Afton.  A large tree was blown over and hit a man.  He was injured, receiving a punctured long and head injuries.  In another wind incident, a semi-tractortrailer was blown over on Interstate 35 about 3 miles south of Clear Lake.

A strong low level jet moving over a warm front from Northern Missouri into Central Illinois combined with an upper level disturbance to produce thunderstorms across Iowa and Northern Illinois.  As the storms became better organized, they were prolific producers of hail from 0.75 to 1 inch.  In Muscatine County Iowa, the storms organized into a small bow echo that moved through Rock Island, Henry, Bureau, and Putnam counties in Illinois.  Southern Henry County Illinois took the brunt of the bow echo with near continuous damage from Interstate 74 to Galva, Illinois with the worst damage in Galva.	A second round of thunderstorms developed around dawn from boundaries left over from the overnight storms.  Due to a drier atmosphere, the hail produced by this round of storms was much larger.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail of 1.00 to 1.50 inches in diameter with some golfball sized hail occurred with thunderstorm number 3 in Iowa City, and 2 miles west, 1 mile south, and 1 mile east of Iowa City.  Hail covered the ground in the southeast part of the city with numerous cars receiving dents.

This caused considerable flooding in the county, especially over the northwest half.  There was one high wind event.  In what turned out to be a high wind event occurred in Marion County.  A 60 MPH wind was recorded in Pella.  The event was in the wake of the line of thunderstorms and actually effected a several county area with winds of 40 to 50 MPH.  Temperatures shot up 9 degrees in a few minutes at the Des Moines Airport as a 51 MPH wind gust pushed the temperature up to 90 during the mid evening hours.

A very unstable situation took shape over Iowa during the day on the 11th.  A nearly stationary frontal boundary extended nearly east to west across the state along U.S. Highway 20, or roughly from Fort Dodge to Waterloo.  Very unstable air was to the south of the front with dew point temperatures in the low to mid 70s common.  By early afternoon, CAPE values were near 5000 J/KG with lifted indices in the -10 to -12 C. range.  A dry line advanced east toward the state along with the passage of a short wave lifting northeast across the area.  Thunderstorms fired along the triple point by the early afternoon hours in northwest Iowa.  The storms became severe very quickly and produced tornadoes as the sounding profile became very favorable.  In addition, significant mid level cooling took place as 700 mb temperatures fell several degrees during the day.   Most of the storms remained over the north third of the state with numerous reports of tornadoes.  The bulk of the tornadoes progressed southeast from northwest Iowa into the central counties of the state as the afternoon turned into evening.  For the most part, the tornadoes were quite small with only a few remaining on the ground for more than a few tenths of a mile.  Two tornadoes in Palo Alto and Emmet Counties were on the ground for a significant period of time, however they tracked over open country.  There were several tornadoes in Webster County.  Most of them were also small and did little damage, however one tornado near Otho hit two farmsteads there causing considerable damage.  One farmstead was nearly destroyed, including significant damage to the house.  Hail was common with the storms with much of it in the three quarter to one and one quarter inch diameter range.  There were a few golf ball size hail reports in Webster and Hamilton Counties, and one tennis ball size hail report in Stratford in Hamilton County.  Spotty reports of high were were received. An old frontal boundary remained nearly stationary across Iowa, extending from central Kansas northeast into central Iowa.  A strong surge of moisture took place over Kansas with dew points in the low to mid 70s pushing over the frontal boundary.  Dew points rebounded across Iowa to near 70 by the afternoon.  The airmass was very unstable to the southwest of the state with CAPE values approaching 5000 J/kg and lifted indices near -15 C.  Thunderstorms erupted around mid day to the west of Iowa and advanced into the state during the afternoon.  The small cluster of storms took on a bow configuration and produced high winds over a large area as it lifted east-northeast across the state.  There was one injury recorded in Union County at Afton.  A large tree was blown over and hit a man.  He was injured, receiving a punctured long and head injuries.  In another wind incident, a semi-tractortrailer was blown over on Interstate 35 about 3 miles south of Clear Lake.
