EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable airmass was over Iowa during the afternoon ahead of a very slow moving cold front.  The cold front was located northeast to southwest over the far northwest corner of the state.  Dew point temperatures rose into the low to mid 70s with actual temperatures in the mid to upper 80s ahead of the front.  The sheer environment was very weak with less than 20 kts of sheer in both the zero to 3 km and zero to 6 km layer.  A few thunderstorms developed along the cold front during the late afternoon hours.  The storms became better organized during the early evening and produced severe weather over northwest and north central Iowa.  Hail up to the size of nickels fell in those areas.  One of the storms produced high winds in Worth County, downing trees and damaging outbuildings and an LP tank east of Northwood.

A strong low level jet moving over a warm front from Northern Missouri into Central Illinois combined with an upper level disturbance to produce thunderstorms across Iowa and Northern Illinois.  As the storms became better organized, they were prolific producers of hail from 0.75 to 1 inch.  In Muscatine County Iowa, the storms organized into a small bow echo that moved through Rock Island, Henry, Bureau, and Putnam counties in Illinois.  Southern Henry County Illinois took the brunt of the bow echo with near continuous damage from Interstate 74 to Galva, Illinois with the worst damage in Galva.	A second round of thunderstorms developed around dawn from boundaries left over from the overnight storms.  Due to a drier atmosphere, the hail produced by this round of storms was much larger.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph created tree damage in Magnolia.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph blew down several large tree limbs in Logan.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph blew down several large tree limbs in Woodbine.
Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph blew down a tree 16 inches in diameter in Council Bluffs.  The winds also caused an unoccupied house that was being refurbished to collapse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical airmass was in place over Iowa as a weak cold front moved southeast into the state.  At the same time, a tropical feature lifted north across Texas into Oklahoma and fed moisture into the state.  Dew points south of the front rose into the mid 70s by the late morning hours.  Precipitable water values were in the one and one half to two inch range with CAPE values across the state ranging from 1500 J/kg along the front to 3500 J/kg a ways ahead of the front.  Zero to 3 km CAPE values were between 100 and 175 J/kg.  During the mid morning hours thunderstorms fired up in northwest Iowa as the MCV from the previous nights MCS moved northeast into western Iowa.  The thunderstorms expanded through the morning into the afternoon hours.  Most of the storms were tropical in nature, but did produce pea to marble size hail at many locations.  There were a few reports of hail up to an inch in diameter.  As the event unfolded, a few of the storms produced downburst winds.  Wind gusts were limited to around 60 MPH.  Two barns were blown down in Webster County by the high winds.  The main event was the heavy tropical rainfall.  A band of heavy rainfall extended from west central into north central Iowa.  A band of two to four inch rainfall extended from near Sac City toward Fort Dodge into the Mason City area.  Locally heavier amounts of 4 to 5 inches were received.  Numerous roads were flooded in Webster County with a main bridge into Fort Dodge close due to high water.  Fifteen roads were flooded in Cerro Gordo County.  In Calhoun County, U.S. Highway 20 east of Rockwell City was under water with numerous county roads flooded.  Heavy rainfall in Des Moines caused flash flooding with water flowing over 2 feet deep over U.S. Highway 6 in town.  Nearly two inches of rain fell within a 30 minute period in the area to produce the flash flood.

In another wind incident, a semi-tractortrailer was blown over on Interstate 35 about 3 miles south of Clear Lake.
An upper level disturbance allowed thunderstorms to develop during the heat of the day, producing isolated severe thunderstorms.

A tropical airmass was in place over Iowa as a weak cold front moved southeast into the state.  At the same time, a tropical feature lifted north across Texas into Oklahoma and fed moisture into the state.  Dew points south of the front rose into the mid 70s by the late morning hours.  Precipitable water values were in the one and one half to two inch range with CAPE values across the state ranging from 1500 J/kg along the front to 3500 J/kg a ways ahead of the front.  Zero to 3 km CAPE values were between 100 and 175 J/kg.  During the mid morning hours thunderstorms fired up in northwest Iowa as the MCV from the previous nights MCS moved northeast into western Iowa.  The thunderstorms expanded through the morning into the afternoon hours.  Most of the storms were tropical in nature, but did produce pea to marble size hail at many locations.  There were a few reports of hail up to an inch in diameter.  As the event unfolded, a few of the storms produced downburst winds.  Wind gusts were limited to around 60 MPH.  Two barns were blown down in Webster County by the high winds.  The main event was the heavy tropical rainfall.  A band of heavy rainfall extended from west central into north central Iowa.  A band of two to four inch rainfall extended from near Sac City toward Fort Dodge into the Mason City area.  Locally heavier amounts of 4 to 5 inches were received.  Numerous roads were flooded in Webster County with a main bridge into Fort Dodge close due to high water.  Fifteen roads were flooded in Cerro Gordo County.  In Calhoun County, U.S. Highway 20 east of Rockwell City was under water with numerous county roads flooded.  Heavy rainfall in Des Moines caused flash flooding with water flowing over 2 feet deep over U.S. Highway 6 in town.  Nearly two inches of rain fell within a 30 minute period in the area to produce the flash flood.
Large hail damaged vehicles and cracked windows. The amount of damage was not known.
Large hail damaged vehicles and cracked windows. The amount of damage was not known.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable airmass was over Iowa during the afternoon ahead of a very slow moving cold front.  The cold front was located northeast to southwest over the far northwest corner of the state.  Dew point temperatures rose into the low to mid 70s with actual temperatures in the mid to upper 80s ahead of the front.  The sheer environment was very weak with less than 20 kts of sheer in both the zero to 3 km and zero to 6 km layer.  A few thunderstorms developed along the cold front during the late afternoon hours.  The storms became better organized during the early evening and produced severe weather over northwest and north central Iowa.  Hail up to the size of nickels fell in those areas.  One of the storms produced high winds in Worth County, downing trees and damaging outbuildings and an LP tank east of Northwood.

An old frontal boundary remained nearly stationary across Iowa, extending from central Kansas northeast into central Iowa.  A strong surge of moisture took place over Kansas with dew points in the low to mid 70s pushing over the frontal boundary.  Dew points rebounded across Iowa to near 70 by the afternoon.  The airmass was very unstable to the southwest of the state with CAPE values approaching 5000 J/kg and lifted indices near -15 C.  Thunderstorms erupted around mid day to the west of Iowa and advanced into the state during the afternoon.  The small cluster of storms took on a bow configuration and produced high winds over a large area as it lifted east-northeast across the state.  There was one injury recorded in Union County at Afton.  A large tree was blown over and hit a man.  He was injured, receiving a punctured long and head injuries.  In another wind incident, a semi-tractortrailer was blown over on Interstate 35 about 3 miles south of Clear Lake.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated to near 60 mph were reported in Avoca.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved southeast slowly southeast across Iowa during the day on the 17th.  The surface front moved about half way through the state, while the front at 850 mb remained over the northwest part of the state.  The airmass was unstable, but not extremely unstable.  Lifted indices were around -2 to -5 C during the afternoon.  The convective inhibition had dropped to zero by mid afternoon with CAPE values around 1500 J/kg.  Thunderstorms erupted in the vicinity to just north of the surface cold front by late afternoon.  An MCS developed over southwest Iowa and tracked east across the state during the late afternoon and evening.  The main severe weather mode from this system was hail.  There were numerous reports of three quarter to one inch diameter hail during the period.  Some of the larger hail reached golf ball to baseball in size.  The largest hail fell in Madison County in the Winterset area as baseball size hail pummeled the area twice.  A few storms produced high winds.  One of the stronger storms was over Adams County.  In what appeared to be a macroburst, winds of around 60 MPH downed power poles and  a few outbuildings east of the Corning area.  The power poles were set on fire as they fell.  Later in the evening, over Monroe and Mahaska Counties, a thunderstorm turned into a bow echo segment and caused considerable damage near the Eddyville area.  One mile east of Eddyville on 340th St. along the Wapello-Mahaska county line was the hardest hit area.  Two farmstead received extensive damage around 9:15 p.m.  A microburst likely occurred on the farmstead located along the north side of the road (western-most farmstead), followed by a damage path spreading one-quarter mile east-southeast to a farmstead along the south side of the road.  At the southern farmstead, two farm outbuildings were destroyed.  Three poles rooted five feet into the ground were lifted complete out of the ground.  The roof of a shed was peeled back.   Pieces of the outbuildings from the northern farmstead spread out to the east and southeast up to 150 yards in width near the southern farmstead.  Length of the visible damage path was around ½ mile.  Persons in both farmsteads reported the duration of the strongest winds to be from 20 to 30 seconds.  Maximum winds were estimated to be around 85 mph.  When all was said and done, high winds flattened a barn and several outbuildings, silos, and trees.  Boards from the barn were driven through a car east of Eddyville along the southern Mahaska County border.  Several roads were blocked by fallen trees throughout the area.  Several hogs were killed when one of the barns collapsed near Eddyville.

A very unstable airmass was over Iowa and helped kick off the seasons first severe weather outbreak.  At the surface a warm frontal boundary extended nearly east to west across the state and provided the focus for thunderstorm development.  By the late afternoon, lifted indices were approaching -10 C. with CAPE values around 5000 J/kg over western Iowa.  Surface temperatures warmed into the upper 80s with dew points in the low to mid 70s.  A southwest surface wind of 15 to 25 kts pushed over the front.  Northeast of the frontal boundary winds were easterly around 10 to 15 kts.  Actually, the surface boundary was further enhanced by outflow from convective complexes during the day, further sharpening the contrast.  Thunderstorms continued to fire along and north of the boundary through the afternoon and into the evening.  This resulted in widespread flash flooding as very heavy rains fell on areas that had had significant rainfall the previous night.  There were reports in north central into northeast Iowa of 2 to 4 inches of rainfall in a little more than an hours time.  Major flooding took place in the Mason City area where evacuations were taking place.  The thunderstorms became most intense during the afternoon into the early evening.  There were several tornadoes across northeast into north central Iowa.  Most were relatively brief touchdowns and were in open areas.  Reports of multi-vortex tornadoes were received from Grundy County.  The days most significant tornado touched down in Pocahontas County east of Rolfe and tracked into Humboldt County through the Bradgate area.  The tornado damaged or destroyed over 75% of the town of Bradgate (pop 120).  Tornado damage in Bradgate was very extensive impacting most of the town.  Hardly a building was not impacted.   Outbuildings, light structures and garages were heavily damaged or destroyed.  Two homes were destroyed.  Several homes were damaged ranging from minor to extensive.  The Survey Team found that the majority of the damage was F1, with a few cases of F2 damage.  The F2 damage was to the snapping of very large trees just west of town and the structural damage to two buildings.  Nearly all of the 53 homes there reported damage with several totally destroyed.  Significant widespread property damage was reported in the tornadoes path.  There were several injuries in the town, the most significant being a broken leg.  A police officer was reportedly hit by debris from the tornado and suffered a broken leg.  According to law officials, the damage path of the tornado was 2 miles wide and about 8 miles long.  Storm chasers on the scene reported the tornado itself to be one half mile wide at one point.  Reports of hail were numerous through the afternoon and evening.  Most of the hail was in the three quarter inch to one inch diameter range.  There were a few reports of golf ball size hail from the stronger storms.  Winds were not a major problem with the activity.  There were spotty reports of winds to around 60 MPH, but most reports were in the 40 to 50 MPH range.  One of the stronger wind reports was a 64 MPH wind gust in Tripoli in Bremer County.  This gust was measured by a mesonet station there.  During the mid evening hours, thunderstorms rumbled across Grundy County.  Lightning struck the Sheriff's office there and destroyed a significant amount of office equipment.  Damage was reported around $100,000.

Storm chasers on the scene reported the tornado itself to be one half mile wide at one point.  Reports of hail were numerous through the afternoon and evening.  Most of the hail was in the three quarter inch to one inch diameter range.  There were a few reports of golf ball size hail from the stronger storms.  Winds were not a major problem with the activity.  There were spotty reports of winds to around 60 MPH, but most reports were in the 40 to 50 MPH range.  One of the stronger wind reports was a 64 MPH wind gust in Tripoli in Bremer County.  This gust was measured by a mesonet station there.  During the mid evening hours, thunderstorms rumbled across Grundy County.  Lightning struck the Sheriff's office there and destroyed a significant amount of office equipment.  Damage was reported around $100,000.

A very unstable airmass was over Iowa and helped kick off the seasons first severe weather outbreak.  At the surface a warm frontal boundary extended nearly east to west across the state and provided the focus for thunderstorm development.  By the late afternoon, lifted indices were approaching -10 C. with CAPE values around 5000 J/kg over western Iowa.  Surface temperatures warmed into the upper 80s with dew points in the low to mid 70s.  A southwest surface wind of 15 to 25 kts pushed over the front.  Northeast of the frontal boundary winds were easterly around 10 to 15 kts.  Actually, the surface boundary was further enhanced by outflow from convective complexes during the day, further sharpening the contrast.  Thunderstorms continued to fire along and north of the boundary through the afternoon and into the evening.  This resulted in widespread flash flooding as very heavy rains fell on areas that had had significant rainfall the previous night.  There were reports in north central into northeast Iowa of 2 to 4 inches of rainfall in a little more than an hours time.  Major flooding took place in the Mason City area where evacuations were taking place.  The thunderstorms became most intense during the afternoon into the early evening.  There were several tornadoes across northeast into north central Iowa.  Most were relatively brief touchdowns and were in open areas.  Reports of multi-vortex tornadoes were received from Grundy County.  The days most significant tornado touched down in Pocahontas County east of Rolfe and tracked into Humboldt County through the Bradgate area.  The tornado damaged or destroyed over 75% of the town of Bradgate (pop 120).  Tornado damage in Bradgate was very extensive impacting most of the town.  Hardly a building was not impacted.   Outbuildings, light structures and garages were heavily damaged or destroyed.  Two homes were destroyed.  Several homes were damaged ranging from minor to extensive.  The Survey Team found that the majority of the damage was F1, with a few cases of F2 damage.  The F2 damage was to the snapping of very large trees just west of town and the structural damage to two buildings.  Nearly all of the 53 homes there reported damage with several totally destroyed. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several brief touchdowns in open fields around the Holland area.  Track was very intermittent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: May 2004 began rather dry with an average of only 0.19 inches of rain over the first week of the month.  However, the second week of May brought seasonally normal rainfall.  Heavy rain occurred during the third week of the month.  May's greatest rain events came back to back.  The first round of heavy rain producing thunderstorms began on the morning of the 21st and continued into the morning of the 22ns.  Heaviest rains were in North Central and Northeast Iowa where storm totals included 4.75 inches at Emmetsburg, 6.22 inches at Mason City, and 5.14 inches at Decorah.  Another round of storms began on the afternoon of the 22nd and continued into the morning of the 23rd.  Some of the rain totals reported with this second series of storms included 6.67 inches near Ames and 4.15 inches at Marshalltown.  A statewide average of 2.97 inches of rain fell from these two systems.  Locally heavy rain continued to fall through much of the remainder of the month.  The greatest rainfall amount occurred during the early morning hours of the 30th when 4.37 inches of rain fell at Fairfield.  Damage reports were still coming in but it does appear from preliminary reports that roughly six percent of Iowa's 2004 crop was flood out.  Based on a $23 per acre cost to replant it is estimated that the floods have cost Iowa farmers some $15.2 million dollars.  The rainfall that occurred over the later part of May was Iowa's greatest since July 1993.  Rainfall totals for the month varied form 4.01 inches at Burlington to 14.87 inches at Lansing.  Radar derived estimates suggest that May rainfall was actually below normal in some areas over the far Northwest...such as Buena Vista and Van Buren Counties.  However, complete rain gage reports were not yet available for these areas.  Excessive rainfall produced major flooding in many areas across the state during the second half of May.  This was the first widespread major flood event in many years.  The heaviest rainfall occurred over the Northeastern sections of the state.  Additionally, several episodes of severe thunderstorms produced large hail, high winds, and tornadoes during May.  Severe weather was reported on 15 days during the month with all but 7 of Iowa's 99 counties reporting severe thunderstorm events.  The month's largest severe weather outbreaks occurred on the 8th (26 counties, mostly west central to northeast) ; 17th (20 counties, mostly southwest to east central); 21st (37 counties, mostly northwest to east central); 22nd (31 counties, mostly southwest to east central) and the 24th (33 counties, mostly southwest one half).  An exact tornado count is not yet available for the state, but central Iowa did report 32 tornadoes for the month with an estimated total of 50 tornadoes statewide.  Nearly all were F0 and F1 intensity with minimal damage to communities.  There was a tornado of F2 intensity that did produce significant damage to the communities of Bradgate and Palo (both on May 21).

A weak trough of low pressure was over southern Iowa as a reinforcing cold front dropped southeast into the state.  The airmass was moderately unstable with dew point readings in the 60s.  Surface low tracked east across central Missouri during the mid part of the day.  Thunderstorms erupted across southern Iowa.  They formed into a line and became severe.  The main severe weather mode was hail.  There were numerous reports of three quarter inch to one and one quarter inch diameter hail over far southern Iowa.  Golf ball size hail pelted parts of Decatur County.  The combination of one inch diameter hail and 50 MPH winds caused considerable crop damage in Taylor County.  A swath 8 miles long and 3 miles wide was destroyed in an area extending from the hamlet of Siam, ease-southeast to the county border south of Bedford.  Heavy rainfall at the rage of of one to two inches per hour resulted in flash flooding in Ringgold County with water running over roads north of Mount Ayr.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado tracked south of Harlan near F-58 and Juniper Road according to emergency management. The tornado was also seen from near the Walnut area in extreme northern Pottawattamie county.  The tornado apparently remained in open country and caused little or no damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three to 6 inches of rain fell over much of southwest Iowa from Friday night through Monday night May 21st through the 24th.  This caused flooding along the West Nishnabotna River from around Hancock in Pottawattamie county downstream through the Randolph area in Fremont county.  Although just mainly agricultural lowlands were flooded, a few roads west of Riverton in Fremont county were closed due to flood waters.  The river at Hancock crested at 17.2 feet at 600 am Cdt on the 25th, flood stage at Hancock is 14 feet.  The river at Randolph crested at 20.9 feet at 545 pm Cdt on the 25th, flood stage at Randolph is 19 feet.  The river at Hancock was above flood stage from around 1015 pm Cdt on the 24th through 930 am Cdt on the 25th.  The river at Randolph was above flood stage from around 11 am Cdt on the 25th through 1145 pm Cdt on the 25th.

A warm, moist, and unstable airmass was in place over Iowa.  The warm front that had produced the convection during the early morning hours of the 8th had become stationary over northern Iowa.  With lifted indices below -10 C. over central Iowa and continued strong moisture advection, thunderstorms erupted over the central and northeast parts of the state during the late afternoon and evening hours.  For the most part, the thunderstorms produced hail during this event.  There were numerous reports of hail of one to two inches in diameter.  Hail reportedly covered the ground in Guthrie County, into parts of Dallas County.  During the later evening hours, the thunderstorms began to sink south and took on a bow echo type configuration, though the line was not a true bow echo.  High winds began to be reported with Cass County being hit the worst.  A swath of damage 7 miles long was reported from west of Lewis to southeast of Lewis.  Embedded in the winds was a small tornado.  The tornado touched down intermittently along the 7 mile path and destroyed or damaged several grain bins and downed numerous trees.  A large corn crib was destroyed southeast of Atlantic and there were numerous reports of trees over highways.  In addition to the hail and high winds, heavy rain occurred from central into parts of west central Iowa.  Flash flooding occurred in Audubon, Guthrie, Madison, and Adair Counties.  Rain fell as the thunderstorms trained and redeveloped repeatedly over the same areas.  Rainfall rates were in the one to three inch per hour range with rainfall total reports of over 4 inches received in a few places in under two hours.  Water flooded over highways, even some U.S. Highways, to as depth of one to two feet.  This resulted in closure of some highways.

Water flooded over highways, even some U.S. Highways, to as depth of one to two feet.  This resulted in closure of some highways.

Thunderstorms developed during the evening hours along a cold front moving through Iowa and became severe as a mesoscale low developed in East Central Iowa. A wake low developed just behind the storms and provided sustained winds over 26 knots (30 mph) that lasted for 20 to 30 minutes.  The storms and wake low moved east along U.S. 20 through Northeast Iowa and Northwest Illinois.  The storms were quite prolific in producing pea to just under .75 inch hail.  Significant damage occurred on the south side of Dubuque, Iowa with numerous trees and power lines down and a radio tower mangled.  In Jo Daviess County Illinois, a continuous area of damage occurred from the flats near East Dubuque, Illinois through Menominee and Vinegar Hill townships.  Numerous trees were either uprooted or decapitated with many buildings (farm and garage) damaged or destroyed.

The synoptic situation was very similar to what it was the previous day.  A complex weather situation was in place due to the convection from the previous night.  An outflow boundary extended east to west across northern Missouri.  The synoptic frontal boundary extended from central Nebraska across northern Iowa.  The air was clear over Iowa during the day, allowing for temperatures to recover.  Dew point values also recovered quite rapidly with readings in the upper 60s to low 70s by the late afternoon hours.  Lifted indices dropped into the -5 to -10 C. range by evening with CAPE values approaching 5000 J/kg.  By the evening hours, the frontal boundary and outflow boundary had converged and were located along Interstate 80.  Explosive thunderstorm development took place during the very late afternoon and evening hours in spite of the weak shear profile.  Discrete supercells formed in a narrow line along Interstate 80 from near Des Moines on west.  The storms became tornadic quickly.  To add insult to injury, a strong thunderstorm complex formed over Nebraska and moved east along the line of convection over Iowa.  As the event took shape, all modes of severe weather occurred.  The storms developed so rapidly that there was little more than a half hour from the first hail and wind report to the first tornado touchdown.  Hail and high wind reports were too numerous to mention individually here.  Numerous reports of golf ball size hail were received.  Boone and Story Counties reported some of the largest hail with the event as baseball size hail pelted the are.  There was one report of very large hail northwest of Slater in Story County.  Hail five and one half inches in diameter punched 8 holes through a steel roof.  There was severe house siding and roof damage in the area.  There were numerous tornadoes, especially in the central Iowa Iowa.  At one point there were several supercells around the metropolitan Des Moines area.  Two of the stronger tornadoes touched down west and south of Des Moines.  Fortunately, most of the tornado tracks were over open areas and resulted in little damage.  High winds became a problem, especially after sunset, as the complex from Nebraska moved in.  The system became more of a derecho during the late night hours.  Eighty plus MPH winds pelted southwest Iowa and downed barns and several buildings in Adams and Cass Counties.  A beta bow element formed in central Iowa shortly before midnight.  The bow echo produced a measured wind gust of 106 MPH in northern Warren County.  The high winds continued to push east along Interstate 80 for the next hour or so before it started to weaken.  The other major problem was with heavy rain.  The convection from the previous night set the stage by saturating soils.  The echos trained for several hours across central Iowa with very heavy rainfall.  Rainfall rates of 4 to 5 inches per hour were common under the heavier cells.  Several locations received 2 to 5 inch rainfalls in under  two hours, resulting in widespread flash flooding.  Reports of major highways being inundated by water were common.  By the end of the night, a swath of 4 to 9 inch rainfall was evident north of Interstate 80.  Longer term river flooding became a major problem by the morning of the 23rd.  There were two lightning related injuries.  Two people were injured when they were struck by lightning out of doors in Nevada.  The lightning struck close by as they were carrying groceries into their home.  The jolt caused them to fall, resulting in laceration to the head.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Touchdown in open fields south of Garwin.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the second time in a week the Nishnabotna River in Fremont county flooded due to heavy rains. The river at Hamburg crested at 28.8 feet at 1145 am Cdt May 30th, flood stage is 18 feet.  The river was above flood stage from 715 am Cdt on the 30th through 415 am Cdt on the 31st.  The flooding affected agricultural lowlands as a dike along the river protected most structures and roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three to 6 inches of rain fell over much of southwest Iowa from Friday night through Monday night May 21st through the 24th.  This caused flooding along the West Nishnabotna River from around Hancock in Pottawattamie county downstream through the Randolph area in Fremont county.  Although just mainly agricultural lowlands were flooded, a few roads west of Riverton in Fremont county were closed due to flood waters.  The river at Hancock crested at 17.2 feet at 600 am Cdt on the 25th, flood stage at Hancock is 14 feet.  The river at Randolph crested at 20.9 feet at 545 pm Cdt on the 25th, flood stage at Randolph is 19 feet.  The river at Hancock was above flood stage from around 1015 pm Cdt on the 24th through 930 am Cdt on the 25th.  The river at Randolph was above flood stage from around 11 am Cdt on the 25th through 1145 pm Cdt on the 25th.

Boone and Story Counties reported some of the largest hail with the event as baseball size hail pelted the are.  There was one report of very large hail northwest of Slater in Story County.  Hail five and one half inches in diameter punched 8 holes through a steel roof.  There was severe house siding and roof damage in the area.  There were numerous tornadoes, especially in the central Iowa Iowa.  At one point there were several supercells around the metropolitan Des Moines area.  Two of the stronger tornadoes touched down west and south of Des Moines.  Fortunately, most of the tornado tracks were over open areas and resulted in little damage.  High winds became a problem, especially after sunset, as the complex from Nebraska moved in.  The system became more of a derecho during the late night hours.  Eighty plus MPH winds pelted southwest Iowa and downed barns and several buildings in Adams and Cass Counties.  A beta bow element formed in central Iowa shortly before midnight.  The bow echo produced a measured wind gust of 106 MPH in northern Warren County.  The high winds continued to push east along Interstate 80 for the next hour or so before it started to weaken.  The other major problem was with heavy rain.  The convection from the previous night set the stage by saturating soils.  The echos trained for several hours across central Iowa with very heavy rainfall.  Rainfall rates of 4 to 5 inches per hour were common under the heavier cells.  Several locations received 2 to 5 inch rainfalls in under  two hours, resulting in widespread flash flooding.  Reports of major highways being inundated by water were common.  By the end of the night, a swath of 4 to 9 inch rainfall was evident north of Interstate 80.  Longer term river flooding became a major problem by the morning of the 23rd.  There were two lightning related injuries.  Two people were injured when they were struck by lightning out of doors in Nevada.  The lightning struck close by as they were carrying groceries into their home.  The jolt caused them to fall, resulting in laceration to the head.

The synoptic situation was very similar to what it was the previous day.  A complex weather situation was in place due to the convection from the previous night. The air was clear over Iowa during the day, allowing for temperatures to recover.  Dew point values also recovered quite rapidly with readings in the upper 60s to low 70s by the late afternoon hours.  Lifted indices dropped into the -5 to -10 C. range by evening with CAPE values approaching 5000 J/kg.  By the evening hours, the frontal boundary and outflow boundary had converged and were located along Interstate 80.  Explosive thunderstorm development took place during the very late afternoon and evening hours in spite of the weak shear profile.  Discrete supercells formed in a narrow line along Interstate 80 from near Des Moines on west.  The storms became tornadic quickly.  To add insult to injury, a strong thunderstorm complex formed over Nebraska and moved east along the line of convection over Iowa.  As the event took shape, all modes of severe weather occurred. Once again it was a very active severe weather day over Iowa.  The frontal system that had moved through the state on the night of the 22nd was returning north as a warm front.  This time Iowa did not get completely into the warm sector.  The surface front was dragged north into Iowa by low pressure that moved out of Kansas northeast into central Iowa by the mid to late evening.  The airmass was pushed southeast out of the state by a cold front that passed through the state during the evening and early morning hours.  The airmass in the warm sector became very unstable.  Lifted indices were around -13 C with CAPE values greater than 5000 J/kg by the late afternoon.  The air advected north into Iowa during the day and into the evening.  Thunderstorms erupted in two places.  The first to affect the DSM CWA was the development of several elevated supercells over west central into southwest Iowa.  The storms continued to lift east and northeast across the area.  A few tornadoes touched down, however with the very elevated nature of the storms it was very difficult for the tornadoes to reach the ground.  By the late afternoon, a solid squall line developed along the cold front.  The line took on a Line Echo Wave Pattern by evening.  Several segments of the line bowed out, resulting in high winds.  At times the winds reached 80 MPH.  One of the thunderstorms produced high winds in the Early area of Sac County.  They overturned a semi-tractortrailer truck, causing $80,000 damage and causing minor injuries to the driver.  Tornadoes that formed along this line actually formed where the Rear Flank Downdraft cut through the line.  A clear meso was formed and the small tornadoes advanced around the meso and were in front of the thunderstorm line.  Touchdowns were all brief.  Hail was common with the stronger storm, with much of it in the one to one and three-quarter inch in diameter.  Given the antecedent soil conditions in Iowa, flash flooding became a major issue.  Numerous counties reported flash flooding as rainfall of one to 4 inches in spots resulted in flooded highways.  The thunderstorms formed into an MCC by the early evening hours.  As they moved out of the state, they delivered one more punch to the state.  High winds developed in the wake of the complex.  Several counties of north central, northeast, and central Iowa received high winds.  Damage occurred in several locations to both trees and out buildings.  Sustained winds across the area were in the 25 to 45 MPH range with some higher gusts.  Clarion reported a wind gust of 63 MPH for the highest wind gust reported.

A bow echo of damaging thunderstorm winds raced from around the Thurman area to Tabor and Randolph. The winds were estimated at over 80 mph and caused extensive damage from Thurman to Tabor and Randolph.  A semi trailer was blown over on I-29 and railroad cars were blown off the track 5 miles northwest of Thurman, significant tree damage was reported throughout the area, some roofs and outbuildings were destroyed or blown off and power lines were downed.

The bow echo produced a measured wind gust of 106 MPH in northern Warren County.  The high winds continued to push east along Interstate 80 for the next hour or so before it started to weaken.  The other major problem was with heavy rain.  The convection from the previous night set the stage by saturating soils.  The echos trained for several hours across central Iowa with very heavy rainfall.  Rainfall rates of 4 to 5 inches per hour were common under the heavier cells.  Several locations received 2 to 5 inch rainfalls in under  two hours, resulting in widespread flash flooding.  Reports of major highways being inundated by water were common.  By the end of the night, a swath of 4 to 9 inch rainfall was evident north of Interstate 80.  Longer term river flooding became a major problem by the morning of the 23rd.  There were two lightning related injuries.  Two people were injured when they were struck by lightning out of doors in Nevada.  The lightning struck close by as they were carrying groceries into their home.  The jolt caused them to fall, resulting in laceration to the head.

The synoptic situation was very similar to what it was the previous day.  A complex weather situation was in place due to the convection from the previous night.  An outflow boundary extended east to west across northern Missouri.  The synoptic frontal boundary extended from central Nebraska across northern Iowa.  The air was clear over Iowa during the day, allowing for temperatures to recover.  Dew point values also recovered quite rapidly with readings in the upper 60s to low 70s by the late afternoon hours.  Lifted indices dropped into the -5 to -10 C. range by evening with CAPE values approaching 5000 J/kg.  By the evening hours, the frontal boundary and outflow boundary had converged and were located along Interstate 80.  Explosive thunderstorm development took place during the very late afternoon and evening hours in spite of the weak shear profile.  Discrete supercells formed in a narrow line along Interstate 80 from near Des Moines on west.  The storms became tornadic quickly.  To add insult to injury, a strong thunderstorm complex formed over Nebraska and moved east along the line of convection over Iowa.  As the event took shape, all modes of severe weather occurred.  The storms developed so rapidly that there was little more than a half hour from the first hail and wind report to the first tornado touchdown.  Hail and high wind reports were too numerous to mention individually here.  Numerous reports of golf ball size hail were received.  Boone and Story Counties reported some of the largest hail with the event as baseball size hail pelted the are.  There was one report of very large hail northwest of Slater in Story County.  Hail five and one half inches in diameter punched 8 holes through a steel roof.  There was severe house siding and roof damage in the area.  There were numerous tornadoes, especially in the central Iowa Iowa.  At one point there were several supercells around the metropolitan Des Moines area.  Two of the stronger tornadoes touched down west and south of Des Moines.  Fortunately, most of the tornado tracks were over open areas and resulted in little damage. Warm advection took place over Iowa during the late night hours of the 28th into the morning of the 29th.  A warm front was positioned to the south of Iowa, extending from southern Illinois into central Nebraska.  A 50 kt low level jet pushed over the top of the front and resulted in the formation of an MCS.  Most of the thunderstorms were not severe, however there were a few supercells embedded in the MCS which produced large hail.  Golf Ball size hail was reported in parts of Crawford and Sac Counties.  The storms initially produced high winds in Carroll County near Breda, totally destroying a barn.  As the night progressed, the storms took on more of a bow shape.  Several beta bow elements tracked east-southeast into western and central Iowa.  They produced winds of 60 to 65 MPH in a few locations.  High winds downed trees in the Pomeroy area of Calhoun County.  A machine shed was blown over south of Lidderdale in Carroll County.

Once again it was a very active severe weather day over Iowa.  The frontal system that had moved through the state on the night of the 22nd was returning north as a warm front.  This time Iowa did not get completely into the warm sector.  The surface front was dragged north into Iowa by low pressure that moved out of Kansas northeast into central Iowa by the mid to late evening.  The airmass was pushed southeast out of the state by a cold front that passed through the state during the evening and early morning hours.  The airmass in the warm sector became very unstable.  Lifted indices were around -13 C with CAPE values greater than 5000 J/kg by the late afternoon.  The air advected north into Iowa during the day and into the evening.  Thunderstorms erupted in two places.  The first to affect the DSM CWA was the development of several elevated supercells over west central into southwest Iowa.  The storms continued to lift east and northeast across the area.  A few tornadoes touched down, however with the very elevated nature of the storms it was very difficult for the tornadoes to reach the ground.  By the late afternoon, a solid squall line developed along the cold front.  The line took on a Line Echo Wave Pattern by evening.  Several segments of the line bowed out, resulting in high winds.  At times the winds reached 80 MPH.  One of the thunderstorms produced high winds in the Early area of Sac County.  They overturned a semi-tractortrailer truck, causing $80,000 damage and causing minor injuries to the driver.  Tornadoes that formed along this line actually formed where the Rear Flank Downdraft cut through the line.  A clear meso was formed and the small tornadoes advanced around the meso and were in front of the thunderstorm line.  Touchdowns were all brief.  Hail was common with the stronger storm, with much of it in the one to one and three-quarter inch in diameter.  Given the antecedent soil conditions in Iowa, flash flooding became a major issue.  Numerous counties reported flash flooding as rainfall of one to 4 inches in spots resulted in flooded highways.  The thunderstorms formed into an MCC by the early evening hours.  As they moved out of the state, they delivered one more punch to the state.  High winds developed in the wake of the complex.  Several counties of north central, northeast, and central Iowa received high winds.  Damage occurred in several locations to both trees and out buildings.  Sustained winds across the area were in the 25 to 45 MPH range with some higher gusts.  Clarion reported a wind gust of 63 MPH for the highest wind gust reported.

A strong push of moisture streamed into Iowa on a 50 to 70 kt low level jet.  Lifted indices were around -9 C at the beginning of the event with CAPE in the 2500 to 4000 J/kg range.  Thunderstorms erupted to the west and southwest of Iowa with the initial development being discrete supercells to the southwest, and linear ahead of an approaching cold front to the west.  The storms became much more multi-cellular as they moved into Iowa.  As a result, competition for moisture caused the storms to weaken.  The storms that were severe in Iowa produced high winds in what appears to be several microburst events.  Winds of 74 MPH were recorded at the Ames Airport, with 73 MPH winds measured at a mesonet site in Adair.  A few other reports of severe winds were received with tree damage south of Adair and also in the Earlham area.  There were only a few reports of hail from the storms.  Hail that did occur was limited to an inch or less in diameter.

A moist and unstable airmass was in place over Iowa during the afternoon of the 19th.  A southerly flow in the low levels brought dew point temperatures of the low 70s F. into southern Iowa.  This moist air was being pushed over a warm frontal boundary that lifted northeast across the state.  The airmass became capped over much of the state during the afternoon with a 2.4 degree C. cap in place over southwest Iowa by the early evening.  A small cluster of thunderstorms formed over northwest Iowa during the mid to late afternoon hours.  The storms moved east across the northern tier of Iowa Counties during the late afternoon and evening hours.  They produced hail of three quarters of an inch to an inch in diameter and wind gusts to around 60 MPH.  At one point the storm spiked in intensity.  It produced one and one half inch diameter hail as it passed southwest of Lakota in Kossuth County.

A very unstable airmass was over Iowa and helped kick off the seasons first severe weather outbreak.  At the surface a warm frontal boundary extended nearly east to west across the state and provided the focus for thunderstorm development.  By the late afternoon, lifted indices were approaching -10 C. with CAPE values around 5000 J/kg over western Iowa.  Surface temperatures warmed into the upper 80s with dew points in the low to mid 70s.  A southwest surface wind of 15 to 25 kts pushed over the front.  Northeast of the frontal boundary winds were easterly around 10 to 15 kts.  Actually, the surface boundary was further enhanced by outflow from convective complexes during the day, further sharpening the contrast.  Thunderstorms continued to fire along and north of the boundary through the afternoon and into the evening.  This resulted in widespread flash flooding as very heavy rains fell on areas that had had significant rainfall the previous night.  There were reports in north central into northeast Iowa of 2 to 4 inches of rainfall in a little more than an hours time.  Major flooding took place in the Mason City area where evacuations were taking place.  The thunderstorms became most intense during the afternoon into the early evening.  There were several tornadoes across northeast into north central Iowa.  Most were relatively brief touchdowns and were in open areas.  Reports of multi-vortex tornadoes were received from Grundy County.  The days most significant tornado touched down in Pocahontas County east of Rolfe and tracked into Humboldt County through the Bradgate area.  The tornado damaged or destroyed over 75% of the town of Bradgate (pop 120).  Tornado damage in Bradgate was very extensive impacting most of the town.  Hardly a building was not impacted.   Outbuildings, light structures and garages were heavily damaged or destroyed.  Two homes were destroyed.  Several homes were damaged ranging from minor to extensive.  The Survey Team found that the majority of the damage was F1, with a few cases of F2 damage.  The F2 damage was to the snapping of very large trees just west of town and the structural damage to two buildings.  Nearly all of the 53 homes there reported damage with several totally destroyed.  Significant widespread property damage was reported in the tornadoes path.  There were several injuries in the town, the most significant being a broken leg.  A police officer was reportedly hit by debris from the tornado and suffered a broken leg.  According to law officials, the damage path of the tornado was 2 miles wide and about 8 miles long.  Storm chasers on the scene reported the tornado itself to be one half mile wide at one point.  Reports of hail were numerous through the afternoon and evening.  Most of the hail was in the three quarter inch to one inch diameter range.  There were a few reports of golf ball size hail from the stronger storms.  Winds were not a major problem with the activity.  There were spotty reports of winds to around 60 MPH, but most reports were in the 40 to 50 MPH range.  One of the stronger wind reports was a 64 MPH wind gust in Tripoli in Bremer County.  This gust was measured by a mesonet station there.  During the mid evening hours, thunderstorms rumbled across Grundy County.  Lightning struck the Sheriff's office there and destroyed a significant amount of office equipment.  Damage was reported around $100,000.

Storm chasers on the scene reported the tornado itself to be one half mile wide at one point.  Reports of hail were numerous through the afternoon and evening.  Most of the hail was in the three quarter inch to one inch diameter range.  There were a few reports of golf ball size hail from the stronger storms.  Winds were not a major problem with the activity.  There were spotty reports of winds to around 60 MPH, but most reports were in the 40 to 50 MPH range.  One of the stronger wind reports was a 64 MPH wind gust in Tripoli in Bremer County.  This gust was measured by a mesonet station there.  During the mid evening hours, thunderstorms rumbled across Grundy County.  Lightning struck the Sheriff's office there and destroyed a significant amount of office equipment.  Damage was reported around $100,000.

Thunderstorms continued to fire along and north of the boundary through the afternoon and into the evening.  This resulted in widespread flash flooding as very heavy rains fell on areas that had had significant rainfall the previous night.  There were reports in north central into northeast Iowa of 2 to 4 inches of rainfall in a little more than an hours time.  Major flooding took place in the Mason City area where evacuations were taking place.  The thunderstorms became most intense during the afternoon into the early evening.  There were several tornadoes across northeast into north central Iowa.  Most were relatively brief touchdowns and were in open areas.  Reports of multi-vortex tornadoes were received from Grundy County.  The days most significant tornado touched down in Pocahontas County east of Rolfe and tracked into Humboldt County through the Bradgate area.  The tornado damaged or destroyed over 75% of the town of Bradgate (pop 120).  Tornado damage in Bradgate was very extensive impacting most of the town.  Hardly a building was not impacted.   Outbuildings, light structures and garages were heavily damaged or destroyed.  Two homes were destroyed.  Several homes were damaged ranging from minor to extensive.  The Survey Team found that the majority of the damage was F1, with a few cases of F2 damage.  The F2 damage was to the snapping of very large trees just west of town and the structural damage to two buildings.  Nearly all of the 53 homes there reported damage with several totally destroyed.  Significant widespread property damage was reported in the tornadoes path.  There were several injuries in the town, the most significant being a broken leg.  A police officer was reportedly hit by debris from the tornado and suffered a broken leg.  According to law officials, the damage path of the tornado was 2 miles wide and about 8 miles long.  Storm chasers on the scene reported the tornado itself to be one half mile wide at one point.  Reports of hail were numerous through the afternoon and evening.  Most of the hail was in the three quarter inch to one inch diameter range.  There were a few reports of golf ball size hail from the stronger storms.  Winds were not a major problem with the activity.  There were spotty reports of winds to around 60 MPH, but most reports were in the 40 to 50 MPH range.  One of the stronger wind reports was a 64 MPH wind gust in Tripoli in Bremer County.  This gust was measured by a mesonet station there.  During the mid evening hours, thunderstorms rumbled across Grundy County.  Lightning struck the Sheriff's office there and destroyed a significant amount of office equipment.  Damage was reported around $100,000.

May 2004 began rather dry with an average of only 0.19 inches of rain over the first week of the month.  However, the second week of May brought seasonally normal rainfall.  Heavy rain occurred during the third week of the month.  May's greatest rain events came back to back.  The first round of heavy rain producing thunderstorms began on the morning of the 21st and continued into the morning of the 22ns.  Heaviest rains were in North Central and Northeast Iowa where storm totals included 4.75 inches at Emmetsburg, 6.22 inches at Mason City, and 5.14 inches at Decorah.  Another round of storms began on the afternoon of the 22nd and continued into the morning of the 23rd.  Some of the rain totals reported with this second series of storms included 6.67 inches near Ames and 4.15 inches at Marshalltown.  A statewide average of 2.97 inches of rain fell from these two systems.  Locally heavy rain continued to fall through much of the remainder of the month.  The greatest rainfall amount occurred during the early morning hours of the 30th when 4.37 inches of rain fell at Fairfield.  Damage reports were still coming in but it does appear from preliminary reports that roughly six percent of Iowa's 2004 crop was flood out.  Based on a $23 per acre cost to replant it is estimated that the floods have cost Iowa farmers some $15.2 million dollars.  The rainfall that occurred over the later part of May was Iowa's greatest since July 1993.  Rainfall totals for the month varied form 4.01 inches at Burlington to 14.87 inches at Lansing.  Radar derived estimates suggest that May rainfall was actually below normal in some areas over the far Northwest...such as Buena Vista and Van Buren Counties.  However, complete rain gage reports were not yet available for these areas.  Excessive rainfall produced major flooding in many areas across the state during the second half of May.  This was the first widespread major flood event in many years.  The heaviest rainfall occurred over the Northeastern sections of the state.  Additionally, several episodes of severe thunderstorms produced large hail, high winds, and tornadoes during May.  Severe weather was reported on 15 days during the month with all but 7 of Iowa's 99 counties reporting severe thunderstorm events.  The month's largest severe weather outbreaks occurred on the 8th (26 counties, mostly west central to northeast) ; 17th (20 counties, mostly southwest to east central); 21st (37 counties, mostly northwest to east central); 22nd (31 counties, mostly southwest to east central) and the 24th (33 counties, mostly southwest one half).  An exact tornado count is not yet available for the state, but central Iowa did report 32 tornadoes for the month with an estimated total of 50 tornadoes statewide.  Nearly all were F0 and F1 intensity with minimal damage to communities.  There was a tornado of F2 intensity that did produce significant damage to the communities of Bradgate and Palo (both on May 21).

A warm, moist, and unstable airmass was in place over Iowa.  The warm front that had produced the convection during the early morning hours of the 8th had become stationary over northern Iowa.  With lifted indices below -10 C. over central Iowa and continued strong moisture advection, thunderstorms erupted over the central and northeast parts of the state during the late afternoon and evening hours.  For the most part, the thunderstorms produced hail during this event.  There were numerous reports of hail of one to two inches in diameter.  Hail reportedly covered the ground in Guthrie County, into parts of Dallas County.  During the later evening hours, the thunderstorms began to sink south and took on a bow echo type configuration, though the line was not a true bow echo.  High winds began to be reported with Cass County being hit the worst.  A swath of damage 7 miles long was reported from west of Lewis to southeast of Lewis.  Embedded in the winds was a small tornado.  The tornado touched down intermittently along the 7 mile path and destroyed or damaged several grain bins and downed numerous trees.  A large corn crib was destroyed southeast of Atlantic and there were numerous reports of trees over highways.  In addition to the hail and high winds, heavy rain occurred from central into parts of west central Iowa.  Flash flooding occurred in Audubon, Guthrie, Madison, and Adair Counties.  Rain fell as the thunderstorms trained and redeveloped repeatedly over the same areas.  Rainfall rates were in the one to three inch per hour range with rainfall total reports of over 4 inches received in a few places in under two hours.  Water flooded over highways, even some U.S. Highways, to as depth of one to two feet.  This resulted in closure of some highways.

Numerous reports of large hail were received by law enforcement officials, amateur radio operators, spotters and the public.  Hail sizes ranged from pennies to as large as baseballs.  In fact, hail as large as 3 inches in diameter caused extensive damage in Charles City (Floyd County), knocking out windows and damaging roofs of residences and businesses.  Over 300 vehicles at car dealerships on the south side of Charles City sustained considerable damage due to the large hail, with damage estimates totaling around a half million dollars.

Warm advection took place over Iowa during the late night hours of the 28th into the morning of the 29th.  A warm front was positioned to the south of Iowa, extending from southern Illinois into central Nebraska.  A 50 kt low level jet pushed over the top of the front and resulted in the formation of an MCS.  Most of the thunderstorms were not severe, however there were a few supercells embedded in the MCS which produced large hail.  Golf Ball size hail was reported in parts of Crawford and Sac Counties.  The storms initially produced high winds in Carroll County near Breda, totally destroying a barn.  As the night progressed, the storms took on more of a bow shape.  Several beta bow elements tracked east-southeast into western and central Iowa.  They produced winds of 60 to 65 MPH in a few locations.  High winds downed trees in the Pomeroy area of Calhoun County.  A machine shed was blown over south of Lidderdale in Carroll County.

A strong push of moisture streamed into Iowa on a 50 to 70 kt low level jet.  Lifted indices were around -9 C at the beginning of the event with CAPE in the 2500 to 4000 J/kg range.  Thunderstorms erupted to the west and southwest of Iowa with the initial development being discrete supercells to the southwest, and linear ahead of an approaching cold front to the west.  The storms became much more multi-cellular as they moved into Iowa.  As a result, competition for moisture caused the storms to weaken.  The storms that were severe in Iowa produced high winds in what appears to be several microburst events.  Winds of 74 MPH were recorded at the Ames Airport, with 73 MPH winds measured at a mesonet site in Adair.  A few other reports of severe winds were received with tree damage south of Adair and also in the Earlham area.  There were only a few reports of hail from the storms.  Hail that did occur was limited to an inch or less in diameter.

The airmass remained unstable over Iowa through the mid day hours of the 30th.  The cold front mentioned above had not yet passed through the state.  At the same time, a mesoscale low pressure system lifted north into the state by mid day.  Thunderstorms erupted along the eastern periphery of the low and became marginally severe over parts of southeast Iowa.  Three quarter inch diameter hail fell in Wapello and Appanoose Counties as the narrow line of storms passed.

A cold upper low settled in over Iowa during the day on the 31st.  With -21 C air at 500 mb over Iowa, the airmass became unstable during the afternoon.  Numerous thunderstorms formed, many of which produced wind of up to 50 MPH and pea to marble size hail.  A few funnel clouds were also reported.  One of the storms became severe as it pass south of Oskaloosa in Mahaska County and dropped three quarter inch diameter hail.

Rainfall amounts of 5 to 8 inches in less than three hours caused flash flooding. Allamakee County was declared a disaster area due to damage resulting from the flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to baselball size fell in the Logan area.
Hail up to nickel size was reported from Woodbine to 4 miles southwest of town.
Rainfall of 2 to 3 inches which fell over Harrison county during the evening hours on 5/22 produced flash flooding across some roads north of Logan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The synoptic situation was very similar to what it was the previous day.  A complex weather situation was in place due to the convection from the previous night.  An outflow boundary extended east to west across northern Missouri.  The synoptic frontal boundary extended from central Nebraska across northern Iowa.  The air was clear over Iowa during the day, allowing for temperatures to recover.  Dew point values also recovered quite rapidly with readings in the upper 60s to low 70s by the late afternoon hours.  Lifted indices dropped into the -5 to -10 C. range by evening with CAPE values approaching 5000 J/kg.  By the evening hours, the frontal boundary and outflow boundary had converged and were located along Interstate 80.  Explosive thunderstorm development took place during the very late afternoon and evening hours in spite of the weak shear profile.  Discrete supercells formed in a narrow line along Interstate 80 from near Des Moines on west.  The storms became tornadic quickly.  To add insult to injury, a strong thunderstorm complex formed over Nebraska and moved east along the line of convection over Iowa.  As the event took shape, all modes of severe weather occurred.  The storms developed so rapidly that there was little more than a half hour from the first hail and wind report to the first tornado touchdown.  Hail and high wind reports were too numerous to mention individually here.  Numerous reports of golf ball size hail were received.  Boone and Story Counties reported some of the largest hail with the event as baseball size hail pelted the are.  There was one report of very large hail northwest of Slater in Story County.  Hail five and one half inches in diameter punched 8 holes through a steel roof.  There was severe house siding and roof damage in the area.  There were numerous tornadoes, especially in the central Iowa Iowa.  At one point there were several supercells around the metropolitan Des Moines area.  Two of the stronger tornadoes touched down west and south of Des Moines.  Fortunately, most of the tornado tracks were over open areas and resulted in little damage.  High winds became a problem, especially after sunset, as the complex from Nebraska moved in.  The system became more of a derecho during the late night hours.  Eighty plus MPH winds pelted southwest Iowa and downed barns and several buildings in Adams and Cass Counties.  A beta bow element formed in central Iowa shortly before midnight.  The bow echo produced a measured wind gust of 106 MPH in northern Warren County.  The high winds continued to push east along Interstate 80 for the next hour or so before it started to weaken.  The other major problem was with heavy rain.  The convection from the previous night set the stage by saturating soils.  The echos trained for several hours across central Iowa with very heavy rainfall.  Rainfall rates of 4 to 5 inches per hour were common under the heavier cells.  Several locations received 2 to 5 inch rainfalls in under  two hours, resulting in widespread flash flooding.  Reports of major highways being inundated by water were common.  By the end of the night, a swath of 4 to 9 inch rainfall was evident north of Interstate 80.  Longer term river flooding became a major problem by the morning of the 23rd.  There were two lightning related injuries.  Two people were injured when they were struck by lightning out of doors in Nevada.  The lightning struck close by as they were carrying groceries into their home.  The jolt caused them to fall, resulting in laceration to the head.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado touched down in fields west of Waukee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the evening hours along a cold front moving through Iowa and became severe as a mesoscale low developed in East Central Iowa. A wake low developed just behind the storms and provided sustained winds over 26 knots (30 mph) that lasted for 20 to 30 minutes.  The storms and wake low moved east along U.S. 20 through Northeast Iowa and Northwest Illinois.  The storms were quite prolific in producing pea to just under .75 inch hail.  Significant damage occurred on the south side of Dubuque, Iowa with numerous trees and power lines down and a radio tower mangled.  In Jo Daviess County Illinois, a continuous area of damage occurred from the flats near East Dubuque, Illinois through Menominee and Vinegar Hill townships.  Numerous trees were either uprooted or decapitated with many buildings (farm and garage) damaged or destroyed.

High winds became a problem, especially after sunset, as the complex from Nebraska moved in.  The system became more of a derecho during the late night hours.  Eighty plus MPH winds pelted southwest Iowa and downed barns and several buildings in Adams and Cass Counties.  A beta bow element formed in central Iowa shortly before midnight.  The bow echo produced a measured wind gust of 106 MPH in northern Warren County.  The high winds continued to push east along Interstate 80 for the next hour or so before it started to weaken.  The other major problem was with heavy rain.  The convection from the previous night set the stage by saturating soils.  The echos trained for several hours across central Iowa with very heavy rainfall.  Rainfall rates of 4 to 5 inches per hour were common under the heavier cells.  Several locations received 2 to 5 inch rainfalls in under  two hours, resulting in widespread flash flooding.  Reports of major highways being inundated by water were common.  By the end of the night, a swath of 4 to 9 inch rainfall was evident north of Interstate 80.  Longer term river flooding became a major problem by the morning of the 23rd.  There were two lightning related injuries.  Two people were injured when they were struck by lightning out of doors in Nevada.  The lightning struck close by as they were carrying groceries into their home.  The jolt caused them to fall, resulting in laceration to the head.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2 to 3 inches fell across much of Shelby county during the night of 5/22 and early 5/23.  Earling reported 3 inches of rain while Harlan reported 2 inches.  The heavy rain produced areas of flash flooding, especially in northeast Shelby county around the Botna area where many fields and several roads were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The synoptic situation was very similar to what it was the previous day.  A complex weather situation was in place due to the convection from the previous night.  An outflow boundary extended east to west across northern Missouri.  The synoptic frontal boundary extended from central Nebraska across northern Iowa.  The air was clear over Iowa during the day, allowing for temperatures to recover.  Dew point values also recovered quite rapidly with readings in the upper 60s to low 70s by the late afternoon hours.  Lifted indices dropped into the -5 to -10 C. range by evening with CAPE values approaching 5000 J/kg.  By the evening hours, the frontal boundary and outflow boundary had converged and were located along Interstate 80.  Explosive thunderstorm development took place during the very late afternoon and evening hours in spite of the weak shear profile.  Discrete supercells formed in a narrow line along Interstate 80 from near Des Moines on west.  The storms became tornadic quickly.  To add insult to injury, a strong thunderstorm complex formed over Nebraska and moved east along the line of convection over Iowa.  As the event took shape, all modes of severe weather occurred.  The storms developed so rapidly that there was little more than a half hour from the first hail and wind report to the first tornado touchdown.  Hail and high wind reports were too numerous to mention individually here.  Numerous reports of golf ball size hail were received.  Boone and Story Counties reported some of the largest hail with the event as baseball size hail pelted the are.  There was one report of very large hail northwest of Slater in Story County.  Hail five and one half inches in diameter punched 8 holes through a steel roof.  There was severe house siding and roof damage in the area. May 2004 began rather dry with an average of only 0.19 inches of rain over the first week of the month.  However, the second week of May brought seasonally normal rainfall.  Heavy rain occurred during the third week of the month.  May's greatest rain events came back to back.  The first round of heavy rain producing thunderstorms began on the morning of the 21st and continued into the morning of the 22ns.  Heaviest rains were in North Central and Northeast Iowa where storm totals included 4.75 inches at Emmetsburg, 6.22 inches at Mason City, and 5.14 inches at Decorah.  Another round of storms began on the afternoon of the 22nd and continued into the morning of the 23rd.  Some of the rain totals reported with this second series of storms included 6.67 inches near Ames and 4.15 inches at Marshalltown.  A statewide average of 2.97 inches of rain fell from these two systems.  Locally heavy rain continued to fall through much of the remainder of the month.  The greatest rainfall amount occurred during the early morning hours of the 30th when 4.37 inches of rain fell at Fairfield.  Damage reports were still coming in but it does appear from preliminary reports that roughly six percent of Iowa's 2004 crop was flood out.  Based on a $23 per acre cost to replant it is estimated that the floods have cost Iowa farmers some $15.2 million dollars.  The rainfall that occurred over the later part of May was Iowa's greatest since July 1993.  Rainfall totals for the month varied form 4.01 inches at Burlington to 14.87 inches at Lansing.  Radar derived estimates suggest that May rainfall was actually below normal in some areas over the far Northwest...such as Buena Vista and Van Buren Counties.  However, complete rain gage reports were not yet available for these areas.  Excessive rainfall produced major flooding in many areas across the state during the second half of May.  This was the first widespread major flood event in many years.  The heaviest rainfall occurred over the Northeastern sections of the state.  Additionally, several episodes of severe thunderstorms produced large hail, high winds, and tornadoes during May.  Severe weather was reported on 15 days during the month with all but 7 of Iowa's 99 counties reporting severe thunderstorm events.  The month's largest severe weather outbreaks occurred on the 8th (26 counties, mostly west central to northeast) ; 17th (20 counties, mostly southwest to east central); 21st (37 counties, mostly northwest to east central); 22nd (31 counties, mostly southwest to east central) and the 24th (33 counties, mostly southwest one half).  An exact tornado count is not yet available for the state, but central Iowa did report 32 tornadoes for the month with an estimated total of 50 tornadoes statewide.  Nearly all were F0 and F1 intensity with minimal damage to communities.  There was a tornado of F2 intensity that did produce significant damage to the communities of Bradgate and Palo (both on May 21).

May 2004 began rather dry with an average of only 0.19 inches of rain over the first week of the month.  However, the second week of May brought seasonally normal rainfall.  Heavy rain occurred during the third week of the month.  May's greatest rain events came back to back.  The first round of heavy rain producing thunderstorms began on the morning of the 21st and continued into the morning of the 22ns.  Heaviest rains were in North Central and Northeast Iowa where storm totals included 4.75 inches at Emmetsburg, 6.22 inches at Mason City, and 5.14 inches at Decorah.  Another round of storms began on the afternoon of the 22nd and continued into the morning of the 23rd.  Some of the rain totals reported with this second series of storms included 6.67 inches near Ames and 4.15 inches at Marshalltown.  A statewide average of 2.97 inches of rain fell from these two systems.  Locally heavy rain continued to fall through much of the remainder of the month.  The greatest rainfall amount occurred during the early morning hours of the 30th when 4.37 inches of rain fell at Fairfield.  Damage reports were still coming in but it does appear from preliminary reports that roughly six percent of Iowa's 2004 crop was flood out.  Based on a $23 per acre cost to replant it is estimated that the floods have cost Iowa farmers some $15.2 million dollars.  The rainfall that occurred over the later part of May was Iowa's greatest since July 1993.  Rainfall totals for the month varied form 4.01 inches at Burlington to 14.87 inches at Lansing.  Radar derived estimates suggest that May rainfall was actually below normal in some areas over the far Northwest...such as Buena Vista and Van Buren Counties.  However, complete rain gage reports were not yet available for these areas.  Excessive rainfall produced major flooding in many areas across the state during the second half of May.  This was the first widespread major flood event in many years.  The heaviest rainfall occurred over the Northeastern sections of the state.  Additionally, several episodes of severe thunderstorms produced large hail, high winds, and tornadoes during May.  Severe weather was reported on 15 days during the month with all but 7 of Iowa's 99 counties reporting severe thunderstorm events.  The month's largest severe weather outbreaks occurred on the 8th (26 counties, mostly west central to northeast) ; 17th (20 counties, mostly southwest to east central); 21st (37 counties, mostly northwest to east central); 22nd (31 counties, mostly southwest to east central) and the 24th (33 counties, mostly southwest one half).  An exact tornado count is not yet available for the state, but central Iowa did report 32 tornadoes for the month with an estimated total of 50 tornadoes statewide.  Nearly all were F0 and F1 intensity with minimal damage to communities.  There was a tornado of F2 intensity that did produce significant damage to the communities of Bradgate and Palo (both on May 21).

Once again it was a very active severe weather day over Iowa.  The frontal system that had moved through the state on the night of the 22nd was returning north as a warm front.  This time Iowa did not get completely into the warm sector.  The surface front was dragged north into Iowa by low pressure that moved out of Kansas northeast into central Iowa by the mid to late evening.  The airmass was pushed southeast out of the state by a cold front that passed through the state during the evening and early morning hours.  The airmass in the warm sector became very unstable.  Lifted indices were around -13 C with CAPE values greater than 5000 J/kg by the late afternoon.  The air advected north into Iowa during the day and into the evening.  Thunderstorms erupted in two places.  The first to affect the DSM CWA was the development of several elevated supercells over west central into southwest Iowa.  The storms continued to lift east and northeast across the area.  A few tornadoes touched down, however with the very elevated nature of the storms it was very difficult for the tornadoes to reach the ground.  By the late afternoon, a solid squall line developed along the cold front.  The line took on a Line Echo Wave Pattern by evening.  Several segments of the line bowed out, resulting in high winds.  At times the winds reached 80 MPH.  One of the thunderstorms produced high winds in the Early area of Sac County.  They overturned a semi-tractortrailer truck, causing $80,000 damage and causing minor injuries to the driver.  Tornadoes that formed along this line actually formed where the Rear Flank Downdraft cut through the line.  A clear meso was formed and the small tornadoes advanced around the meso and were in front of the thunderstorm line.  Touchdowns were all brief.  Hail was common with the stronger storm, with much of it in the one to one and three-quarter inch in diameter.  Given the antecedent soil conditions in Iowa, flash flooding became a major issue.  Numerous counties reported flash flooding as rainfall of one to 4 inches in spots resulted in flooded highways.  The thunderstorms formed into an MCC by the early evening hours.  As they moved out of the state, they delivered one more punch to the state.  High winds developed in the wake of the complex.  Several counties of north central, northeast, and central Iowa received high winds.  Damage occurred in several locations to both trees and out buildings.  Sustained winds across the area were in the 25 to 45 MPH range with some higher gusts.  Clarion reported a wind gust of 63 MPH for the highest wind gust reported.

A warm, moist, and unstable airmass was in place over Iowa.  The warm front that had produced the convection during the early morning hours of the 8th had become stationary over northern Iowa.  With lifted indices below -10 C. over central Iowa and continued strong moisture advection, thunderstorms erupted over the central and northeast parts of the state during the late afternoon and evening hours.  For the most part, the thunderstorms produced hail during this event.  There were numerous reports of hail of one to two inches in diameter.  Hail reportedly covered the ground in Guthrie County, into parts of Dallas County.  During the later evening hours, the thunderstorms began to sink south and took on a bow echo type configuration, though the line was not a true bow echo.  High winds began to be reported with Cass County being hit the worst.  A swath of damage 7 miles long was reported from west of Lewis to southeast of Lewis.  Embedded in the winds was a small tornado.  The tornado touched down intermittently along the 7 mile path and destroyed or damaged several grain bins and downed numerous trees.  A large corn crib was destroyed southeast of Atlantic and there were numerous reports of trees over highways.  In addition to the hail and high winds, heavy rain occurred from central into parts of west central Iowa.  Flash flooding occurred in Audubon, Guthrie, Madison, and Adair Counties.  Rain fell as the thunderstorms trained and redeveloped repeatedly over the same areas.  Rainfall rates were in the one to three inch per hour range with rainfall total reports of over 4 inches received in a few places in under two hours.  Water flooded over highways, even some U.S. Highways, to as depth of one to two feet.  This resulted in closure of some highways.
